---
title: "[HOW-TO] X-fi beta driver"
date: 2007-10-09
forum: x86 64-bit Users (Pre April '08)
---

### Post by NullHead on 2007-10-09
[FONT="Tahoma"]
[CENTER][SIZE="5"]***Readme***[/SIZE][/CENTER]

Creative has attempted and failed at making a driver for their X-FI sound card line. They finally broke and released datasheets to both OSS and ALSA (**A**dvanced **L**inux **S**ound **A**rchitecture). Oss, obviously, has released a driver before ALSA has even started development. Therefore I choose not to have Creative's section included on this page anymore. It causes users too much trauma trying to get it to work and OSS works great anyways.

Enough chatter ... here is the link to Temüjin's wonderful guide on how to get your high-end soundcard working:

[CENTER]
[SIZE="5"]***Guide***[/SIZE][/CENTER]

Use this guide to install OSS: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2)

If OSS fails to install or update use this guild to help you uninstall it: [http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054](http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054)

[SIZE="2"]**This guide supports all versions of Ubuntu from 7.04 and up.**[/SIZE]

[CENTER][SIZE="2"]!!!BOTTOM SECTION FOR ADVANCED USERS **ONLY**!!![/SIZE][/CENTER]

Amtam wrote a guide here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675)
You can use that to try to setup Creative's officially unofficial driver.[/FONT]

---

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-09
```
sudo gedit Makefiel.conf
```

Little typo there... ;)

> Well I've only got this to work on Ubuntu 7.04 and Debian Lenny

The Gutsy-beta will work also, but you will have to compile your kernel to use SLAB instead of SLUB.

### Post by Urgazhi on 2007-10-13
Alright, I followed another persons how to (somewhat similar though...) at [http://blackbox.lostwave.net/x-fi/readme.txt](http://blackbox.lostwave.net/x-fi/readme.txt)

after following it, I realized that I had the error 

Q: during "make install" I get: "./ctsound: 35: Syntax error: Bad substitution" 
A: If you are using Debian/ubuntu, your /bin/sh probably point to /bin/dash. 
---> Edit ctsound and change #!/bin/sh to #!/bin/bash 

Well I fixed that, and the install got all the way though... but when it tried to run ctsound this is the errors I get...

```

### Post by NullHead on 2007-10-13
ok Urgazhi you should do a ```
make clean
``` and then redo the ctsound change from sh to bash then ./configure and then  add the -fno-stack-protector to Makefile.config and it should work just fine. You're using debian testing or Ubuntu feisty?

### Post by NullHead on 2007-10-14
> **Urgazhi said:**
> I am using Ubuntu feisty... I just found that particular how to, to be the one that looked the most in depth ^_^

[EDIT]

Alright... I tried to do what you suggested... and it bascially stopped at a point where it said that ctsound already existed... 

I think i messed up, at some point... the bad thing is the computer seized up at this point, and I had to do a hard reset... so I dont have the exact output of the error...

I think it has to do with the fact that when I did a 'make clean' it did not remove /etc/init.d/ctsound?

if that is true, how would i go around to removing it? (sorry for all the questions... a little bit of a noob, but trying to learn...)

(This might be becuase i RECREATED the XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04 forlder from the tars... caused i deleted them all?  (Yea hindsight is always 20/20))

Everyone had to start somewhere! 

OK well we will try this do a ```
make clean
``` and then a ```
sudo rm -rf /etc/init.d/ctsound 
``` then edit ctsound then do a ```
./configure
```
then edit the Makefile.config and do a ```
make
``````
sudo make install
``````
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```

OK? well that should do it. Let me know what happens.

### Post by strumluff on 2007-10-15
I had to do a ```
sudo make
``` instead of just make. 

Unfortunately for me upon loading the driver its fine until a sound plays and then the sound stutters and system crashes =(

I will be upgrading to Gutsy soon though and I would like a guide for re-compiling the kernel. I would also like to know if there any disadvantages to doing that, and what the difference between SLAB and SLUB are =)

Cheers !

--------------------------

It is trying to patch ctalsa.h and it cant find it... I dont know how you would fish your script, but just something you should be aware of...

Edit (again)

I finally gave up on the sound drivers... lol

I decided to replace the X-Fi with a Dell Soundblaster Live that I had laying around... ^_^  Still a good sound card, not the same, but better than what I have been hearing about the X-Fi's sound quality in Linux from the Beta driver... ^_^

### Post by NullHead on 2007-10-17

### Post by tpdean on 2007-10-18
Does anyone know how to get this driver working under the final version of Gutsy?  I recompiled my kernel with SLAB support and was able to get the driver to compile, however it's not working very well.  Whenever I play a sound, the soundcard is basically repeating itself over and over to infinity.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2007-10-18
> **tpdean said:**
> Does anyone know how to get this driver working under the final version of Gutsy?  I recompiled my kernel with SLAB support and was able to get the driver to compile, however it's not working very well.  Whenever I play a sound, the soundcard is basically repeating itself over and over to infinity.

Well you've been more successful than myself. You shouldn't have a problem using xmms. I've found totem and the startup sound that gdm makes when you login make it repeat like you said. Give xmms a try and let me know how it goes. you should be able to change you sound output driver in the xmms options ... you want it to be on alsa.

---

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-19
Both XMMS and Audacious work just fine in Gutsy. They use a different output-plugin then Totem, which uses the gstreamer-plugin. The X-Fi driver just doesn't work very well with that driver. There's not much you can do about that, until Creative releases a better driver. You know, until hell freezes over...

For now, I can live with the very limited capabilities of my X-Fi in Gutsy, but if they don't release something better soon, I'm going to sell my X-Fi and move on. The amount of patience a man has is limited....

---

### Post by Hexaphim on 2007-10-19
So I figured I'd try my hand at Linux for the first time with Ubuntu 7.10, and now I find out I won't get audio. This pretty much means that's it back to Vista for me, unless there are any other Linux distributions that support the X-Fi?

---

### Post by Hobo2021 on 2007-10-19
I don't think its a matter of linux supporting the X-Fi, rather Creative supporting its customers using linux.

I'm still waiting till I hear that someone fixed these sh1t drivers...but until then I'm using onboard sound, which is just awful (not that the X-Fi's sound quality is the best), but the ubuntu experience is worth it to me for a little inconvenience.

### Post by Hexaphim on 2007-10-19
I managed to get the onboard soundcard working, which should keep me going until Creative get their asses in gear. :)

### Post by tpdean on 2007-10-19
You're right.  xmms works, it's just everything else that's messed up.  Man this driver is BUGGY!

You know, it's funny...  I bought the X-fi to get a better sound experience in World of Warcraft.  Well, 2 months later Blizzard goes and installs a purely software driven sound engine.  No more 3D effects for me.

If I'd known just how buggy the Vista drivers were for this card, and then the utter lack of linux support, I wouldn't have bought it.  I'm thinking maybe I should just hawk this card on ebay and go back to onboard audio.  At least that worked..

> Well you've been more successful than myself. You shouldn't have a problem using xmms. I've found totem and the startup sound that gdm makes when you login make it repeat like you said. ### Post by tpdean on 2007-10-19
I'm of the opinion that Creative should be well beyond this point though.  The X-Fi was originally released in August of '05 ([http://www.creative.com/press/releases/welcome.asp?pid=12163](http://www.creative.com/press/releases/welcome.asp?pid=12163)).  Here we are, over two years later, and only now are seeing an alpha quality linux driver.  It's crazy.

### Post by Garyu on 2007-10-19
[http://connect.creativelabs.com/linux/Lists/Driver%20Issues/AllItems.aspx](http://connect.creativelabs.com/linux/Lists/Driver%20Issues/AllItems.aspx)

You know, Creative is looking for your feedback on this driver. So why don't you go there and tell them how it works and what doesn't. The more people that give them feedback, the quicker they will respond. I'm already there trying to write my limited experiences and everyone who has a X-Fi should be!

EDIT: you don't even have to sign in there or create an account or anything. Just click "New" in the blue bar at the top and write about what you know.

---

### Post by Danger7789 on 2007-10-19
Firstly, nice how-to, glad i could find one on this X-Fi driver. gez it's a pain.

So here's what's happening to me. i follow the steps you've given, everything completes, but no sound. also after running the make install there was a nice little pop from my speakers ... but it turns out it was just a teaser as i get not sound when playing an mp3 (had to install codecs too ... but still nothing).

so i then took a closer look at the massive amount of output from the make commands and noticed that make echoed alot to Warnings ... i did a make clean, then ran "make >& make-text' as there was more output then my terminal windows 'history' (over 400 lines)

### Post by NullHead on 2007-10-20
> **Danger7789 said:**
> ok so now i feel kinda silly ... if i load up Volume Control and switch to Creative X-Fi (Alsa mixer) and unmute the microphone, it work perfectly. but i still get no sound from ubuntu, no start up sound and no sound when playing an mp3. also i had to re run" sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start" after doing a restart to get the Device option in Volume Control. do i have to load the X-Fi driver like that ever time i boot, or is this not supost to happen ...

hope this helps you help me ...

Danger

Well this is normal to not start up as expected. The driver doesn't like to behave with the startup sounds ... so I recommend that you disable the statup sound and use xmms to play music because you can easily select the audio driver you want to use ... for the x-fi you want to use alsa. If you want the driver to start automatically you can type ```
sudo modprobe ctsound
```and it should work like you want.

---

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-21
> **Gelupah said:**
> Is there a How-to for gutsy? I am struggling, I get errors at the "make" stages - and I've no idea about the whole "recompiling Kernel" thing!

I already made a HOWTO about compiling the kernel with SLAB in the other X-Fi thread, but some **a****s****s****h****o****l****e** started flaming me because he didn't read the entire HOWTO before he started with it...

Anyway, I'll give it another shot. For me, it works 100%, but ofcourse, YMMV... ;)
So read the entire howto before starting.

-----------------------------------------------------------


For people using Gutsy, you wil have to re-compile your kernel to use the X-Fi driver.
That is because the Gutsy-kernel uses the SLUB-allocator instead of the SLAB-allocator and you need the latter. This is how I do it, there may be other (better?) ways of doing this. But it works for me.

I use the root-account to compile, so I don't have to sudo all the time. If you don't, remember to put sudo in front of all the commands!

First, you should use /bin/bash instead of /bin/sh, because that shell can sometimes cause problems when compiling software.

```
rm -f /bin/sh
ln -s /bin/bash /bin/sh
```

Then, be sure to update the system:

```
apt-get update
```

Some necessary packages:

```
apt-get install kernel-package libncurses5-dev fakeroot wget bzip2
```

You may also need to

```
apt-get install build-essential
```

Download the vanilla kernel-sources to /usr/src, unpack it and create a symlink to it
I found that the newest 2.6.23-kernel doesn't work with the X-Fi driver, so go with the latest 2.6.22:

```
cd /usr/src
wget http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/linux-2.6.22.10.tar.bz2
tar xjf linux-2.6.22.10.tar.bz2
ln -s linux-2.6.22.10 linux
cd /usr/src/linux
```

Use the config from your working, default kernel as a basis for the new kernel:

```
cp /boot/config-`uname -r` ./.config
```

Then run:

```
make menuconfig
```

Go to Load Alternate Config File and choose .config (in the lower part of the main menu)

In General Setup (first item in main menu) select "Choose SLAB Allocator" and the SLAB instead of the pre-selected SLUB. Thats all you have to do for this HOWTO, but you can make other changes if you want. I always change stuff like processor-architecture. But if you don't really know what your doing, leave everything untouched because you can seriously screw up things... ;-)

Exit the main-menu and save the new kernel-configuration (it will ask you to).

Then build the kernel with the following two commands:

```
make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

This can take a while, depending on the speed of your system. It takes about 40 minutes on my Core 2 Duo@ 3GHz.

Installing the new kernel.
There are now 2 .deb-packages in /usr/src.

```
cd /usr/src
ls -l
```

The are called something like:

linux-image-2.6.22.10-custom_2.6.22.10-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb and
linux-headers-2.6.22.10-custom_2.6.22.10-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb

Install them with these commands:
```
dpkg -i linux-image-2.6.22.10-custom_2.6.22.10-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
```
```
dpkg -i linux-headers-2.6.22.10-custom_2.6.22.10-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
```

Check /boot/grub/menu.lst to see if the new kernel is present.

Reboot the system with 'shutdown -r now' and after that, check with uname -r if the new kernel is active.

Then you can proceed with compiling the X-Fi-driver using the HOWTO in [this thread](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=571656).

### Post by TheCursor on 2007-10-22
Thanks for your help - this worked and I'm now able to get sound out of the XFi. I'm never buying Creative hardware again, though - about the worst support I've ever seen for Linux.

Again, thanks. Keep up the good work!

Oh - Amtam, How did you get your nvidia driver to install for the custom kernel? I ran the custom installer from nvidia, without error. Upon restarting X I'm sent to low-graphics-mode with it saying that it can't find the driver for my video card. Upon further inspection, 'modprobe nvidia' yields:

FATAL: Error running install command for nvidia

Do you have any ideas? I looked around and it seems that some others have a similar problem... Is it trying to insert the module into the correct place? Is it a problem with links? 

Thanks for your prompt help.

-TC.

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-22
> **TheCursor said:**
> 
Oh - Amtam, How did you get your nvidia driver to install for the custom kernel? I ran the custom installer from nvidia, without error. Upon restarting X I'm sent to low-graphics-mode with it saying that it can't find the driver for my video card. Upon further inspection, 'modprobe nvidia' yields:

FATAL: Error running install command for nvidia

Do you have any ideas? I looked around and it seems that some others have a similar problem... Is it trying to insert the module into the correct place? Is it a problem with links? 

Thanks for your prompt help.

-TC.

I've noticed that behaviour too. For some reason, you have to compile the nVidia-driver **twice** to get it to insmod properly.

I do it like this: I compile my kernel with SLAB. Then I reboot with the new kernel. I get dropped back to the console, just as is to be expected. Then I install the nVidia-driver:

```
sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-100.14.19-pkg2.run
```

This seems to work OK. The script first uninstalls the driver, compiles and then installs it again. Reboot. No desktop. Then again I do:

```
sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-100.14.19-pkg2.run
```

Reboot again and it works! I have no idea why this happens. Maybe it's because there's some stuff left from the original nvidia-module that came with the default kernel?

> sudo modprobe/ctsound

This is the answer:

FATAL: Module ctsound not found.

ctsound is not a module. You either do

```
/etc/init.d/ctsound start
```

or you do

```
modprobe ctalsa
```

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-23
It looks like you are trying to compile from the wrong directory.

```
/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.22-14-generic
```

Those are the headers from the default 2.6.22-14 kernel. You need the **sources** to compile a kernel. You can either use the sources from the repositories or you can download them from kernel.org, like I did in the howto. Be sure to make a proper symlink to them. So /usr/src/linux points to /usr/src/your-kernel-sources.

You didn't mess anything up (yet ;) ), because you still have your old kernel.

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-23
I thought so... ;)
I always use a somewhat 'dirty' trick to make sure the X-server doesn't come up so I can install a new nvidia-driver. Edit xorg.conf and find Driver "nv" or "nvidia" and replace it with some garbage like "foo". Then reboot and Xorg will fail. Now you can install the driver. I know, it's not very pretty, but it always works. :)

If you see no login-prompt, just press Alt-F1 or Alt-F2 to switch to a terminal.

OK then you need to use envy it will fix that problem and get you a working nvidia! 1st you need to uninstall the binary nvidia that you've been trying to install ```
sudo ./"where ever your driver is" --uninstall

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-24
> **Atriach said:**
> when i try to: 

First, you should use /bin/bash instead of /bin/sh, because that shell can sometimes cause problems when compiling software.

Code:

rm -f /bin/sh
ln -s /bin/bash /bin/sh


I get an error like :

ln: creating symbolic link `/bin/sh' to `/bin/bash': Permission denied

How can I fix this?

Are you logged in as root when you get that error?

Try placing 'sudo' in front of the commands:

```
sudo rm -f /bin/sh
sudo ln -s /bin/bash /bin/sh
```

This really shouldn't be a problem...

@tim heeter: you did remember to remove the "foobar"?

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-26
> **NullHead said:**
> A custom kernel could be a good thing though ... such as you can remove drivers you don't use like a matrox video card driver if I am recalling correctly there old. You can set your custom kernel architecture to a Intel core 2 duo or to an Amd athlon ... witch IMO is worth it.

That's what I did. I ripped out **a lot** of modules and kernel-drivers that I don't need for my system (ISDN, Infrared, parallel ports, serial ports, obsolete drivers etc. etc. ). The time needed to compile my kernel has dropped from 40 minutes to 10 minutes plus I end up with a kernel which is lean and mean and optimized for my system :)

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-27
> **devzero said:**
> Hi,

Yes i did that.
Before I did make menuconfig the two files are the same (checked with diff).
After that you get the results i posted.

Hmm do u think it will work if i open the .confic with vim and change the slab/slub entry (if i find it)
and compile with it?

Btw cant I look for the2.6.22.14 Kernel an use slab in there? It doesn't seems to be 2.6.23

You could edit your .config manually if you wish to do so. Just make it look like this:

```
CONFIG_SLAB=y
# CONFIG_SLUB is not set
# CONFIG_SLOB is not set
```

In the default kernel, it's SLUB that's enabled and not SLAB, so you have to change that.

BTW, the 2.6.22-14-generic default kernel from Gutsy is actually older then the 2.6.22.10 from kernel.org. You can't compare the different numbering-schemes.

> if i try opening restricted drivers manager it says me i need restricted modules
Once you have compiled your kernel, you can't use the restricted modules anymore, because they are based on the default kernel. You have to install drivers from nvidia or ati manually or with the help from envy.

If you want to listen to your X-Fi, it just takes some work. Leaving everything on default just won't get you there...

### Post by amtam on 2007-10-27
> **devzero said:**
> Hi, 

okay i got it run somehow atm without compiz but the sound crackles and repeats all time
did anyone found a solution for this?

For audio/mp3, use XMMS or Audacious and select the alsa-plugin.
For video, use VLC. I use those and I get no crackles nor looping :)

It has been said before in this thread and I'll say it again: the driver just isn't very good yet.

### Post by wolfc on 2007-11-06
I had some problems with Demik's patch. Looping sound and unable to interrupt sound. So I've created an alternative patch.

Note that the original code contains some dirty bits and pieces, so it won't end up as a textbook example.

Before you try the patch make sure you have gcc-3.3 installed.
```
apt-get install gcc-3.3
```

Then follow NullHead's instructions until you get to downloading Demik's patch.
Now do the following instead:
```
wget http://www.nerdnet.nl/~carlo/x-fi-0.1.patch && patch -p0 <x-fi-0.1.patch
cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04
./configure **CC=gcc-3.3**
make
sudo make install
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```

Most notably I've solved the work queue code differently by using the structures correctly instead of creating a static variable. The configure scripts now detects the kernel version correctly and autoconf.h is always included.
I've also enabled -Werror and tried to solve all errors as best as possible. There are some 64-bit pointer assignments into 32-bit integers in the code, which might give problems.

Tested against kernel 2.6.22.6 on Feisty.

---

### Post by wolfc on 2007-11-06
Okay, I made sure the interrupt_callback is abiding the contract. Next patch version.
> **wolfc said:**
> I had some problems with Demik's patch. Looping sound and unable to interrupt sound. So I've created an alternative patch.

Note that the original code contains some dirty bits and pieces, so it won't end up as a textbook example.

Before you try the patch make sure you have gcc-3.3 installed.
```
apt-get install gcc-3.3
```

Then follow NullHead's instructions until you get to downloading Demik's patch.
Now do the following instead:
```
wget http://www.nerdnet.nl/~carlo/x-fi-0.2.patch && patch -p0 <x-fi-0.2.patch
cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04
./configure **CC=gcc-3.3**
make
sudo make install
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```
> **wolfc said:**
> 

Most notably I've solved the work queue code differently by using the structures correctly instead of creating a static variable. The configure scripts now detects the kernel version correctly and autoconf.h is always included.
I've also enabled -Werror and tried to solve all errors as best as possible. There are some 64-bit pointer assignments into 32-bit integers in the code, which might give problems.

Tested against kernel 2.6.22.6 on Feisty.

### Post by wolfc on 2007-11-06
> **NullHead said:**
> wolfc 

I'm not quite sure what's going on hear. It looks like you've made an alternative to demik's patch ... am I correct in thinking so?

secondly I'm wondering if you altered demik's code or did you start from scratch? 
I started from scratch. Demik's code was based on using gcc 4, which was not a viable option as I read the release notes from Creative.
> **NullHead said:**
> Is this patch suitable for Gutsy or a recompiled kernel?
Haven't tried yet. I've only tried it in 2.6.22.6.
> **NullHead said:**
> Does this patch fix repeating problems with totem and the like?
Yes! :)

The schedule callback handling was broken in Demik's code and the interrupt handling broken in the original Creative code. It should not give any problem now.

Install script files...

### Post by wolfc on 2007-11-07
> **kmurray79 said:**
> [B]/bin/sh: /sbin/chkconfig: not found
./ctsound: 35: Syntax error: Bad substitution
make: *** [load] Error 2[/B]

You can ignore the chkconfig error and install the rc.d links with:
```
sudo update-rc.d ctsound defaults 95 5
```

The ctsound is build to use bash, so this must be your default shell (or change the 1st line).
See also [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/dash/+bug/141481](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/dash/+bug/141481).

Note that installing the driver sometimes hangs the kernel. Reboot and try again. :twisted:

---

### Post by wolfc on 2007-11-08
> **Dawoodmm said:**
> but however when i do this:
```

sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```
i get a 
```
Loading x-fi driver...
```
and the terminal just hangs
all the other programs and windows run fine though
It can take up to a minute or so for the driver to come alive. As long as other windows are responding you don't have a kernel hang.

Keep an eye on /var/log/kern.log or dmesg to see what's happening.

Once it has finished try:
```
$ cat /proc/asound/cards
 0 [X-Fi           ]: X-Fi - Creative X-Fi [8c00]
                      Creative X-Fi at 0x8c00, irq 18
```

### Post by NullHead on 2007-11-09
Yes it takes some messing around with ctsound though I'm working on a way to get that easily done. But it will work nicely up until you restart you computer, but like i said i found a way around that but i need time to work out an easy way to get that done.

EDIT: OK the easiest way I know how to do this is .. follow the instructions in the how to, but after you do the sudo make install step you need to install ksysv ... yes I know there is better ways of doing this but it's the most user friendly.

So install ksysv```
sudo apt-get install ksysv
``` Run ksysv only as root. So ```
sudo ksysv
``` It will ask you to tell it what OS your using ... select Debian and press next. After it loads up it should have a small window .. maximize it and it should have 7 different runlevels .. look to the left of 0 and find ctsound ... drag the selection to the top of runlevel 2. Save the changes with the button near the top left of the window and close ksysv. You should be good to go after all of that. 

Let me know if it doesn't.

---

### Post by thepocketdrummer on 2007-11-11
> **NullHead said:**
> ok Urgazhi you should do a ```
make clean
``` and then redo the ctsound change from sh to bash then ./configure and then  add the -fno-stack-protector to Makefile.config and it should work just fine. You're using debian testing or Ubuntu feisty?

How exactly do you ad a -fno-stack-protector to Makefile.config? Do you just edit the text file and paste that in there somewhere?

---

### Post by thepocketdrummer on 2007-11-11
> **NullHead said:**
> it should look like 

CFLAGS = -Wall -fomit-frame-pointer -Os -fno-strict-aliasing -pipe -D__CT_SYS_LINUX -DNO_SWSYNTH -D__CT_LITTLE_ENDIAN -fno-stack-protector \

Where in the file does this go?

---

### Post by NullHead on 2007-11-11
> **thepocketdrummer said:**
> Where in the file does this go?

Ok well you found the CFLAGS line in Makefile.config? well I always turn off text wrapping so that it's easier to find, but anyways you look to the end of the CFLAGS line in the text file and if it looks like this "CFLAGS = -Wall -fomit-frame-pointer -Os -fno-strict-aliasing -pipe -D__CT_SYS_LINUX -DNO_SWSYNTH -D__CT_LITTLE_ENDIAN -fno-stack-protector \" then you've already done what needs to be done, but if you haven't just copy and past the "-fno-stack-protector" line into the CFLAGS line right at the end ... just like in the example.

### Post by devzero on 2007-11-18
Since the driver wont start automatically you need to start it everytime manually.

i put a little start-script on the desktop:

#/bin/sh
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start

set it to executable, doubleclick it an press the terminalbutton bacause u need to enter your password. then it should work fine again.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2007-11-18
Well I know the problem that you're having ... I get the same thing, but what I do is edit my /etc/rc.local file with this command ```
sudo gedit /etc/rc.local
``` and add the following** before the designation** "*end"* ```
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```
and after I do that I use the program "sysv-rc-conf" to edit my run-levels witch are like modes that the computer uses to load different drivers ... so what I do is ```
sudo sysv-rc-conf
``` and look for the "ctalsa" in the list on the left
 
[IMG]http://i209.photobucket.com/albums/bb7/masterchief-117/screenshot.png[/IMG]

What you want to do is look for ctalsa in the list on the left and move over one space to the right until you're cursor is under the 2 then press space to check it .. then press "q" to quit the program and restart the computer and thats what does it for me.

PS: This should set you up to have the driver started automatically.

### Post by devzero on 2007-11-25
Hi,

I don't have the loop problem, but I cant restart the driver it says that its in use even
I don't have a soundprog open.

Your loop description sounds the same as I have it sometimes under windows i I enabled Line in, aux in, aux2, linein 2
( I have the Frontpanel (the Fatality version))

Try following: If you still have windows on another partition, boot it and disable all input sources with the "2" on it.

The Linux driver don't support the Front Panel but it dont change the settings, so if you enable the in and output of the front panel under windows, the linux driver will keep the settings, so I can use my Headphones on the front panel.

Maybe Digital out works the same way, as i cant afford a external digital sound device I cant test that :D


HTH

### Post by bawbag31 on 2007-11-25
Hi,
I thought that it was possibly a feedback loop, but muting all the inputs doesn't help.  What gets me is that it works fine for a while, then starts with the looping.  Something must be triggering it.  I'll try restarting alsa when I get home, I'm working all day today.  And to answer my own question about digital  ... from [http://connect.creativelabs.com/linux/FAQ/Home.aspx](http://connect.creativelabs.com/linux/FAQ/Home.aspx)

> ALSA Mixer does not show the S/PDIF module. Why?
The current driver has limited S/PDIF functionality only.

> I cannot play compressed AC3 signal from my external decoder. Why?
The current driver does not support external decoders.

By-the-by Linux Format magazine ran a big article months ago about building the ultimate linux box, as if you really needed a top of the range machine to play UT2004.  One of the pieces of recommended hardware was the X-Fi.  WTF!  At the time there was NO driver, not even a 64bit BETA one.  Shear muppetry!  And this is from (supposedly) the UK's premier Linux mag.

### Post by wolfc on 2007-11-26
For those of you experiencing crashes in the likes of:

[<ffffffff8873b72c>] :ctalsa:_ZN12RendererList4FindEP8_rndInfo+0x7a/0x90
[<ffffffff8873bead>] :ctalsa:_ZN13CtAudioDevice11QueryObjectERK5_GUIDPcS2_PP10ICtUnknown+0x73/0xb8
[<ffffffff8872977f>] :ctalsa:ctdrv_HwObj_ctor+0x295/0x317
[<ffffffff88728a75>] :ctalsa:snd_ctalsa_probe+0x253/0x3c6
[<ffffffff802b32ee>] __sysfs_make_dirent+0x1b/0x81
[<ffffffff802b337d>] sysfs_make_dirent+0x29/0x38
[<ffffffff8033a9c3>] pci_device_probe+0xca/0x12f
[<ffffffff8038d7ab>] driver_probe_device+0xd3/0x150
[<ffffffff8038d93e>] __driver_attach+0x6f/0xaf
[<ffffffff8038d8cf>] __driver_attach+0x0/0xaf
[<ffffffff8038d8cf>] __driver_attach+0x0/0xaf
[<ffffffff8038cb9d>] bus_for_each_dev+0x43/0x6e
[<ffffffff8038cf17>] bus_add_driver+0x7d/0x19a
[<ffffffff8033ab9b>] __pci_register_driver+0x5b/0x8d
[<ffffffff887af023>] :ctalsa:ctalsa_init+0x23/0x31
[<ffffffff8024a9ef>] sys_init_module+0x1540/0x162f
[<ffffffff8020939e>] system_call+0x7e/0x83

It appears the during the install the module dependencies are hard coded. So any time you change something that does a depmod, these custom dependencies are gone.

To fix the dependencies use the following work-around:
```
# cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04
# make update_modulesdep
```

### Post by NullHead on 2007-11-28
> **Westies said:**
> Well, it was working fine in a few audio apps I tried, pidgin, and flash videos in firefox. I installed the driver for my webcam which wasn't recognized until I rebooted. After rebooting, the X-Fi driver appears to have not started up. ALSA mixer only sees the mic in my webcam now. Any ideas?

edit: I tried "sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start" which resulted in:
 Loading X-Fi driver...
/etc/init.d/ctsound: line 90:  5816 Killed                  $modprobe ctalsa
edit2: tried "sudo modprobe ctalsa" and it just sits there as if it's loading something
edit3: I tried the first command again and now it stalls at "Loading X-Fi driver..."

You can add ```
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```To you're /etc/rc.local ```
sudo gedit /etc/rc.local
``` before the exit 0 to make it work. 

> **hackeron said:**
> Anyway to get this to work on 2.6.23?

It is reportedly nonfunctional on that kernel, but you're welcome to give it a try.

### Post by NullHead on 2007-11-28
OK well we can try this next ... 

Install sysv-rc.conf```
sudo apt-get install sysv-rc-conf
``` And run it ```
sudo sysv-rc-conf
``` and look down the list on the left called services and find "ctsound" and then move you're cursor under the two and press space to "X" that box ... press q to exit that window and save you're changes. Next restart you're computer.

### Post by wolfc on 2007-11-29
The more permanent solution for correct module dependencies:

/etc/modprobe.d/creative:
```
# Wolf: By default x-fi doesn't install blacklist correctly
# ctalsa must be loaded explicitly
blacklist ctalsa

# Wolf: always load the modules in correct order
install ctalsa  { for i in emupia ctsfman ct20xut ctexfifx cthwiut haxfi; do /sbin/modprobe $i; done } && /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install ctalsa
```

---

### Post by RiddareRalf on 2007-12-01

First of all, the guide at post #1 is refering to use wolfcs (I think) old patch version 0.1 and not 0.2.

Second of all, after I've recompiled my kernel I saw a post form a guy saying that it's not neccesary to recompile to an older kernel. So I formated again and tried this with my old kernel, and that's when my sound started looping.

So I formated yet again and used kernel 10 and then did EXACTLY as it says on #1 but with some "sudo" on some places that the threadmaker forgot.

Okay, well first it froze at sudo make install, then it froze at the login screen, but now (on the third reboot) it's working.

I've tried whole yesterday and whole today, a total of maybe 20 hours of pure configuring and just testing the easiest way to install this and I've got you who created this thread to thank for my audio.

Yet again, thanks.

### Post by wolfc on 2007-12-04
I posted the second patch [_here_]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3715824&postcount=122"). You must have missed the post. :)

So it's not Demik's patch you've got on the first page, but my old patch.

Anyway for kernel 2.6.23.9 this one on top of x-fi-0.2.patch:
```
diff --git a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c b/XF
index b23877b..8454bd8 100644
--- a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
+++ b/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
@@ -31,7 +31,9 @@ See bottom for preserved copyright notices
 #include <linux/errno.h>
 #include <linux/smp.h>
 #include <linux/delay.h>
+#include <linux/fs.h>
 
+#include <asm/fcntl.h>
 #include <asm/io.h>
 #include <asm/uaccess.h>
 #include <asm/irq.h>
```
Hmm, I need to tidy up my directory structure.

### Post by quad456 on 2007-12-04
Hi

Not to be negative on my first post but I installed Ubuntu 7.10 with high hopes of an out of box complete OS with all parts working and I am extremely dissapointed that something as basic as sound is so hard to get working. These XFI sound cards have been around for a while now. In 1997 I played around wih Red Hat and could play mp3 files with no problem on my old Sound Blaster AWE64.

How can Ubuntu be seriosuly considered over an OS like VISTA when it is such a pain just to get the sound card to work?? This is precisely the reason why Linux is not ready for prime time yet.

I will follow the instructions and thanks a lot for posting. I am just a bit dissapointed. Not sure whom to blame - Creative or Linux.

These XFI sound cards have been around for a while now. In 1997 I played around wih Red Hat and could play mp3 files with no problem on my old Sound Blaster AWE64.

How can Ubuntu be seriosuly considered over an OS like VISTA when it is such a pain just to get the sound card to work?? This is precisely the reason why Linux is not ready for prime time yet.

I will follow the instructions and thanks a lot for posting. I am just a bit dissapointed. Not sure whom to blame - Creative or Linux.

Please .. this is a support thread for creative's crappy driver .. lets keep this to helping not ranting, but on the subject blame creative NOT Ubuntu.

> **wolfc said:**
> I posted the second patch [_here_]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3715824&postcount=122"). You must have missed the post. :)

So it's not Demik's patch you've got on the first page, but my old patch.

Anyway for kernel 2.6.23.9 this one on top of x-fi-0.2.patch:
```
diff --git a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c b/XF
index b23877b..8454bd8 100644
--- a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
+++ b/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
@@ -31,7 +31,9 @@ See bottom for preserved copyright notices
 #include <linux/errno.h>
 #include <linux/smp.h>
 #include <linux/delay.h>
+#include <linux/fs.h>
 
+#include <asm/fcntl.h>
 #include <asm/io.h>
 #include <asm/uaccess.h>
 #include <asm/irq.h>
```
Hmm, I need to tidy up my directory structure.

... Yes I do think I missed it ... Ill get right on testing you're new patch on Gutsy :D

---

### Post by Westies on 2007-12-04
> **wolfc said:**
> Okay, I made sure the interrupt_callback is abiding the contract. Next patch version.

```
wget http://www.nerdnet.nl/~carlo/x-fi-0.2.patch && patch -p0 <x-fi-0.2.patch
cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04
./configure **CC=gcc-3.3**
make
sudo make install
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```
I uninstalled the 0,1 patched driver and installed the 0,2 patched one via this method. Sound is working again after the install. I'll report back in a moment to see if it'll work after a reboot.
edit: Rebooted and the startup Ubuntu sounds worked. Listening to some music in Audacious right now :D I removed the "sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start" from /etc/rc.local before installing the new driver. Just checked the file and it's still not there, yet it loads fine on boot. Lets see how long this works properly for :P Thanks for the help :)

---

[COLOR="Red"]Compiling a new kernel means you won't have your linux-restricted-modules package anymore ...  [/COLOR]

### Post by NullHead on 2007-12-04
> **quad456 said:**
> Fair enough. So far it is working nicely except for sounds. Before I get my Xfi working with this tutorial, should I be concerned about the following warning?:

[COLOR="Red"]Compiling a new kernel means you won't have your linux-restricted-modules package anymore ...  [/COLOR]

What does the linux-restricted modules package do? Do I even need it. Could I get it back after installing the creative driver?

Thanks.

Quad

Linux-restricted-modules are what makes the restricted-driver manager work. You need this file to use the manager to install you're fglrx or nvidia drivers that ubuntu recommends you use. Therefore I've liked to a program called envy witch will do this fore you. I used it and it worked like a charm but there is people that haven't had success like I have. 

Someone has made a guide for making a kernel with this restricted-modules package as well ... it's hear [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=594619](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=594619) I haven't tried it out, but feel free to give it a try and please come back and let us know if it worked for you or not :D

### Post by NullHead on 2007-12-07
> **Konrad-kun said:**
> Hi,

I've followed your tutorial and - wooh - the sound is working! Though it appears to me that it's looping when my processor is stressed, i.e when I open new window or something. But nevermind.

My question is - has anyone managed to get the surround sound working? Dunno about shared-jack, but here, on my independent jack connections it seems to just redirect all the sound to front speakers. And though it's great that I've the sound on Ubuntu, but still it's not "listening-to-music" ready. Anyone know how to enable surround?

Oh, and by the way, why is that sound on Amarok is unclear and noisy, and on Audacious/XMMS it's working as it "should"?

I'm not sure why that is .. I'm sorry, but I haven't had the time to test that yet. There is a newer patch you could try if you want to. Just use the following code. I'm not sure how this will effect you're already working sound, but if you want to you can. I'm going to test it in a little wile if you just want to wait on how it goes for me you're welcome to :D

```
wget http://www.nerdnet.nl/~carlo/x-fi-0.2.patch && patch -p0 <x-fi-0.2.patch
cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04
./configure CC=gcc-3.3
make
sudo make install
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```

---

### Post by NullHead on 2007-12-07
> **Konrad-kun said:**
> Hi,

I've followed your tutorial and - wooh - the sound is working! Though it appears to me that it's looping when my processor is stressed, i.e when I open new window or something. But nevermind.

My question is - has anyone managed to get the surround sound working? Dunno about shared-jack, but here, on my independent jack connections it seems to just redirect all the sound to front speakers. And though it's great that I've the sound on Ubuntu, but still it's not "listening-to-music" ready. Anyone know how to enable surround?

Oh, and by the way, why is that sound on Amarok is unclear and noisy, and on Audacious/XMMS it's working as it "should"?

OK I got it installed. Hears what I did.

unload the creative driver.```
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound stop
```

and then install ```
wget http://www.nerdnet.nl/~carlo/x-fi-0.2.patch && patch -p0 <x-fi-0.2.patch
cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04
./configure CC=gcc-3.3
make
sudo make install
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```

and then I added made this into a file like so.```
sudo nano /etc/modprobe.d/creative
``` and I copied this into it > # Wolf: By default x-fi doesn't install blacklist correctly
# ctalsa must be loaded explicitly
blacklist ctalsa

# Wolf: always load the modules in correct order
install ctalsa  { for i in emupia ctsfman ct20xut ctexfifx cthwiut haxfi; do /sbin/modprobe $i; done } && /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install ctalsa to do this in a  gnome terminal I used ctrl+shift+v and then save the file by doing ctrl+x and then pressing "y" and then pressing enter, you just made a file with some text in it :D thats why I love nano !!:popcorn: 

Restart you're computer and it should load up like normal and sound good.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2007-12-08
> **pasha2891 said:**
> Thanks, I don't want to trouble you, I have a comp sci background so you'd think I wouldn't be totaly helpless :).  I read up and it was saying that the main reason for the kernel change was that the kernel needed to be compiled with the SLAB option.  The problem I run into is when I try to install the deb for the linux-image it complains

"Failed to find suitable ramdisk generation tool for kernel version."

So this error is not related to your guide.  I'll keep looking hopefully I'll find out what I'm doing wrong.

OK I think I might know whats wrong. Try installing yaird ```
sudo apt-get install yaird
``` If you go through synaptic you can read the description. After the install of yaird try to remake you're kernel.```
make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

EDIT: also you should have initramfs-tools installed as well.```
sudo apt-get install initramfs-tools
```

### Post by NullHead on 2007-12-18
I've gotten it to work on testing before with the default kernel. I used this guide [here]("http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-587921.html"), but keep in mind it's a guide for Linux in general .. not gentoo only. Also make sure to have these things made with you're kernel. This was copied from the gentoo forums. > Required Kernel-Modules
Code:
Device Drivers  --->
Sound  --->
<M> Sound card support

Advanced Linux Sound Architecture  --->
<M> Advanced Linux Sound Architecture
<M>   Sequencer support
<M>     Sequencer dummy client
<M>   OSS Mixer API
<M>   OSS PCM (digital audio) API
[*]     OSS PCM (digital audio) API - Include plugin system
[*]   OSS Sequencer API
<M>   RTC Timer support (optional)
[*]     Use RTC as default sequencer timer
[*]   Dynamic device file minor numbers
[*]   Support old ALSA API

Generic devices  --->
<M> Virtual MIDI soundcard

PCI devices  --->
<M> Emu10k1 (SB Live!, Audigy, E-mu APS)

The bare minimum to ALSA is Virtual "MIDI soundcard" (CONFIG_SND_VIRMIDI=m), "Emu10k1 (SB Live!, Audigy, E-mu APS)" (CONFIG_SND_EMU10K1=m)

### Post by wolfc on 2007-12-20
I finally got a good trace via netconsole for one of the hangups.

Although is says 'kernel BUG', I wouldn't bet any money on that. :mrgreen:

Anyway don't hold your breath for a fix, because this one is tricky. It can be a race condition because of SMP (can someone reproduce this on single core? I can't.), some poking in the driver going badly or a locking problem (on the interrupt).

```
[  821.932742] ------------[ cut here ]------------
[  821.932840] kernel BUG at kernel/sched.c:612!
[  821.932929] invalid opcode: 0000 [1] SMP 
[  821.933095] CPU 1 
[  821.933212] Modules linked in: ctalsa(PF) haxfi(PF) cthwiut(PF) ctexfifx(PF) ct20xut(PF) ctsfman(PF) emupia(PF) snd_pcm_oss snd_mixer_oss snd_pcm snd_page_alloc ctossrv(P) snd_seq_midi_emul snd_seq_dummy snd_seq_oss snd_seq_midi snd_rawmidi snd_seq_midi_event snd_seq snd_timer snd_seq_device snd soundcore netconsole nls_utf8 ntfs fuse ipv6 nvidia(P) af_packet i2c_nforce2 psmouse serio_raw k8temp i2c_core pcspkr button shpchp pci_hotplug tsdev joydev evdev ext3 jbd mbcache raid10 raid456 async_xor async_memcpy async_tx xor raid1 raid0 multipath linear md_mod dm_mirror sg ide_cd cdrom sd_mod ata_generic generic usb_storage usbhid hid libusual floppy sata_nv ohci_hcd libata scsi_mod amd74xx forcedeth ehci_hcd usbcore thermal processor fan dm_mod
[  821.937072] Pid: 0, comm: swapper Tainted: PF       2.6.23.9-alien #1

[  821.939687]  <IRQ>  [<ffffffff80231c8a>] try_to_wake_up+0x2ea/0x370

### Post by garydale on 2007-12-20
Thanks for the Howto. I tried it on a computer running 7.10 with an Athlon 64X2 processor. It eventually worked. A few notes:

Gutsy Gibbon uses the 2.6.22-14 kernel not the 2.6.22-10 you mention. I download the 2.6.22-14 kernel instead and worked things from there. I had previously tried the Ubuntu kernel sources but the one they had posted was 2.6.22-9. I don't know what patches they've applied but the kernel.org version seems to work OK.

I ran into some problems with the make process. It was failing looking for an alsa header. I'm not sure if that was a result of some earlier attempts I'd made or if it really did need the alsa drivers headers. Anyway, I cleaned out my old code and went back to step one after downloading the asla drivers source and putting it in /usr/src. The make then worked.

Make install locked up my computer each time I tried it however. The last message on the console was that it couldn't find chkconfig. I haven't looked at what else it was doing beyond that, but it appears that it was complete enough to work. I finished off the last steps in your howto and the sound driver loaded.

Running with the custom kernel means that the proprietary drivers applet doesn't work. If you need to update your ATI or NVidia driver, you will need to reboot into the generic kernel.

---

### Post by wolfc on 2007-12-22
> **czarnyckm said:**
> ...

Please try the following patch. It won't solve your problem, but it should give an indication what is wrong.

```
diff --git a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c b/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
index 30dc605..9149d8c 100644
--- a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
+++ b/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
@@ -1563,7 +1563,15 @@ sysGetFileSize(CTVOID *This, CTDWORD *pdwSize, CTHANDLE hFile)
     */
     
     if (!pFileHdl) 
+       {
+               printk(KERN_WARNING "sysGetFileSize: no file handle\n");
         CTRETURN(E_INVALIDARG);
+       }
+    if (!pdwSize) 
+       {
+               printk(KERN_WARNING "sysGetFileSize: no pointer to return value (pdwSize)\n");
+        CTRETURN(E_INVALIDARG);
+       }
 
 #if LINUX_VERSION_CODE < KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
     set_fs(get_ds());
@@ -1572,6 +1580,8 @@ sysGetFileSize(CTVOID *This, CTDWORD *pdwSize, CTHANDLE hFile)
         offset = lseek(nFD, 0, SEEK_END);
         close(nFD);
     }
+       else
+               printk(KERN_ERR "sysGetFileSize: failed to open file %s\n", pFileHdl->szPath);
     set_fs(fs);
 #else
     filp = filp_open(pFileHdl->szPath, pFileHdl->nFlags, pFileHdl->nMode);
@@ -1579,6 +1589,8 @@ sysGetFileSize(CTVOID *This, CTDWORD *pdwSize, CTHANDLE hFile)
         offset = filp->f_op->llseek(filp, 0, SEEK_END);
         filp_close(filp, current->files);
     }
+       else
+               printk(KERN_ERR "sysGetFileSize: failed to open file %s\n", pFileHdl->szPath);
 #endif
 
 #if LINUX_VERSION_CODE < KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
```

### Post by wolfc on 2007-12-24
There seems to be a problem with the interrupt handler and multi cpu. I've tried just about everything to get the handler to stop firing, but in the end another cpu will pick up too soon. This might actually be a kernel bug after all. I think the other drivers are just being too nice to the kernel. ;)

As I said, I'm unable to reproduce this single core. So the patch is actually very simple: disable SMP during modprobe. :D

Finally I can hear jingle bells again. :biggrin:

```
diff --git a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/ctsound b/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/ctsound
index 04519f9..c4bbe8f 100755
--- a/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/ctsound
+++ b/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/ctsound
@@ -7,6 +7,45 @@
 # chkconfig: 2345 95 5
 # description: Load or unload Creative X-Fi Linux driver.
 
+set -e
+
+# Wolf: allow for cpu handling
+
+# Disable all cpus except cpu0
+# and return their current status
+disable_cpus()
+{
+	set -e
+	state=`cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu[!0]/online`
+	set $state
+	for i in /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu[!0]/online; do
+		# Don't disable a cold cpu
+		if [ $1 == 1 ]; then
+			echo 0 >$i
+		fi
+		shift
+	done
+	echo $state
+}
+
+# Enable cpus except cpu0
+# based on the state argument
+enable_cpus()
+{
+	set -e
+	if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then
+		echo 1>&2 "enable_cpus: states expected"
+		return 1
+	fi
+	# TODO: sanity check on state
+	for i in /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu[!0]/online; do
+		if [ $1 == 1 ]; then
+			echo $1 >$i
+		fi
+		shift
+	done
+}
+
 if [ -x /sbin/lsmod ]; then
 	lsmod=/sbin/lsmod
 else
@@ -100,6 +139,8 @@ load_mods() {
 		chmod 666 /dev/x-fi;
 	fi
 
+	# Disable SMP during modprobe
+	state=`disable_cpus`
 	if [ -f /etc/creative/creative.state ]; then
 		#echo "Restore Drv Default Mix Val"
 		$modprobe ctalsa && alsactl -f /etc/creative/creative.state restore > /dev/null 2>&1 || :
@@ -108,6 +149,7 @@ load_mods() {
 		#echo "alsactl restore"
 		$modprobe ctalsa && alsactl restore >/dev/null 2>&1 || :
 	fi
+	enable_cpus $state
 }
 
 # Unload all modules
```

---

### Post by mrhorrible78 on 2007-12-24
Just a quick note, While the walkthrough worked straight away for many things I still had problems getting system sounds to work as well as anything that'd try and play through the browser such as Youtube videos, and I think anything that utilized gstreamer.  Just in case anyone runs in to the same problems here is what eventually worked for me.  I'm not sure precisely why it wasn't working, though I suspect it's because I have an onboard soundcard too.

First, Ensure that the X-Fi is listed in ALSA's Configuration, you should see something like this:
```
sudo asoundconf list
Please note that you are attempting to run asoundconf as a privileged superuser, which may have unintended consequences.

Names of available sound cards:
V8237
1 [X-Fi           ]: X-Fi - Creative X-Fi [b400]

```

Second, Set the default ALSA device using this command:

```
sudo asoundconf set-default-card x-fi
```

After doing this, I was able to hear system sounds, YouTube videos and such.  I haven't found anything yet that didn't work.  Your mileage may vary.

---

### Post by mrhorrible78 on 2007-12-24
Oh yeah, after doing that, I was able to go back to System > Preferences > Sound  and get everything to work by specifying "Autodetect" rather than manually specifying ALSA.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2007-12-31
I would recommend updating to Gutsy , but you don't actually need to. You don't need to make the new kernel BTW. Also I would start over by removing the working directory ```
sudo rm -rf XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04
``` and starting from  the "Install X-FI driver" step. I'm not sure why this is, but I also have this sometimes and doing this helps.

PS: yes this is my most up to date version of this guide.

### Post by erluk on 2008-01-01
I have the same problem as czarnyckm: a NULL pointer in ctossrv:sysGetFileSize.

For me, the crash happens in this line:
```
offset = filp->f_op->llseek(filp, 0, SEEK_END);
```

Here, llseek is NULL. My kernel version is 2.6.22.15, and the filesystem in question is ReiserFS.

Is this a kernel bug?

EDIT: I was able to solve that crash.

**Warning: Uneducated guessing ahead! ;)**

This is a patch for the kernel's fs/reiserfs/file.c that adds the llseek field to the file operations.

```
--- fs/reiserfs/file.c.old      2008-01-02 03:38:15.000000000 +0100
+++ fs/reiserfs/file.c  2008-01-02 02:03:23.000000000 +0100
@@ -1521,6 +1521,7 @@ static ssize_t reiserfs_file_write(struc
 }
 
 const struct file_operations reiserfs_file_operations = {
+       .llseek = generic_file_llseek,
        .read = do_sync_read,
        .write = reiserfs_file_write,
        .ioctl = reiserfs_ioctl,

```

After recompiling the kernel and rebooting, the X-Fi finally works! :)

Thanks to NullHead for the how-to and to wolfc for the diagnostic LinuxSys.c patch!

### Post by DualC on 2008-01-07
Now i have tried many diffrent How-to's to make a X-FI driver for my system....
And today whis evening i sucess... i use this How-to's

<-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------->

I managed to get the X-Fi driver compiled on Ubuntu 7.10 (amd64). However, like GAThrawn says, it's buggy as hell. I'm going to post my steps here and see if it helps anyone else out (these are taken from an amalgam of posts):

First, you have to get your system ready for recompiling the kernel:

$ sudo apt-get install linux-source-2.6.22
$ sudo apt-get install libqt3-mt-dev
$ sudo apt-get install kernel-package
$ cd /usr/src
$ sudo tar xvjf linux-source*
$ cd linux-source*
$ cp /boot/config-`uname -r` ./.config
$ sudo make xconfig

Load profile from /boot/linux-latest-kernel-you've-installed

Look for (find) "SLAB" and turn it on.
Save changes to .config, exit.

Then compile the kernel (will take about an hour):
$ sudo make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=flashstar kernel_image kernel_headers

This will create a pair of .deb files in /usr/src, which you need to install:
$ sudo dpkg -i linux-image-2.6.22.9flashstar_2.6.22.9flashstar-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
$ sudo dpkg -i linux-headers-2.6.22.9flashstar_2.6.22.9flashstar-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb

If you're running any restricted drivers (like nvidia) disable them, reboot, and select the new kernel within grub.

After the new kernel is booted, follow the directions here: [http://blackbox.lostwave.net/x-fi/readme.txt](http://blackbox.lostwave.net/x-fi/readme.txt)

You will have to edit ctsound and change the first line to /bin/bash. After running "sudo ./configure", edit Makefile.conf and add -fno-stack-protector to the CFLAGS line.

From the forum Thread: 

[http://forums.creative.com/creativelabs/board/message?board.id=soundblaster&message.id=31220&view=by_date_ascending&page=96](http://forums.creative.com/creativelabs/board/message?board.id=soundblaster&message.id=31220&view=by_date_ascending&page=96)

<-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------->

After this, i use the last part ...in NullHeads How-To, aka this Thread.

[Now we need to set the correct order of how the driver is loaded with the rest of thing that load on boot-up. So to do that type the following]

[Code:]

$ gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/creative

[copy and past this text into the editor that should have opened.]

[Quote:]

# Wolf: By default x-fi doesn't install blacklist correctly
# ctalsa must be loaded explicitly
blacklist ctalsa

# Wolf: always load the modules in correct order
install ctalsa { for i in emupia ctsfman ct20xut ctexfifx cthwiut haxfi; do /sbin/modprobe $i; done } && /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install ctalsa

[Save the file and close the window.]

[Due to errors in the loading of the driver upon restarting ... there is another step.]

[You need to add a line to you're rc.local file. This file is read on boot and the commands in it are executed.]

[So to do that we need to open it first]

[Code:]

gksudo gedit /etc/rc.local

[and add the text]

[Code:]

sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start

[in the space before the]

[Quote:]

exit 0

<-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------->

Anyway, i got sound.. BUT.. the systemsounds in Ubuntu it's kinda looped then i log into the system, and it's kinda annoying..

Does somone got any suggestion how, to make what stop ?

/M

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-01-07
You should install Wolfc's patch. It will fix that problem. Also you should follow the "Install X-FI driver" section of my guide because it goes through the step of using gcc-3.3 for the driver.

### Post by devzero on 2008-01-08
hmm which kernel do you use? the gutsy slub kernel or your slab kernel?

Did you completely reunpack the crative directory and applyed the patch from wolfc?

Wolfs patch is this:
```

wget http://www.nerdnet.nl/~carlo/x-fi-0.2.patch && patch -p0 <x-fi-0.2.patch

```

as you see in nullheads howto.
He updated his how to after wolfc gave us his patch, so you should follow it, so you only get wolfs version.

---

### Post by Matthew_PL on 2008-01-08
SLAB kernel, kernel is own 2.6.23, I applyed the patch from wolfc.
I think that I don't have some headers but i don't know which. :(

---

### Post by Colin The Grey on 2008-01-10
I also get the hard lock when executing - sudo make install

However continuing the install procedure after a reboot seems to work fine, sound is working pretty well with the 2.6.22 Kernel.

System specs for anyone interested:

Asus P5K Deluxe.
Q6600 G0 @ 3.2GHz
4 Gb Ram
Soundblaster Fatality Xi-Fi Extreme Gamer Pro (I think - lol).
8800GTX

Before I installed I did a total fresh setup of Ubuntu from the 7.10 Live DVD, Added all updates and fixes, then installed Envy but did not install the Driver.

After rebooting into the new 2.6.22 Kernel with sound working I then ran Envy and installed the Nvidia driver.

EDIT:

Well after some more testing:

System sounds - all working fine.
XMSS - clear sound no errors.
Mplayer - good sound but the occasional crackle - still a lot better than no sound.

---

### Post by mnnn on 2008-01-10
Hello, This worked for me! But I'm surprised that there's **NO 5.1 support.** No upmix like CMSS (which I didn't expect, but expected simple upmix) and even not basic 5.1 support. I tried to play 5.1 movie in VLC. In stereo mode played flawlessly. Just great. But when I switched to 5.1 it played only in 2 front speakers (FL,FR) and there was glitches like cracking, but I was able to hear something.

There isn't any setting (K)ubuntu sound system (any, like ALSA...) to set type of speakers (headphones, 2,5.1,7.1...)???? Veery weird, but I know it would depend on drivers. However it isn't just there!

Can anybody help with 5.1 support?

Another problem is **MIDI synth support**. If I set Dosbox to start with X-Fi HwSynth, there's no music (e.g. MIDI synth) in games. And there are 3-4 thin horizontal white lines through screen, Dosbox is then likely to crash. If i play something in pmidi with X-Fi HwSynth I only get weird sound glitches and pmidi freezes. In Timidity it plays OK but the synth itself is ugly.

It's hard, but **I'm switching back to windows** as there's no decent sound support for my XFi Gamer in Kubuntu (I don't demand stuff like EAX, but CMSS could be handy and 5.1 Logitech speakers, I wasn't able to make compiz window manager to run. ATI sucks. And only Total Commander ran in Wine correctly (from programs). I really liked Kubuntu. Great system but I don't blame linux or someting else. I just wanted to do basic things under linux and I'm not able to do it.

Anyway **thanks for the HOWTO**. There weren't any problems! Distro: Kubuntu 7.10 Gutsy Gibbon.

Does anybody have 5.1 support?

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-01-10
> **mnnn said:**
> Hello, This worked for me! But I'm surprised that there's **NO 5.1 support.** No upmix like CMSS (which I didn't expect, but expected simple upmix) and even not basic 5.1 support. I tried to play 5.1 movie in VLC. In stereo mode played flawlessly. Just great. But when I switched to 5.1 it played only in 2 front speakers (FL,FR) and there was glitches like cracking, but I was able to hear something.

There isn't any setting (K)ubuntu sound system (any, like ALSA...) to set type of speakers (headphones, 2,5.1,7.1...)???? Veery weird, but I know it would depend on drivers. However it isn't just there!

Can anybody help with 5.1 support?

Another problem is **MIDI synth support**. If I set Dosbox to start with X-Fi HwSynth, there's no music (e.g. MIDI synth) in games. And there are 3-4 thin horizontal white lines through screen, Dosbox is then likely to crash. If i play something in pmidi with X-Fi HwSynth I only get weird sound glitches and pmidi freezes. In Timidity it plays OK but the synth itself is ugly.

It's hard, but **I'm switching back to windows** as there's no decent sound support for my XFi Gamer in Kubuntu (I don't demand stuff like EAX, but CMSS could be handy and 5.1 Logitech speakers, I wasn't able to make compiz window manager to run. ATI sucks. And only Total Commander ran in Wine correctly (from programs). I really liked Kubuntu. Great system but I don't blame linux or someting else. I just wanted to do basic things under linux and I'm not able to do it.

Anyway **thanks for the HOWTO**. There weren't any problems! Distro: Kubuntu 7.10 Gutsy Gibbon.

Does anybody have 5.1 support?

I too would like surround sound, but it just isn't possible with the current driver. You can review the driver features [here]("http://opensource.creative.com/soundcard.html#X-FI"), but please be aware that it is still in beta stage and isn't even a finished product. I'm also it worked for you in the first place. 

Creative does say there should be mini playback through alsa > ALSA MIDI Playback, but I've never really used midi that much.

---

### Post by mnnn on 2008-01-10
Yeah, but that MIDI just don't work. At least for me. And I don't think Creative will release new drivers (soon), because they've done this beta, so we can't blame them that XFI owners doesn't have ANY sound on Linux

OT: why isn't current (K)Ubuntu (or other distributions) compiled with SLAB? It's depricated or something and Creative is lazy to use SLUB? Thanks.

### Post by kiil on 2008-01-15
Yay, I finally got it to work. 

Many thanks to erluk, wolfc and NullHead

NullHead, you should consider updating the HOWTO with a note about the reiserFS issue and a link to erluk's post (#258). A filesystem error was definitely not the thing I thought of as I was troubleshooting my sound card. And wolfc, I don't think it would hurt anyone if the SMP patch to the ctsound file (from #250) was included in the general patch.

Good work all of you!

Cheers
Kiil

```

What I need to do?

I ran into this problem also. One of the guys over at Gentoo found that LinuxSys.c was missing a couple of includes.
jmrk@gentooforums wrote:

open XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c
and add the following include-lines somewhere at the top of the file:

//added for 2.6.23 kernel
#include <linux/fs.h>
#include <asm-generic/fcntl.h> 

I tried it, and the driver built on 2.6.23.X

### Post by agentmarmite on 2008-01-23
Hi guys,

I'm an Ubuntu noob, but I've been keeping up with this for quite sometime now and thought I'd register to say thanks for all your hard work on this issue. I myself have an XFi Elite Pro and have to dual boot between XP and Ubuntu due to the sound not working, which is ashame as I'd like to leave behind XP altogether and it's the last thing holding me back.

Knowing that clever folks as yourselves are working on this (most of it is WAY to technical for me to grasp) is good enough for me to hang on in there and see if anything comes of it which I might be able to use.

I'm going to leave a grumpy message with Creative this afternoon. Thanks again for all our efforts and I'll cross my fingers that one day I'll be able to use my snazzy new soundcard in my even snazzier new OS.

Regards,

**Agent Marmite** (secret operative)

.....
#

make[1]: Leaving directory `/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.22-14-generic'

scripts/kconfig/conf -s arch/x86_64/Kconfig

I did change my architecture from Generic to Core 2 in the menuconfig, but the error to me seems like its trying to use the 2.6.22-14 headers when I've down loaded the 2.6.22.10 as per the first page of here....

Any ideas gratefully accepted...

Thanks....

Although I did:

```
ln -s linux-2.6.22.10 linux
```

Edit 2: Fixed. Had to delete the link as root and recreate it to 2.6.22-10

### Post by Philio on 2008-01-26
Great guide, thanks, worked perfectly for me.

I had some problems with odd things not working using the kernel.org source (not the driver specifically) so I compiled using the Ubuntu source which solved these problems for me.

I also managed to recompile the linux restricted modules which is a bit of a pain, but is handy to get your nvidia/ati driver working again.

If anyone wants to save some time, here are my compiled packages:

Kernel: [http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-image-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb]("http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-image-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb")
Kernel headers: [http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-headers-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb]("http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-headers-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb")
Linux Restricted Modules: [http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.4-14.9_amd64.deb]("http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.4-14.9_amd64.deb")

Note: I compiled for Intel CPU and added dmraid45 module support,

---

### Post by Kosh42 on 2008-01-26
Got it working. Thanks for the howto....

Only issues I had were an unresponsive PC when the kernel packages had finished their build, and a complete freeze when I did sudo make install for the driver... Seemed to still work...

> Great guide, thanks, worked perfectly for me.

If anyone wants to save some time, here are my compiled packages:

Kernel: [http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-image-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb]("http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-image-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb")
Kernel headers: [http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-headers-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb]("http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-headers-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.9-14-generic-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb")
Linux Restricted Modules: [http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.4-14.9_amd64.deb]("http://www.philbayfield.com/downloads/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.22.9-14-generic_2.6.22.4-14.9_amd64.deb")

Note: I compiled for Intel CPU and added dmraid45 module support,

Philio,
I would love to save some time (and not screw up) since I am totally new to Ubuntu.  How would I utilize the compiled packages you have provided?  I am also running an Intel CPU and Creative X-Fi card.
Thanks for any help you can provide.
RA

### Post by XPS600 on 2008-01-26
Success! :)  It's my first day with Linux, and everything is going smoothly so far thanks to the great support and information on this community forums.  Way better than calling up some help desk!

Many many thanks to Nullhead, wolfc, et.al. for generating this solution.  And also for sticking with it, and creating patches to help us out.

I followed the tutorial exactly, using a new install of 7.10 GG.  I was tempted to compile with the 2.6.24, but it seems out of my league to troubleshoot those potential issues, and what is the benefit anyways?

I had minor problems similar to other users, including the lock-up at the sudo make install step. But I just followed Nullhead's advice for deleting the folder and restarting at the XFi install step.

For the record, based on their cruddy support, this soundcard is the last creative product I ever purchase (even though that zen player looks nice).

Thanks Again!

### Post by Siyfion on 2008-01-30
Being the curious kind of person I am, I was wondering if someone would explain to me why a kernel change is required to make the Creative package work? Im sure Creative could build a version of the drivers which would work without? (But obviously Creative, being Creative, wont!)

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-01-30
Yes creative won't because they're creative! :lolflag:

You need to redo you're kernel because the kernel that was compiled for gutsy gibbon uses the slub allocator rather than the needed slab allocator. Now I'm still fairly new to all this kernel stuff, but I was told to use slab .vs slub ... I hope that helps, but I can tell you that the kernel in Feisty Fawn or 7.04 work out of the box ... all you need to do is run the x-fi portion of the guide and you should have it up and running.

---

### Post by Siyfion on 2008-01-30
> **NullHead said:**
>  I hope that helps, but I can tell you that the kernel in Feisty Fawn or 7.04 work out of the box ... all you need to do is run the x-fi portion of the guide and you should have it up and running.

Thats the main point I was looking for! Also I noticed that there is a new kernel available since the guide was written, 2.6.24 is that what GG is built with, thus the reason it works?

EDIT: Also whats the purpose of the patch bit of the installation? What does the patch do? and more importantly, is it still necessary?

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-01-30
Yes the patch is necessary and no the default kernel will not work. The kernel that is suggested installed is the same version of the one included in 7.10, but simple put Ubuntu gave they're kernel a different version number. 

The patch is necessary because, Creative failed at making a proper install system or working drivers. As near as I can tell the patches heavily patch the libraries for better functionality.

&#65279;[INDENT][SIZE="2"]/bin/sh: .depend: Permission denied

### Post by JasonFH on 2008-01-30
It seems that I have figured it out.  And your tutorial worked great.  After a re-boot and a change in the BIOS it's fixed.

It turns out if I "disable hardware memory hole" in my BIOS it will limit me to 3GB of RAM (vs the 4 I have).  But sound works.  I had a similar problem in Vista 64, where the static occurred intermittently while playing sounds, but not constant like this.

For anyone else with a similar "Satic that will make your ears bleed" problem, my system specs are below and switching the hardware memory hole setting in my BIOS to disabled did the trick.

Ubuntu Gusty 7.10 with 2.6.22.10 Kernel
Vista Ultimate X64

ASUS A8N32-SLI Deluxe BIOS 1405

AMD FX-60 Toledo (OC'd to 2.86 GHz with a Zalman 9700 heatsink)

4GB OCZ PC3200 @ 220MHz (PC3520) 2.5,3,2,5 2T

eVGA 7950 512MB 560MHz

Creative X-FI Extreme Gamer

150GB Raptor, 74GB Raptor, 120GB WD HD

FSP Group FX700 Power Supply

Saitek X52 Joystick

Logitech Z-5300 Speaker System

Antec P180 w/ a sunbeam fan controller for the 4 120MM case fans

---

### Post by Syn- on 2008-01-30
> **JasonFH said:**
> It seems that I have figured it out.  And your tutorial worked great.  After a re-boot and a change in the BIOS it's fixed.

It turns out if I "disable hardware memory hole" in my BIOS it will limit me to 3GB of RAM (vs the 4 I have).  But sound works.  I had a similar problem in Vista 64, where the static occurred intermittently while playing sounds, but not constant like this.

For anyone else with a similar "Satic that will make your ears bleed" problem, my system specs are below and switching the hardware memory hole setting in my BIOS to disabled did the trick.


I am having the exact same issue you were experiencing and I wish my BIOS had this option, but alas Dell seems to skimp on the 'normal' options - though I can't complain since it's a computer provided by work. 

My computer is a Dell XPS 720, Q6600, 4GB memory, 8800GT.  

Ironically, I also changed the Processor type when I recompiled the kernel to Intel Core2  instead of AMD naturally (and also adjusted the Kernel preemptive setting) as a start on a custom kernel.  Currently, I'm recompiling my kernel again, going back to default with ONLY the setting changed to get this to work to see if that could potentially be my issue. 

If not, I guess I'll pull an "old" (but reliable) SB Live! card out of an old system.

UPDATE: After trying a recompile of the kernel, again, to change the processor type to generic the computer first hung up on reboot then proceeded to crash. Time to find my SB Live! card until the drivers become stable...

### Post by NullHead on 2008-02-05
> **thepocketdrummer said:**
> It fails... and gives no reason why.

Then when I try to uninstall, it says this...

```
E: oss-linux: subprocess pre-removal script returned error exit status 2
```

How do I fix this?

Maybe this would be more informational. 

Try using the terminal to remove the oss-linux package.```
sudo dpkg -r oss-linux
```

If it returns with an error you'll be able to see it and paste it here. If/When you're done uninstalling it try it re-install it.
EDIT: Also I think you should make sure to have build-essential installed ```
sudo apt-get install build-essential
```
 ```
sudo dpkg -i oss-linux*.deb
```

### Post by NullHead on 2008-02-05
Yes I suppose I should have elaborated on this a little bit ... well here goes.

This driver is still the same beta driver that was released by Creative, it is simply included in the new OSS driver for ease of installation. I've noticed (through the 3 hours I spent testing)  that it doesn't seem to integrate very well with Ubuntu. The original method seemed to play on the main sound server, Alsa, where as this OSS driver is the secondary sound server and doesn't really have the same setup as Alsa. 8.04 is supposed to be using pulseaudio witch (from what I've read) is just a wrapper for other sound systems e.g Alsa and OSS, witch means that (I'm hoping) it should work better with 8.04 .vs  7.10. 

Now everyone needs to remember that the features of this driver are limited and using you're built in sound device (thought I don't) is still the best course of action for stability, surround sound and ease of use.

With this new OSS driver, I've only gotten pidgin, xmms and audacious to work, because they can have they're default sound server manually set to what ever you want. Programs such as bmpx and the startup sound Ubuntu make when you login don't seem to work as one might expect. 

I've come to realize that I have a bad memory module and I can't do any more testing until I get a new one. So I will be doing less testing. So it might be up in the air for a wile.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-02-09
Well there was supposed to be a warning about loosing you're restricted drivers manager. If you missed let me inform you that when you made your new kernel it causes the restricted drivers manager to stop working. The solution to this is rather complicated and I haven't tried it ... so I recommend you download [envy]("http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html") and install your graphics drivers from there. If you used the restricted drivers manager for you're bcm43xx card than I'd suggest [this thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=405990"). 

Note: The wifi thread  should work in gutsy ... contrary to what the thread says it should work.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-02-13
> **rasczak said:**
> delete the directory I installed it into?

Is there any command to run to see If I have all the necessary things installed to get it working right?

To check if your kernel is active run this:```
uname -r
``` and to check if the driver got installed all of the way, run this:```
sudo /etc/init.d/ctsound start
```It'll either crash the computer or it'll say "Loading X-FI driver"(or something to that effect) and halt, or it'll it will complain about something not being somewhere.
