### Post by devUser on 2008-03-05
Took me about 2 days and a huge pile of errors to work through, but got it working.. **And what is more, i got the XFi AND NVidia (8800GTX) working (and compiz!) at the same time on ubuntu 7.10.** (using kernel 2.6.22-14-novastar2 (yes, i named it myself hehe)) (Had to compile 3 or 4 kernels before i got it though)

A tip for people who want to have both sound and compiz working:

It seemed to me that the graphics and sound driver are depending on the actually running kernel at the time they were compiled. This would mean that first compiling and installing linux-restricted-modules and/or XFi driver and then building and compiling the kernel is not the way to go.
Some time yesterday i got the sound driver kind of working though.. The sound was stuttering and repeating everything that was played. I don't know what the exact cause of that is, but maybe it has something to do with kernel settings, as I played around a little with some other options as well. (Like setting the preemtion model for the low-latency desktop..) With that kernel, i also got things that looked like hangups, but in fact, every time this happened, the system would also unfreeze after something like 15 seconds or so..

Just to give my two cents back to the community, I'll try to give some (hopefully helpful) instructions or rather guidance. I hope people reading this (yes, that is you too! ;) ) won't see this as a rule, but more like a guideline; a way to understand the process. Names might have slight differences, but the goal is understanding here. Because with understanding comes the possibility to resolve errors yourself.. And then perhaps maybe someone who is new to linux (and wants to contribute) might even solve this problem once and for all ;) (after all, i consider myself only just beyond noob stage and i fixed it over here :) )

sources i used:
The first post in this thread of course! (tnx a million NullHead!)
[This thread](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=594619)
The sources at [this](http://blackbox.lostwave.net/x-fi/) directory.
[This thread about kernel compilation for noobs](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=56835)
[This documentation and howto about building custom restricted modules](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/CustomRestrictedModules)

1. download the kernel sources and dependencies for building etc..
2. configure the kernel (don't forget setting the SLUB to SLAB).. (I also set the cpu model to amd64, so in case anyone is wondering if they should not, it clearly works fine.)
3. download the sources for the restricted modules (linux-restricted-modules-common)
4. Check the abi version and while you are there, modify those files according to instructions from the various howto's. (check out [this thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=594619") as well)
5. Build and install your kernel.. Don't forget to use the abi version with --append-to-version (it's kind of a waste to discover after 50 minutes of building that you forgot this little detail ;)
6. Reboot and see if this kernel will let you do the basics.. (remember to use uname -r to see if you are using the kernel you are expecting to use)
7. Compile the restricted modules (it was giving me errors that something in the 2.6.22-generic did not exist.. So i threw away that entire directory and symlinked it to the 2.6.22-14-novastar/ i was working on anyway and this worked. I am suspecting some hardcoded -generic references though..)
8. Install the restricted modules (this is the part that i find could use some extra attention, as i don't see any code examples, so i will give them here.. For me this worked:
```
dpkg -i linux-restricted-modules-2.6.22-14-novastar2_2.6.22.4-14.10_amd64.deb
dpkg -i nvidia-glx-new_100.14.19+2.6.22.4-14.10_amd64.deb
dpkg -i nvidia-glx-new-dev_100.14.19+2.6.22.4-14.10_amd64.deb
``` (after this, you may get notifications from some update manager that there is a "new" nvidia driver package available. The problem, as described in the restricted modules documentation, is that this package does not have a version number itself, so there is no way to determine if it really is a newer version or not. I would recommend waiting with installing this "update" until after you got the whole shebang working. I for me am doubting ever to update this module and i think i might even put this one on ignore if i knew how)
9. Go to the appearance settings in the gnome menu and check the extra visual effects on the visual effects tab. It will ask you if you want to enable the driver, answer yes and close the appearance settings, then reboot and check it's working.
10. Now, download the XFi driver tar.gz archive, unzip/tar it and follow the rest of NullHead's instructions, as they worked like a charm for me :)

To bad that the buttons on the console of my XFi Elite are now almost without function (oh yes, i can press the volume knob to mute/unmute, whooy! :) )

Although i suspect that Creative is far too busy getting the drivers to play nice on vista, maybe they will do something with these complaints/tips and make a driver that is more compatible for the more standard kernels out there and actually get all the functions of this awesome soundboard working.. I'm not complaining (yet).

Tnx and hope to help some of u out there!

---

Code:

make-kpkg clean
make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers

### Post by devUser on 2008-03-10
> **NullHead said:**
> Thanks for the post I'm glad you have your sound working :D 

I will try the documentation on the custom linux-restricted-modules and see if I can't just post a .deb here for all to use :D I might be able to pre-make some Intel and AMD kernels so everyone can just download 'em and install them.

Very welcome.. I hope someone will benefit from it.. Just adding my two cents back to the community :)

Too bad i couldn't quite get the feeling of ubuntu and am now in the process of looking for something that will suit me better.. So i'm now writing this from mandriva 2008.0.. It's gcc3.3 supported and what is more, i looked at the config for the kernel i'm currently using, and it looks like this kernel was compiled with SLAB in stead of the usual SLUB! So i guess that means i don't have to compile a kernel myself to get the SLAB allocator back.. Unless the building of the creative driver actually DOES patch the kernel sources, like i've suspected before.. I still find that kinda hard to believe though..

There is a major plus for ubuntu gutsy though: I've tried to get it working on fedora, gentoo, pclinuxos and ubuntu and the latter was the only one on which i got it to work. On fedora i got it to work as far a success for the test function for the soundcard detection.. But that's it.. Ubuntu 7.10 was the only one on which i had it working with xmms and acceptable quality sound (so no loops, noise or that kind of problems).
(honesty obliges me to say that pclinuxos made a good chance as it had a 2.6.22 kernel and gcc-3.3, but unfortunately, i didn't pay enough attention and it turned out to be a 32 bit edition and i couldn't find a 64 bit edition.. Too bad, because i might have kept it for some time if only i could have had sound..)

### Post by NullHead on 2008-03-12
> **Shakey_Jake33 said:**
> So is there any disadvantage in using the OSS driver, rather than waiting for ALSA support, for a mere normal user (not developer) like me?  I'm using my onboard (Realtek HD Audio), but I'm quite keen to use my X-Fi.  My onboard is quite fussy, and any attempt to mess with the sound (for example, installing the newest ALSA) seems to wipe out sound completely and make me reinstall.

Well OSS never worked very well with ubuntu (from what I've tested) it doesn't seem to flow very well .... for example when you install the OSS driver there will not be any startup sounds that you would traditionally hear on startup... another example: I use a music player called BMPx ... well there is a setting to change your sound device... so I try to set it to OSS and all it does is crash ... there is full arrays of programs that I've found to do just what I described earlier ... 

So if you want a quick sort of working sound in programs like audacious and xmms than OSS is for you, but if you want full sound support than go with the Alsa/creative/method 2 driver. 

Well that's my opinion. **please feel free to do what you want!!**

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-03-23
Well after a lot of pain I finally have sound in Ubuntu using my X-Fi fatality card. I didn't manage to get Creatives own Beta drivers installed at all no matter how hard I tried using Ubuntu 7.04, 7.10 or 8.04 however I have finally got sound using Ubuntu 8.04 (64 bit) beta and the latest OSS drivers.

For those of you interested the procedure I used was as follows and was arrived at after reading this thread and many many others along the way..

'Get the build essential and related files (OSS wont install properly without them)..
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-generic linux-source build-essential

'Get the latest OSS4 package..
wget [http://www.4front-tech.com/release/oss-linux_v4.0-1014_amd64.deb](http://www.4front-tech.com/release/oss-linux_v4.0-1014_amd64.deb)
'DO NOT INSTALL THE PACKAGE YET!

'Create  blacklist entries for current ALSA drivers..
lsmod | grep snd

'This will provide a list of modules starting with "snd" which we need to blacklist to prevent them loading..

'Open another terminal..
sudo -s

'Now add the blacklist entries for the Alsa drivers:
nano /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

'Scroll to the way end of the file and add the list of snd* drivers
'Should look something like this..
# blacklist alsa
blacklist snd_intel8x0
blacklist snd_ac97_codec
blacklist snd_pcm
blacklist snd_timer
blacklist snd

'Save the file and reboot..
sudo reboot

'Install the new OSS4 drivers..
sudo dpkg -i oss-linux_v4.0-1014_amd64.deb

'Thats it - if you have reached this point with no errors reboot and you should have OSS sound within Ubuntu 8.04.

I should add I'm a total Linux and Ubuntu noob with a primarily MS Windows background so if any of the above is obvious to you all then I appologise!

I should probably also add that Ubuntu 8.04 rocks, it's very fast and although deep down I want to hate all things Linux I have to confess that I am slowly falling in love with it :)

### Post by NullHead on 2008-03-24
> **cdean said:**
> Here's another HOWTO resource so that folks can cross-reference. It seems that CC doesn't have to forced to gcc-3.3. In fact, that throws errors for me.

This may have been posted already, but it'll take longer than I have today to search this entire thread for it.

[http://blackbox.lostwave.net/x-fi/readme.txt](http://blackbox.lostwave.net/x-fi/readme.txt)

That will work for 7.04 but for gutsy the recompilation of the kernel is required. As for CC=gcc-3.3 well it works better with wolfc's patch. 

The instructions were originally designed for gentoo and the write of the patch is a gentoo user. It does work on gentoo but I feel wolfc's patch works better and actually it seems to improve sound quality, but if you were able to get your sound working than I'm very glad for you :D

As any of my future sound cards I'll EVER buy non of them will ever be a creative card again, but they will be a  Auzentech card [http://www.auzentech.com/site/index.php](http://www.auzentech.com/site/index.php) They seem to be attempting to create a driver for linux, but it seems there won't be a new driver untill Q4 of 08 for these card makers.

### Post by cdean on 2008-03-24
OK, I've complete all of the steps and come to this point.

I recompiled the kernel with SLUB instead of SLAB. There should probably be more intricate directions on how to do this pulled from another source. It is wise not only to recompile linux-image and linux-headers, but linux-restricted-modules, as well. Fortunately, I use only the nVidia driver and envy can take care of that.

I have no errors in dmesg. In fact, the only thing out of the ordinary in dmesg is this:```
[   55.175166] emupia: no version for "InterlockedIncrement" found: kernel tainted.
[   57.961859] ACPI: PCI Interrupt 0000:06:06.0[A] -> GSI 21 (level, low) -> IRQ 21
```
I know my regular dmesg output pretty well from debugging something else, and that's all that i could find that is different.

Whenever I play sound, the first perhaps millisecond continually repeats until the playing program is *killed*--I cannot exit the playing program normally. It still takes a few seconds for the stuttering to stop.

However, the card shows up in alsa and in Ubuntu's volume control.

The only thing which I fancy could be conflicting is PulseAudio. I installed it in an effort to stream sound from this computer to another whilst I messed with the Xfi driver. I had also tried the OSS package from a few posts above this one, but it did not work acceptably -- sound in too few applications if any at all (I couldn't find one that worked). PulseAudio can no longer connect to its server, and I can't find anything in dmesg or /var/log that would lead me to believe why. 

I just removed PulseAudio, and the stuttering/looping continues to occur. Any ideas, folks?

I *may* try installing Hardy on another partition and see what it can do. This installation of Gutsy is only a few weeks old, but I tend to install things rapidly and rarely keep track of what is installed.

Interestingly enough, I just tried to use alsamixer at the command line and got this error message, with and without sudo:```
ALSA lib control.c:874:(snd_ctl_open_conf) Cannot open shared library /usr/lib/alsa-lib/libasound_module_ctl_pulse.so

alsamixer: function snd_ctl_open failed for default: No such file or directory
```

Hmm.

Which is the proper patch? **x-fi-0.2.patch**, which is listed in the HOWTO, or **XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04_all-in-one_v0.2.patch**, which is listed in the other HOWTO I posted above? They are certainly different, but appear the bear a similar version number--it could simply be a coincidence, though. After the applying the former, the driver doesn't even finish compiling for me, whereas after the latter, it does. The former spits this as the error of choice:
```
LinuxSys.c:1715: error: SSE register return with SSE disabled
```

Is getting only VLC and XMMS working is all the farther you've gotten?

Oh my goodness you've gotten it all mixed up :lolflag:

The first 10-20 pages of this thread are mostly for 7.04 and are talking about 7.04. With the current guide it uses x-fi-0.2.patch witch is the one Wolfc wrote. The path XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.04_all-in-one_v0.2.patch is one that someone called Demic wrote mainly for Gentoo and it doesn't work that well for Ubuntu. Where as wolfc's patch was written just for ubuntu and it works quite well! 

My instructions, method 2, is written for gutsy gibbon. They are taken as-is and are designed to be used as they are with no exceptions, like a different kernel version. Lots of people are having troubles with compiling the driver and I simply don't know why, but the instructions I provided is, simply put, the steps that worked for me. As you can see from the pole there is a mixed outcome from the guide and there is some success and some failure, but don't let this get you down :D it's always worth a try.

I hope I cleared it all up for you cdean.

EDIT: Oh by the way I'm using the OSS driver with 8.04 and it's working wonderfully with the applications that work ... most don't work, but the ones that do work, BMPx, rhythmbox and pidgin (witch doesn't work very well but it works) do seem to preform well (except pidgin).

Method 2 seams to work very well and I even play games in wine and they sound just fine and run just fine (for a game in wine anyways ;)) no jitters or anything. If you get it working with method 2 you're really doing very good as fare as working sound goes.

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-03-25
> **NullHead said:**
> EDIT: Oh by the way I'm using the OSS driver with 8.04 and it's working wonderfully with the applications that work ... most don't work, but the ones that do work, BMPx, rhythmbox and pidgin (witch doesn't work very well but it works) do seem to preform well (except pidgin).

Nullhead, I use the latest OSS drivers and have sound in pretty much everything including system sounds (startup and shutdown, VLC, MythTV, and whatever the default media player is.

I did a few additional steps which may have helped such as replacing libesd-alsa0 with libesd0 (dont try removing libesd-alsa0 just do an apt-get on libesd0 instead because this will remove the conflicting libesd-alsa0 package. If you try and manually remove libesd-alsa0 rather than replacing it you could end up in a total mess apparently. 

For system sounds I believe they started working after I installed the esound package.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-03-27
> **AndyJS said:**
> Nullhead, I use the latest OSS drivers and have sound in pretty much everything including system sounds (startup and shutdown, VLC, MythTV, and whatever the default media player is.

I did a few additional steps which may have helped such as replacing libesd-alsa0 with libesd0 (dont try removing libesd-alsa0 just do an apt-get on libesd0 instead because this will remove the conflicting libesd-alsa0 package. If you try and manually remove libesd-alsa0 rather than replacing it you could end up in a total mess apparently. 

For system sounds I believe they started working after I installed the esound package.

Thank you for that useful information! It had the exact same effect you're describing. I'll add those two packages to the guide. 

dirtyern, You should install the these two packages (given earlier by AndyJS): ```
sudo apt-get install build-essential libesd0 esound
``` Then uninstall the OSS driver and re-install it again and it should work.

### Post by mcescher on 2008-03-29
A Linux noob here...but I did eventually get sound working by mostly following Method 1. I did have to slightly alter the code. I am running a 32bit system with X-Fi sound card.

I grabbed the OSS software RPM package [**[COLOR="Orange"]here[/COLOR]**]("http://www.opensound.com/download.cgi")
I then installed a program called *alien* from the Synaptic Package Manager
alien is used to install RPM files using the following code
```
sudo alien -i --scripts /home/username/Desktop/filename.rpm
```

Rebooted and I had sound. I was ready to uninstall Ubuntu due to the lacking support for my sound card...but this way worked like a charm for me...hope this helps someone else.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-04-03
> **Shakey_Jake33 said:**
> Curious, how are the drivers in terms of sound quality?  I'm so tempted to install it, but last time I tried to upgrade OSS drivers, I couldn't get my HD Audio working again!

If you're looking for HD audio STAY AWAY from these drivers. They're simply provide basic sound support. There is no surround support, there is no crystalizer control. I have not tested midi, but someone, posted earlier in the thread, said it didn't work. 

If you have onbpard HD audio you're better off using that instead of your x-fi. 

When alsa gets support I'll be updating the guide to support it.

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-04-05
Ok here are a couple of handy tweaks if you have used method 1 and chosen to use the OSS packages for 64bit Ubuntu 7.10 or 8.04..

**To use ossxmix as the menu volume control..**
Right click on Applications and select Edit Menu. Now Select Sound and Video and locate the entry for the Volume Control. Right click on the Volume Control and select properties. Now edit the command line to read '/usr/bin/ossxmix' (without the quotes) and click done. You should now be able to launch ossxmix by selecting the volume control from the applications, sound and video menu. Note that the volume applet at the top right of the screen will launch ossxmix when double clicked but will report an error when single clicked - we will fix this next..

**To get the Volume Applet working..**
Download the following file to obtain a patched version of the libgstossaudio.so file, unpack it and follow the instructions in the readme..

[http://ubuntuforums.org/attachment.php?attachmentid=55770&d=1199848927]("http://ubuntuforums.org/attachment.php?attachmentid=55770&d=1199848927")

You may need to remove the volume applet from the panel, reboot and then add it back to the panel for it to work.

Hope this info is of some use to you :)

---

### Post by loldawson on 2008-04-05
Found a little work around for Flash videos using wine. 

1. Install wine, set audio to OSS drivers (If it reports an error restart)
2. Install flash for xp
3. Install .exe windows firefox
4. youtube.com :)

### Post by NullHead on 2008-04-07
About flash I've just as of now found a way to watch flash items. You need to install gnash flash player instead of flashplugin-nonfree. ```
sudo apt-get install gnash mozilla-plugin-gnash
```make sure flash is uninstalled before you do this. Then edit /etc/firefox-3.0/firefoxrc```
gksudo gedit /etc/firefox-3.0/firefoxrc
``` and change the line> FIREFOX_DSP="none"to this > FIREFOX_DSP="/dev/oss/sbxfi0/pcm0" and now I can watch videos with my x-fi :D 

NOTE: gnash is in beta stage and crashed firefox allot for me. Also the sound is scratchy and the quality isn't as good, but never the less it IS working. You should only have one tab open when watching flash because I've found the more tabs you have open the easier it'll crash. 

Have fun, NullHead

          ( test -f debian/official && test -f debian/control) ||          \
           sed -e 's/=V/2.6.22.9-custom/g'         -e 's/=D/2.6.22.9-custom-10.00.Custom/g'        \
               -e 's/=A/amd64/g'   -e 's/=SA//g'  \
                -e 's/=L/lilo (>= 19.1) | grub, /g' -e 's/=I/initramfs-tools (>= 0.53) | yaird (>= 0.0.11) | linux-initramfs-tool, /g'     \
                -e 's/=CV/2.6/g'                      \
                -e 's/=M/Unknown Kernel Package Maintainer <unknown@unconfigured.in.etc.kernel-pkg.conf>/g'                       \
                -e 's/=ST/linux/g'      -e 's/=B/x86_64/g'   \
                         /usr/share/kernel-package/Control> debian/control
test -f stamp-debian  ||    test -f debian/official ||                \
           sed -e 's/=V/2.6.22.9-custom/g' -e 's/=D/2.6.22.9-custom-10.00.Custom/g'                   \
            -e 's/=A/amd64/g' -e 's/=M/Unknown Kernel Package Maintainer <unknown@unconfigured.in.etc.kernel-pkg.conf>/g' \
            -e 's/=ST/linux/g'     -e 's/=B/x86_64/g'           \
                /usr/share/kernel-package/changelog > debian/changelog
test  -f debian/rules || install -p -m 755 /usr/share/kernel-package/rules debian/rules
test  -f stamp-debian || test -f debian/official ||                     \
           for file in ChangeLog  Control  Control.bin86 config templates.in rules; do                                      \
               cp -f  /usr/share/kernel-package/$file ./debian/;                               \
           done
test  -f stamp-debian || test -f debian/official ||                     \
           for dir  in Config docs examples ruleset scripts pkg po;  do                                      \
             cp -af /usr/share/kernel-package/$dir  ./debian/;                                 \
           done
echo done >  stamp-debian
echo done >  debian/stamp-conf

### Post by AndyParka on 2008-04-10
Hi, I'm pretty new to linux and have spent all week on learning how to use it. I'm pretty sure what i have to do to my ubuntu to get my x-fi xtremegamer running but i got an error while following the how to for the kernel for Gutsy 64 bit users like me. i get to the last line i have to type in to the console.

```
make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

and when i enter this to console i get this (errors are shown at the very bottom)

If you're using the OSS driver it will work on both amd64/x86_64 and regular x86/32bit. I recommend OSS over the other way now. It all is working so well I'm just very pleased with it now.

sudo apt-get purge oss-linux
``` and then try to install it again.

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-04-11
> **CarollaTony said:**
> Well, neither method works for me.

I noticed from your previous posts that you initially tried installing the 32bit package and then in your follow up post you were installing the 64 bit package so I would imagine now that you have a very corrupted package installed.

Try following the advice here [http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054]("http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054") to remove a corrupted OSS Install. And be sure to install the correct package at the end - either the 64bit assuming you have a 64bit version of Ubuntu or the 32bit package if you are running 32bit Ubuntu.

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-04-14
> **mhenriday said:**
> If I open the extracted «gstreamer-ossv4» directory, I see the items shown in the screenshot below, but if I attempt to open «libgstossaudio.so», I'm informed that no program is installed for this type of file. I seem to have missed something vital along the way and would be most grateful for any suggestions as to what it might be....

Henri

Open a terminal and cd to the gstreamer-ossv4 directory and then try the following as 2 seperate commands..

**sudo gzip /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/libgstossaudio.so**

followed by..

**sudo mv libgstossaudio.so /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/**

If that is successful then you can remove the applet from the panel, reboot and then add it back to the panel to see if it now works.

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-04-15
> **mhenriday said:**
> **AndyJS**, after cd-ing to the extracted directory, I tried ```
sudo gzip /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/libgstossaudio.so
``` but got an error message. I then tried **NullHead**'s suggestion and entered ```
sudo gunzip /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/libgstossaudio.so
``` instead, which was accepted. After removing the applet from the pane and rebooting, I re-added it to the pane. I noticed that the icon had changed - from a loudspeaker to a round volume dial (with a tiny red square at about 4 o'clock). In any event, while I can open a drop-down menu and confirm that the «Mute» box is not ticked, I can't open the volume control ; when I attempt to do so, it doesn't load. Back to the drawing board !...

Henri

The error you received when performing the gzip was probably due to the file already existing from a previous attempt.

Let me explain what we are actually trying to do here, we are trying to achieve 2 things..
1) by performing the gzip line we are making a compressed backup copy of the original file. Now in your case this is failing and most likely because you have already done it - This step is not critical and you can safely ignore it.
2) We need to replace the current version of the file with the patched copy in the extract directory. This is why we run the following command..

```
sudo mv libgstossaudio.so /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/
``` from within the gstreamer-ossv4 directory.

If you don't run that command you will not have replaced the file with the patched one and it definitely won't work.

Step 1 simply makes a compressed backup copy of the original file - you only need to gunzip the file again if you wish to undo the changes. There was no need for you to run gunzip at all :confused:

### Post by NullHead on 2008-04-15
So you have a compressed backup the the file, as Andy suggested, and you now, no thansk to me, have another copy of the default, un-patched, library installed into /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/. Lets remove the default library and then follow Andy's instructions again. ```
sudo mv /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/libgstossaudio.so ~/.Trash

```

Now you'll notice that the file appeared in your trash bin. You can do what you like with it because Andy had you make a backup :)

So now follow sudo mv libgstossaudio.so /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/ again and make sure you're in the directory with the patched library. 

Sorry for the confusion.

---

> **NullHead said:**
> So now follow sudo mv libgstossaudio.so /usr/lib/gstreamer-0.10/ again and make sure you're in the directory with the patched library. 

Sorry for the confusion.

It may be necessary to download und unpack the patch again if the patched file can't be found - it should be in the extract directory but could be anywhere by now lol.


mhenriday - Do you have OSS working at all?  because none of this will actually fix it if it's not currently working, all this patch will do is allow the volume applet to work correctly and control a working OSS mixer volume.

### Post by GeekGirl1 on 2008-04-16
Creative Labs has posted an update to the X-Fi driver. Just downloaded it, will start testing. 

Here's the info:

 Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi Series Linux 32-bit / 64-bit Beta Driver  (14.88 MB)  	 16 Apr 08
Filename: XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18.tar.gz

This download is a beta driver providing Linux® 32-bit / 64-bit OS support for Creative Sound Blaster® X-Fi&#8482; series audio devices. For more details, read the rest of this web release note.

Take note of the following:

    * THIS IS AN UNSUPPORTED BETA DRIVER. There is no technical support for this driver.
    * This beta driver has only been tested on the following 32-bit / 64-bit Linux distributions: Ubuntu® 7.10, openSUSE® 10.3 and Red Hat® Enterprise Linux® 5.1 (64-bit only).
    * We recommend that only experienced users install this driver. Do not install this driver on a system used to perform critical tasks.
    * Your feedback is a valuable part of our development process. To submit feedback on this driver, visit the Sound Blaster X-Fi Linux Beta Driver Feedback Program.

This download is intended for the following audio devices only:

    * Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi Elite Pro 
    * Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi Platinum
    * Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi Fatal1ty®
    * Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi XtremeGamer
    * Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi XtremeMusic

Current release features:

    * ALSA PCM Playback
    * ALSA Record
    * ALSA Mixer

Added Features or Enhancements:

    * Supports GCC version 4
    * Supports Linux 64-bit and 32-bit OS

Known issues:

    * S/PDIF passthrough is not supported in this driver release.
    * External I/O modules are not supported in this driver release.
    * Applications from the original Sound Blaster X-Fi Installation CD will not work with this driver.

Requirements:

    * Linux x86 / x86_64 OS
    * Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi audio devices listed above

Notes:

    * To install the driver, do the following:
         1. Download the XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18.tar.gz package onto your local hard disk.
         2. Double-click the downloaded package to unpack its contents.
         3. Read the README file and follow the instructions.

### Post by GeekGirl1 on 2008-04-18
> **AndyJS said:**
> Useless to me - They don't work..
```
configure: creating ./config.status
config.status: creating Makefile.conf
make: *** [all] Error 2
make: *** [install] Error 2
Installation Unsuccessful

```I have the same error. Hardy 8.04. However, I'll keep working it. The script creates a directory called "drivers" that can be run manually. It's failing in there. In progress....

I was getting an error "Bash: Bad interpreter: Permission denied". Permissions were set correctly. After further research, I found that the partition I was running the install from was set to "noexec" in /etc/fstab.

My /etc/fstab with the problem and the fix on the 2nd line. I removed "noexec".
```
#/dev/sdb5 /media/disk ext3 nouser,atime,auto,rw,nodev,noexec,nosuid 0 0
/dev/sdb5 /media/disk ext3 nouser,atime,auto,rw,nodev,nosuid 0
```

One comment:
The Creative Labs release notes state that the I/O modules are not supported. I assume there will be no output from the headphone jack. :(

Runnig local boot script (/etc/rc.local)            [OK]

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-04-18
> **mikko28 said:**
> I just purchased new Creative Sound Blaster X-Fi Xtreme Audio PCI Express sound card and now I have problems. It works fine with Windows Xp but Ubuntu doesn't even boot with the card. Starting up Ubuntu stops after the following line:
Runnig local boot script (/etc/rc.local)            [OK]
and then nothing happends.

I used intergrated sound card before. Ubuntu works if I take the card out. How can I get Ubuntu booted with the card? Where is the problem?

If I had to guess I would say that it's an IRQ/Resource assignment issue - if you are savy enough to mess in the bios you can try resetting the PCI/IRQ assignment info and make sure PNP OS is set to disabled or failing that try a fresh install now that the card is already installed.

Best option unless you have a lot of user data that you want to keep is to try a fresh install - its so fast with ubuntu that I tend to do it a lot :neutral:

### Post by GeekGirl1 on 2008-04-18
Not working yet, but I think I see some problems. I am debugging the extracted scripts.

Files of interest (not including the other make files, etc.):
(extracted directory)/installer
drivers/configure
drivers/config.status
drivers/config.log
drivers/makefile.conf

A first possible problem: I am using gedit (Gnome editor) to view all the scripts. All of the code near the bottom of config.status was keyword colored 'pink', an indication that something is broken. As config.status is generated from drivers/configure, I found where this code was generated. 

In drivers/configure, there is a nice 'happy face' on line 9480 which I think was interpreted as an extra closing ')'.
```
 # First look for the input files in the build tree, otherwise in the
  # src tree.
  ac_file_inputs=`IFS=:
    for f in $ac_file_in; do
      case $f in
      -) echo $tmp/stdin ;;
      [\\/$]*)
	 # Absolute (can't be DOS-style, as IFS=:)
	 test -f "$f" || { { echo "$as_me:$LINENO: error: cannot find input file: $f" >&5
echo "$as_me: error: cannot find input file: $f" >&2;}
```I removed the phrase '(can't be DOS-style, as IFS=: )' from line 9480 (so only the # Absolute remains) and config.status looked in much better form. drivers/configure now completes with no error.

On to 'make': Running with default kernel is a good clue. I am using kernel 2.6.24-16-generic. I think there is a hard-coded limit to the kernel (?). Makefile.conf:
```
kbuild			= $(shell if [ $(kvercode) -ge 132626 ]; then echo "y"; fi)
```The content of /lib/modules/2.6.24-16-generic/build/include/linux/Version.h:
```
#define LINUX_VERSION_CODE 132632
``` I think that (132632 >= 132626) would cause an error (?). I tried modifying Makefile.conf, but it still doesn't work- make: *** [all] Error 2.

One other thing I did was to chmod drivers/install-sh from 644 to 744 to show execute privilege. I don't think it will help, but I have it set that way now.

After more research, the build is failing in drivers/src/utils/alsaver. (Successful compiled modules are copied to /tmp/xfisrc). The log file: /var/log/creative-installer.log shows errors occuring here:```
make[2]: Entering directory `/media/disk/Ubuntu/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/src/utils/alsaver'
gcc -Wall -O  alsaver.c -o alsaver
make[2]: Leaving directory `/media/disk/Ubuntu/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/src/utils/alsaver'
cp -f /tmp/xfisrc/src/utils/alsaver/alsaver .
cd /tmp/xfisrc/src/ossrv; make
(all is OK to here)

make[2]: Entering directory `/media/disk/Ubuntu/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/src/ossrv'
(a very long gcc parameter arg here)
LinuxSys.c: In function &#8216;sysRegisterInterrupt&#8217;:
LinuxSys.c:643: warning: cast from pointer to integer of different size
LinuxSys.c:648: error: &#8216;SA_SHIRQ&#8217; undeclared (first use in this function)
LinuxSys.c:648: error: (Each undeclared identifier is reported only once
LinuxSys.c:648: error: for each function it appears in.)
LinuxSys.c: In function &#8216;sysGetPag
(a lot more errors)
make[2]: *** [LinuxSys.o] Error 1
make[2]: Leaving directory `/media/disk/Ubuntu/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/src/ossrv'
make[1]: *** [ctossrv] Error 2
```Inspection of drivers/src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c shows compiler directives for 2.6.x. There are definite differences between 2.6 and earlier versions.
```
#if LINUX_VERSION_CODE < KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
    struct tq_struct SchedulerQueue;
#else
    struct work_struct SchedulerQueue;
#endif
...
#if LINUX_VERSION_CODE < KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
        schedule_task(&phTimerCbk->SchedulerQueue);
#else
        schedule_work(&phTimerCbk->SchedulerQueue);
#endif
```There are also many references to kernel version 2.6 in ./configure.

The source code suggests that it is a problem with the kernel version, not Ubuntu. Has anyone compiled successfully with kernel 2.6 (is this a problem only for me or everyone)? If not, is there a way to patch around the problem? I have no experience with the kernel architecture, so I am stopped.

### Post by effaceur666 on 2008-04-19
```
LinuxSys.c:648: error: &#8216;SA_SHIRQ&#8217; undeclared (first use in this function)
```

this error is due to use of a kernel version greater than 2.6.24, this variable doen not exist in include/linux/interrupt.h
so you have to replace SA_SHIRQ by IRQF_SHARED in opt/Creative/...../src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c and all should well compile

Restart compilation with the ./installer script and delete line doing a "tar xzvf" of the archive otherwise you'll delete your previous correction...

Restart the system and the driver will be installed :)

---

### Post by effaceur666 on 2008-04-19
```
make -C /lib/modules/2.6.24-16-generic/build SUBDIRS=/tmp/xfisrc/src/ossrv modules
make[3]: Entering directory `/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.24-16-generic'
scripts/Makefile.build:46: *** CFLAGS was changed in "/tmp/xfisrc/src/ossrv/Makefile". Fix it to use EXTRA_CFLAGS.  Stop.
make[3]: *** [_module_/tmp/xfisrc/src/ossrv] Error 2
make[3]: Leaving directory `/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.24-16-generic'
make[2]: *** [module] Error 2
make[2]: Leaving directory `/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/src/ossrv'
make[1]: *** [ctossrv] Error 2
make[1]: Leaving directory `/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers'
```

the easiest way to fix it would be to comment the lines testing these CFLAGS in the usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.24-16-generic/scripts/Makefile.build:46

```
#ifeq ($(KBUILD_NOPEDANTIC),)
        #ifneq ("$(save-cflags)","$(CFLAGS)")
                #$(error CFLAGS was changed in "$(kbuild-file)". Fix it to use EXTRA_CFLAGS)
        #endif
#endif

```

 because it is anyway loading the CFLAGS from creative, this is a test for backward compatibility.

The problem is you may get errors if you do that... but try it :-P

### Post by effaceur666 on 2008-04-19
I think it's a bad idea to have both OSS and ALSA installed because of conflicts in your environment.

For me, this driver is working fine and is well loaded, the only thing I did after rebooting was to get the good right access because by default, the /dev/snd created by the installation are only accessible for root. So you have to be in the audio group or in the root group (that's the most efficient way) to have sound.

### Post by GeekGirl1 on 2008-04-19
More information for you. Since I didn't know what the problem was, I was fixing all errors as they occurred. To get to the point of the LinuxSys.c problem, I first worked through a problem with /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/configure. 

./configure creates ./config.status which then executes to continue the build process.

I can manage shell scripts, but I am not an expert.  Examination of the generated config.status script showed keywords colored as comments from this section of ./configure:```
     # Absolute (can't be DOS-style, as IFS=: )
	 test -f "$f" || { { echo "$as_me:$LINENO: error: cannot find input file: $f" >&5
echo "$as_me: error: cannot find input file: $f" >&2;}
...
cat >>$CONFIG_STATUS <<_ACEOF
  sed "$ac_vpsub
$extrasub
_ACEOF
cat >>$CONFIG_STATUS <<\_ACEOF
...
s,@INSTALL@,$ac_INSTALL,;t t
" $ac_file_inputs | (eval "$ac_sed_cmds") >$tmp/out
```I tried to fix  the following:
[INDENT]Line 9480: Remove "(can't be DOS-style, as IFS=: )" - a possible side-effect of the "happy face" closing parenthesis
Line 9502:  sed "$ac_vpsub, I think it's missing a closing ' " ', but when I tried to fix it, the script was broken
Line 9518: Delete the ' " ' in front of $ac_file_inputs
[/INDENT]
After getting the keywords colored correctly (?) in config.status, the script ran to create the makefile.conf but then it stops completely and does not exit. I have no idea how to fix it. Maybe the code has always been like that. If I leave the original code in place, the script runs.

This is very good "live training" for me. I get more "live training" to recover my crashed OSS / ALSA mixup. Interesting that I have no problem to boot in a previous kernel.

### Post by Organon on 2008-04-20
> **NullHead said:**
> [FONT="Tahoma"][SIZE="5"][CENTER]***ALSA***[/CENTER][/SIZE]

It's so very simple it's almost confusing. ... here it goes: 
Install dependencies: ```
sudo apt-get install build-essential
``` 
Download the driver to your desktop: [http://us.creative.com/support/downloads/](http://us.creative.com/support/downloads/) 

Move into the directory```
tar -xf ~/Desktop/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18.tar.gz && cd ~/Desktop/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18
``` and run ```
sudo ./installer
```
You don't even need to restart the computer! 

Please note that there has been error reports revealing no sound at boot-up. 
thats it! Enjoy :D

[SIZE="5"][CENTER]***OSS***[/CENTER][/SIZE]

Install these dependencies: ```
sudo apt-get install build-essential libesd0 esound oss-compat
```Then download the latest OSS driver from [here]("http://www.opensound.com/download.cgi") choose the .deb according to your architecture and install it. Restart your computer and sound should be working for either an i386 or an AMD64 machine.

If OSS fails to install or update use this guild to help you uninstall it: [http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054](http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054)[/FONT]

First, allow me to admit that I did not read the entire thread.

Second, this should work with the latest Hardy 8.04 LTS RC, yes?

### Post by GeekGirl1 on 2008-04-20
No, stay with Hardy. Production release is soon... Use the OSS drivers instead. They work fine. All you need to do is install as suggested in Post #1.

The headphone jack does not work, but the sound is good quality. The Creative Labs driver won't support the headphones either, so there is no benefit except that it is a proprietary driver.

### Post by effaceur666 on 2008-04-20
ok, I've a fully working driver now :D

that's incredible. So I think the first thing to do before installing this driver is not to correct configure scripts or other things but to to be in the audio or root group (this is because by default this f***** driver is installed for root and doesn't care of any users) : 

1 that's the first thing to do. You can do this in the user management menu.

2, comment-out the lines in you source/script/Makefile.build as I've previously posted.

3, if you're using a 2.6.24 kernel, replace the SA_SHAIRQ br IRQF_SHARED in the src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c. I think the best to do is to untar the original archive, modify it, and do a new bzip2 compressed archive.

4 run again the ./installer, and all should well work, reboot and enjoy the working sound with gstreamer, totem, flash, and so on :razz:

---

> **GeekGirl1 said:**
> No, stay with Hardy. Production release is soon... Use the OSS drivers instead. They work fine. All you need to do is install as suggested in Post #1.

The headphone jack does not work, but the sound is good quality. The Creative Labs driver won't support the headphones either, so there is no benefit except that it is a proprietary driver.

1) Well, I stay with XP till there would be 5.1 support :) Either in OSS drivers, or Creative drivers. which will have it first I will use them. I won't install Hardy on main computer just because it would be final. Look at KDE4, final release was almost unusable, now I guess 4.0.3 is good (I don't use it). 
Gutsy is good. But I might reinstall it but with 32bit. 64bit sucks :)

2) Well, I have Logitech X-540 with [this wired remote]("http://www.laaudiofile.com/images/x540c.jpg"). It has jack for headphones, so I just don't care :)

### Post by kj74 on 2008-04-20
> **effaceur666 said:**
> ok, I've a fully working driver now :D

that's incredible. So I think the first thing to do before installing this driver is not to correct configure scripts or other things but to to be in the audio or root group (this is because by default this f***** driver is installed for root and doesn't care of any users) : 

1 that's the first thing to do. You can do this in the user management menu.

2, comment-out the lines in you source/script/Makefile.build as I've previously posted.

3, if you're using a 2.6.24 kernel, replace the SA_SHAIRQ br IRQF_SHARED in the src/ossrv/LinuxSys.c. I think the best to do is to untar the original archive, modify it, and do a new bzip2 compressed archive.

4 run again the ./installer, and all should well work, reboot and enjoy the working sound with gstreamer, totem, flash, and so on :razz:

All users should already be members of audio group. These drivers do compile and (kind of) load but they don't work. Can't hear a note. Also i can't use Nvidia binary drivers and these together. 
Might be this [http://forums.creative.com/creativelabs/board/message?board.id=soundblaster&thread.id=31220&view=by_date_ascending&page=114](http://forums.creative.com/creativelabs/board/message?board.id=soundblaster&thread.id=31220&view=by_date_ascending&page=114) issue. They don't segfault for me but it takes about 10 seconds to get past modprobe and xserver doesn't work if i try to use binary drivers.

I think people will just mess up their audio if they try to use these "beta" drivers. Oss ones are better. You even get flash audio if you have libssl-dev package installed.

---

### Post by GeekGirl1 on 2008-04-20
I followed effaceur666's instructions except for the user group management. I ran everything as root and I had a good install. The driver loads during boot (I can hear a 'click' sound) which is followed by a complete freeze of my PC when the X-server is loaded. So, I now have a recovery process.

1a. Boot using the "recover" version of your kernel- it should be the 2nd entry in your boot loader. Select the menu choice "root - Drop to root shell prompt".
[indent]cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/
make uninstall
reboot[/indent]
 -or-

1b. If you have an earlier kernel version in your boot loader that has not been compiled with this driver. Boot with this version. The sound drivers will fail. dmesg will report many error messages starting with "snd". That is good because you can continue to work.
- Go to the /drivers directory as root and uninstall the drivers. Start a terminal window.
[INDENT]su
cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/
./configure
make uninstall
[/INDENT]- Reboot with your latest kernel.

The log file ( /var/log/creative-installer.log ) will contain the unstall process details.

---

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-04-20
To be honest I don't know why we are bothering waiting for Creative to develop anything workable anymore :???:

Creative do not offer support for these drivers as they stand, and bearing in mind most of us can't even install them without having to patch or edit them what hope of support do we really have?
It also appears that even if you do manage to sucessfully install these drivers you get worse compatability than the current OSS drivers.

The X-Fi is now what 3 years old at least and in those 3 years all Creative have managed for Linux are 2 releases of botched drivers that only support basic stereo output. Let's face facts here - Creative are never going to supply fully functional drivers for this card.

The only conclussion that I can arrive at is that Creative have only now decided to make any effort at all as a PR stunt due to the bad press they have received lately and the fact that they do not wish to lose face to the likes of the OSS developers. We the consumers and owners of the mighty X-Fi are being mocked by Creative - they clearly have no interest in providing us with any drivers that work - I mean come on they designed the card over 3 years ago, they have all the specs for the card, have developed functional drivers for Windows both 32 and 64bit and the best they can do for Linux after all this time is a bodged set of unsupported drivers with only very basic stereo audio output support :confused:

Most of us probably have perfectly adequate and well supported onboard audio that will not only work under Linux but will also provide full surround sound and working audio inputs - perhaps it's time to throw in the towel and use hardware from vendors that either support their own products or support the 3rd party developers that do. 

:evil:

</EndRant>

We the consumers and owners of the mighty X-Fi are being mocked by Creative - they clearly have no interest in providing us with any drivers that work - I mean come on they designed the card over 3 years ago, they have all the specs for the card, have developed functional drivers for Windows both 32 and 64bit and the best they can do for Linux after all this time is a bodged set of unsupported drivers with only very basic stereo audio output support :confused:

### Post by GeekGirl1 on 2008-04-20
OK, I have chased this to the limits of my PC. My system freezes after the modprobe and when the X-server starts. I have updated my post on how to uninstall the Creatve Labs driver, [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4752058&postcount=552](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4752058&postcount=552). There is no problem to uninstall.

My system is broken with this driver and I will revert back to the OSS drivers until I hear news of an update. I am using the proprietary NVidia drivers and have no Linux alternative (generic drivers do not work). Here is a summary of the changes:

Following the thread on Creative's site, [http://forums.creative.com/creativelabs/board/message?board.id=soundblaster&thread.id=31220&view=by_date_ascending&page=113](http://forums.creative.com/creativelabs/board/message?board.id=soundblaster&thread.id=31220&view=by_date_ascending&page=113), I added the missing include files and corrected the IRQ parameter to /drivers/ossrv/LinuxSys.c:```
#include <linux/fs.h>
#include <asm/fcntl.h>
...
/* if (request_irq(phInterrupt->uIRQ, interrupt_callback, SA_SHIRQ, "x-fi", phInterrupt)) { */
   if (request_irq(phInterrupt->uIRQ, interrupt_callback, IRQF_SHARED, "x-fi", phInterrupt)) {

```I was expecting a clean compile, but there are warnings:```
LinuxSys.c: In function &#8216;sysRegisterInterrupt&#8217;:
LinuxSys.c:645: warning: cast from pointer to integer of different size
LinuxSys.c: In function &#8216;sysGetPagePhysAddr&#8217;:
LinuxSys.c:963: warning: passing argument 1 of &#8216;kvirt_to_phys&#8217; makes integer from pointer without a cast
LinuxSys.c: In function &#8216;sysGetPageBusAddr&#8217;:
LinuxSys.c:990: warning: passing argument 1 of &#8216;kvirt_to_bus&#8217; makes integer from pointer without a cast
LinuxSys.c: In function &#8216;sysWriteFile&#8217;:
LinuxSys.c:1642: warning: pointer targets in passing argument 2 of &#8216;filp->f_op->write&#8217; differ in signedness
LinuxSys.c: In function &#8216;sysReadFile&#8217;:
LinuxSys.c:1691: warning: pointer targets in passing argument 2 of &#8216;filp->f_op->read&#8217; differ in signedness
```As this code links to the proprietary asm modules, I am very afraid of byte alignment problems. Casts to integers of different size and creating integers from pointers is dangerous. This especially true when the developer needs to be aware of both 32 bit and 64 bit OS's. Is this why everything freezes? I have no idea, but it is a good place to investigate.

For those that have working drivers, does the log file show any warnings during the compile of drivers/ossrv/LinuxSys.c? It would be interesting to compare.

The modification of <kernel>/script/Makefile.build to bypass the CFLAGS check was a test only. There is a better fix for the CFLAGS build process as it does not require you to modify your kernel's source code (it is not a good idea to modify your kernel unless you really, really need to). Also in that same Creative forum thread. Add KBUILD_NOPEDANTIC=1 to the make argument.

```
make KBUILD_NOPEDANTIC=1
make install

```You can modify the ./installer script for this change, but you will need to update LinuxSys.c in the embedded tar file as well or it will get overwritten.

A reminder for everyone to post a bug report directly at Creative Labs: [http://connect.creativelabs.com/linux/Lists/Driver%20Issues/AllItems.aspx](http://connect.creativelabs.com/linux/Lists/Driver%20Issues/AllItems.aspx). The form is easy to fill out. No need to register.

---

### Post by kj74 on 2008-04-20
> I cannot get Flash audio to work with the OSS drivers. Is there a good resource to turn to for a decent OSS setup?

You need to have libssl-dev package installed before compiling(installing) OSS drivers. If you don't have it, libflashsupport compile fails silently.

Anyway, it just makes flash very unstable like libflashsupport for pulseaudio. You can do it afterwards too [http://www.4front-tech.com/wiki/index.php/Configuring_Applications_for_OSSv4](http://www.4front-tech.com/wiki/index.php/Configuring_Applications_for_OSSv4)

There is libflashsupport package in repos but it is for pulseaudio. Make sure you don't have it installed, flashplayer-nonfree does have dependency for that package. So you have to get flashplayer from adobe site instead.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-04-23
OK your soundcard is detected by the system "05:02.0 Multimedia audio controller: Creative Labs SB X-Fi" but there is no proper drivers installed to make it work. 

Did you install the oss driver that goes to your architecture respectively? For example if you have an i386 or PC version of Ubuntu you need the x86 DEB version of OSS; if you have an AMD64 version of Ubuntu you need the amd64 DEB version.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-04-24
Can you try using these commands and try to re-install the OSS driver? 

this will unload your old onboard sound driver:
```
sudo rmmod snd_hda_intel
``` And then this one: 

This will disable the sound driver from loading at the boot time: 
```
echo 'blacklist snd_hda_intel' | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

Reboot and then use the link to the guide on the first page to remove the OSS driver and try to re-install it after.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-04-24
No, you're able to do that because of esound. Esound is a sound system (older version of pulseaudio) that allows OSS to connect. This allows programs to easily connect to this universal sound system and still be independent of driver incompatibilities like OSS. 

(correct me if I'm wrong)

---

As for a fully supported uber sound card there is this one [http://www.razerzone.com/index.php?main_page=product_info&cPath=2_16&products_id=41](http://www.razerzone.com/index.php?main_page=product_info&cPath=2_16&products_id=41) it's fully supported by ALSA and I hear it's supposed to rival the x-fi.

### Post by mhenriday on 2008-04-25
After an earlier, vain attempt to get my **Creative SB X-Fi SB 0670** (**Xtreme Music**) audio card to deliver a little sound (see pp 47-48 above) to my (64-bit) **Hardy beta** setup, I purged the previous downloads, went back to page 1 and followed **NullHead**'s clear and concise instructions with regard to installing the **OSS** dependencies and drivers. I found it necessary to complement the instructions by opening the **Synaptic Package Manager** and installing «flashplugin-nonfree». Re-booting the computer, I found, contrary to the reports regarding no sound at boot-up, that I was now able to hear the signature **Ubuntu** boot-up melody for the first time since I began to use my present computer. Moreover, I can now play CDs from my simple 2.1 speaker setup. BUT, if I wish to hear the sound on, say, an **YouTube** video, I have to re-boot the computer to second of my HDD, where I have both **Windows XP** and **Windows Vista** installed. Any suggestions as to what could be the problem here ? I'd very much like to avoid being forced into **Windows** in order to enjoy on-line videos ! My **Hardy beta** installation is an upgrade from **Gutsy** ; is it conceivable that performing a clean **Hardy** install could resolve the difficulty ?...

Henri

### Post by kj74 on 2008-04-26
It seems they fixed very little between 1.16 and 1.18. Most issues are still not fixed. You need to modprobe modules in right order(default is broken), issues with depmod, issues with SMP, you still might need slab etc. Conflict with Nvidia drivers. You don't need Kernel 2.6.25. I have not tried again, so can't help with installing. It's just stuff i read from Creative and Gentoo forums.

I wouldn't use those broken alien created OSS deb's. Instead use tarballs. Don't install esd again. Check /usr/lib/oss/conf/vmix.conf, docs and [http://www.4front-tech.com/forum/index.php](http://www.4front-tech.com/forum/index.php) for more information. Newer OSS versions does have software mixing, you need to edit confic files.

GStreamer Bad Plug-ins 0.10.7 supports OSS4(it means that volume applet works) but Hardy version is 0.10.6...

Remember to change Sound properties to OSS and do same for applications like Audacious, Mplayer and Vlc etc.

Remove libflashsupport if you have it installed because it is for Pulseaudio and Adobe flashplayer. It's also useless if you use Gnash(gnash if broken in Hardy for me anyway).

If you use 32-bit version of Ubuntu/Kubuntu install libssl-dev package before OSS. That way you get libflashsupport for OSS and flashplayer. It can cause lots of Firefox stability issues but that's another story.

### Post by Jedimaniac on 2008-04-27
I think I have a solution to the default soundcard issue (with oss anyway). [plus u dont have to disable any other sound devices]

Open terminal

run the following command:

```
sudo gedit /usr/lib/oss/etc/installed_drivers
```

In the file that opens move the xfi entry(s) to the top and save the changes.

Restart and enjoy sound :D

I dont know why it works. I stumbled across this fix purely by frustration and guesswork. I can only assume the kernel looks at this file first when determining what drivers to load.

I am still technically a noob on linux so will not be able to support any problems this may cause. Hope this helps.

### Post by amtam on 2008-04-28
Below are the steps I made to get the Creative Alsa-driver working under Ubuntu Hardy 8.04 AMD64. 
It *should* work on 32-bit (i386) machines as well, from both AMD and Intel.

Disclaimer: just because it worked on my machine, doesn't mean it will on yours. YMMV.

Basically, you need to do 2 things:

**1.** Recompile your kernel. It needs some changes, mostly ALSA-related.
If you don't recompile your kernel, the driver will still compile and install, but it will lock up your system or segfault. At least, it did so on my system and on many others.

**Note:** if you never compiled a kernel before, then maybe this howto is not suited for you. You can use [Temüjin's OSS-guide](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2) instead.


**2.** [Download](http://www.xs4all.nl/~tachyon1/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz) this patched Creative-installer to your Home Folder (and install it; see Part 2).
It is patched with several fixes I found in this and other X-Fi threads. But first Part 1.

 

**_Part 1 - Compiling the kernel with the Alsa-modules_**

A little warning upfront: a recompile of the kernel will almost certainly break your restricted Nvidia-driver! **[SIZE="5"]*[/SIZE]**
You can download and install it manually afterwards from [here](http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_amd64_173.14.09.html) (64-bits driver) or [here](http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_ia32_173.14.09.html) (32-bits driver).
Or you can check out the [NVIDIA](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=301499) or [ATI](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=515573) HOW-TO's... 

So here we go. I use the root-account when compiling stuff, so I don't have to sudo all the time. 
If you don't, remember to put sudo in front of all the commands! 

Or just type
```
sudo bash
```
in a terminal so you only have to sudo once.

Be sure to update the system first:

```
apt-get update && apt-get upgrade
```

Install some necessary packages:

```
apt-get install kernel-package libncurses5-dev fakeroot bzip2 build-essential
```

Download the 2.6.24 kernel-sources from the Ubuntu-repository

```
apt-get install linux-source-2.6.24
```

It will download to /usr/src. There, you have to unpack it and make a symlink to it:

```
cd /usr/src
tar jxvf linux-source-2.6.24.tar.bz2
ln -s linux-source-2.6.24 linux
cd /usr/src/linux
```

You are now ready to start compiling.
Use the .config from your working, default kernel as a basis for the new kernel:

```
cp /boot/config-`uname -r` ./.config
```

Or, if you're really feeling lucky, you can download my own customized, lean-and-mean kernelconfig [here](http://www.xs4all.nl/~tachyon1/config-lean.txt) ;-)
Be warned though, I ripped out a lot of stuff. There is only one network driver for instance, the sky2-module for Marvell Yukon.

Then run:

```
make menuconfig
```

Go to Load Alternate Config File and choose .config (in the lower part of the main menu):
[img]http://intranet.etin.nl/images/menuconfig1.gif[/img]

Then change the following (if not already checked):

```
Kernel hacking -> Enable unused/obsolete exported symbols [*]
```
[img]http://intranet.etin.nl/images/menuconfig2.gif[/img]


(thanks to BigMichi1 on the Gentoo-forum)

And make sure ALSA is build as follows:
```
Device Drivers -> Sound -> Sound card support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> Advanced Linux Sound Architecture <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Sequencer support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Sequencer dummy client <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS Mixer API <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS PCM (digital audio) API <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS PCM (digital audio) API - include plugin system [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS Sequencer API [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> RTC Timer Support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Use RTC as default sequencer timer [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Verbose procfs contents [*]

```
Untick "Support old ALSA API".
It should look like this:
[img]http://intranet.etin.nl/images/alsa1.gif[/img]
[img]http://intranet.etin.nl/images/alsa2.gif[/img]

That's all you have to do for this particular HOWTO, but you can make other changes if you want.
I always change stuff like processor-architecture and I remove a LOT of unused stuff (like ISDN, PCMCIA and other hardware I just don't have.
It will shave a LOT from your compiling time... ;-)
But if you don't really know what your doing, leave everything untouched because you can seriously screw up things... ;-)
Exit the main-menu and save the new kernel-configuration (it will ask you to). Then build the kernel with the following two commands:

```
make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

This can take a while, depending on the speed of your system. It takes about 10 minutes on my Core 2 Duo@ 4GHz.

Installing the new kernel.
There are now 2 .deb-packages in /usr/src.

```
cd /usr/src
ls -l
```

They are called something like:

linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb and
linux-headers-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb

**NOTE:** If you have the i386-version of Hardy, the filenames will be slightly different, but you get the idea.
Install them with these commands:

```
dpkg -i linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
dpkg -i linux-headers-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
```

Check /boot/grub/menu.list to see if the new kernel is present.
Reboot the system with 'shutdown -r now' and after that, check with 'uname -r' if the new kernel is active.
If yes, you can proceed with compiling the X-Fi driver.



**_Part 2 - Installing the patched X-Fi driver_**


First, make sure you uninstall it first, in case you already tried to compile but it failed. I found that if I just left it there, it would still give errors, so this is important! **[SIZE="5"]**[/SIZE]**

```
cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers
./configure
make uninstall
```

Then delete the entire Creative-dir. Again, only needed if you tried to install earlier.

Now unpack the patched driver you downloaded to your Home Folder earlier:

```
cd ~
tar zxvf XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz && cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18
```

And run the installer:

```
./installer
```

It *should* compile and install. If not, then maybe you did something wrong, it just won't work on your system or you're simply out of luck... ;-)

Then reboot. I had to reboot first or the driver wouldn't detect my X-Fi.
After the reboot, check if you have sound in a terminal:

```
alsamixer
```

A terminal-mixer should come up now. Ofcourse you can try the default mixer (right-click on the volume-icon --> Open Volume Control) too.
On my system, the drivers works just fine. Headphones work and sound in Flash-apps like YouTube too.
I used the Adobe-Flash package.

```
apt-get install flashplugin-nonfree
```

But like I said in the beginning, it may or may not work on your system.
Don't give up if it doesn't work right away. I spent almost two days getting it to work, but the satisfaction when it finally did made it all worth it...

Good luck!

**[SIZE="5"]*[/SIZE]** Sometimes after you install the nvidia-driver manually, this breaks the X-Fi-driver again. Just repeat Part 2 in that case. Or install the nvidia-driver first and then the X-Fi-driver.

**[SIZE="5"]**[/SIZE]** Uninstalling the Creative-driver from previous attempts is probably best done in recovery-mode, because no parts from the driver are loaded then.

### Post by amtam on 2008-04-28
> **mnnn said:**
> I'm using Kubuntu 7.10 64-bit with default kernel (SLUB) and it works (1.18 beta) with SLUB kernel so I don't understand why it is still neccessary to recompile kernel with SLAB. I thought Creative modified drivers to work with SLUB.

Yes, I know :)
7.10 indeed uses SLUB and the driver works OK in that distro. But maybe Creative used a work-around that only works in 7.10 and not in 8.04?

All I know is that if I don't change SLUB into SLAB and recompile the kernel, the Creative-driver will still compile but my system will lockup **every time** I try to load it. And it doesn't lockup if I re-compile with SLAB...:)

I read somewhere it might have something to do with the 2.6.22 kernel that 7.10 uses. Maybe if you 'downgrade' 8.04 to use the 2.6.22-kernel (don't even know if that's possible?) you won't need to recompile?

Your guess is as good as mine. In the mean time, I'll keep enjoying my fully operational Hardy... ;-)



Edit: mmmmm... it MAY not be necessary to change SLUB into SLAB after all... I loaded my working .config and changed it BACK to SLUB, recompiled and (after re-installing the driver) the x-fi still works? I tried it many times with the default, generic kernel and every time it failed. Maybe it's just the re-compiling itself that makes it work? Or the kernel hacking option?

Still, recompiling is still necessary I guess and changing SLUB into SLAB isn't hurting things eiher, so for now, I'll leave things as they are and do some more testing.

### Post by amtam on 2008-04-28
> **CentralX said:**
> Didn't you forgot to add "modules_image" to the make-kpkg line? After following your howto my wireless, video and internal audio driver were not loaded.

Isn't that only necessary for third-party modules with source code outside of the kernel source tree? The Creative-driver uses a separate installer with only partly 'open' source code.

> **mnnn said:**
> without surround sound (audio device surround40, surround51 doesn't exist)

Yes, but you have Creative to blame for that, since they didn't put it in the driver in the first place... ;)

### Post by Helskov on 2008-04-28
Finally got it to work with kernel 2.6.25 on Ubuntu 8.04. :)

for 2.6.25 kernel verify that the following kernel options are set:

needed for the obsolete symbol init_mm - may disappear in 2.6.26
Kode:
Kernel hacking -> Enable unused/obsolete exported symbols [*]


required by creative
Kode:
General setup -> Choose SLAB allocator (SLAB)


and verify that the entire ALSA sound system is build as modules nothing compiled in
Kode:
Device Drivers -> Sound -> Sound card support <M>

Download this modified driver from here:
[http://cincev.com/mnt/store/users/admin/xfidrv-1.18.2.tgz](http://cincev.com/mnt/store/users/admin/xfidrv-1.18.2.tgz)

Stolen from the thread on gentoo forum:
[http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-587921-postdays-0-postorder-asc-start-225.html](http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-587921-postdays-0-postorder-asc-start-225.html)

---

### Post by amtam on 2008-04-29
> **djchester said:**
> Same here, got problem with Nvidia driver after doing the custom kernel so I went back to vanilla. OSS works for most things except in flash videos so I'll be ok for now.

The reason you have a problem with your Nvidia-driver is because it is a restricted driver that is linked to the default, generic 2.6.24 kernel. So if you re-compile your kernel, the restricted driver/modules don't work anymore.

It can be fixed by [downloading](http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_amd64_169.12.html) the latest driver from Nvidia's site and install it manually.

If you move away from the default kernel, you always will have this problem with Nvidia-drivers. It probably is the same for ATI too.

---

tachyon@holodeck3:~$ lsmod | grep ctalsa
ctalsa                579564  2 
snd_pcm                94344  3 ctalsa,snd_pcm_oss
snd                    69416  12 ctalsa,snd_pcm_oss,snd_mixer_oss,snd_pcm,snd_seq_oss,snd_rawmidi,snd_seq,snd_timer,snd_seq_device

ctossrv               161600  7 ctalsa,haxfi,cthwiut,ctexfifx,ct20xut,ctsfman,emupia
```


If you don't get any output there, then obviously the modules aren't loaded.

tachyon@holodeck3:~$ dmesg | grep ctalsa
 [<ffffffff894938fa>] :ctalsa:_ZN13CtAudioDevice24_CommitFeatures_RendererE18enFMgrInitialState+0xc2/0x180

### Post by amtam on 2008-04-29
I just thought of something.... There might be a permissions problem with the patched driver I put on-line. Because I edited some files in the archive and then saved them, they took over my own user name and group.

I checked /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/src$ and sure enough, they were changed (not root, but my user name I use on my system)

It's 2 files: Makefile.build in /drivers and LinuxSys.c in /drivers/src/ossrv

Maybe you first have to change them back to root in the patched archive?
I don't really now how to restore permissions inside an archive, anyone?


Edit: I uploaded a new patchfile, hopefully with better permissions now. Could you please download it again and try compiling/installing? Just to be sure, I did so myself and I still have sound.

Just make sure you uninstall the old driver properly. Goto /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/
and do a ./configure and then a make uninstall and remove the entire directory.

### Post by djchester on 2008-05-02

Well it's a standard .deb package. But firefox asks the first time you find a flash content wich flash package to install. You can choose gnash, sfwcodec or Adobe Flash.

### Post by kj74 on 2008-05-02
I discovered pretty interesting bug in OSS drivers. It seems to be this one [http://www.4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2610](http://www.4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2610) I also hear tiny thump between songs. It's hard to spot, you might need headphones but it is nasty one. You can forget gapless playback, even fake crossfade crap. It's very easy to spot with Pink Floyd's Darkside Of The Moon.

Good thing is, i found very silly fix for it. You need to make sure you have vmix enabled. Basically start watching video file or listening to music, pause it and minimize. Start another player and watch/listen to whatever you want and everything should be fine. This might not fix it entirely but it helps a lot.

I am very close to throw this Creative crap out of window.

---

### Post by amtam on 2008-05-02
People who are feeling lucky can try [this](http://www.xs4all.nl/~tachyon1/config-lean.txt) customized kernel-config.

I used this .config for my own system. I changed a lot of things in it and one of them **might** be responsible for the fact that the XFi runs fine on my system and not on other people's systems who have tried my little howto... ;-)

There is only one network-driver enabled in it (sky2 for Marvell Yukon) so if you don't have that card, you will have to enable your own modules before you use it to compile a kernel.

I'm really beginning to like my X-Fi. Everything basically works as it should (except of course for stuff like surround, crystallizer etc etc.). I can even play games (Quake 4, UT2004) with it :)

Let's all hope Creative will soon release a better installer so we don't have to hack our systems all the time. Or that the Alsa developers come up with a decent driver now that Creative has finally released the specs to them.

---

### Post by mikko28 on 2008-05-03
I solved all my problems and now sound works perfectly. My solution was to return my Creative sound card and change it to Terratec Aureon which was my first option untill I made a bad decision based on illusions of mighty Creative. I thought that if I'll buy sound card it should be creative just because it is creative. I wish I'd done some research before buying anything so I would have learn more about misleading brands. Just a well-known brand doesn't mean it is the best option or at least the cheapest. I even got 25€ back when I changed to terratec and I don't notice any difference in sounds. The best of all is that I didn't have to tune anything beause all worked out-of-the-box. Luckily all ended well even thouhg I slipped and was a Creative owner some time.

Good luck with your Creatives!

---

I used the compiled 32bit version gst-plugins-good-0.10.6_v0.1.patch

My audio applications work such as VLC media player and the sound recorder.  I also can hear youtube after the flash install on the same site above.

System/Preferences/Sound

(have mic connected in mic input but does not record and spit back anything)

May I say the graphics are FASTER than it is in windows but I have no sound at all.  I also have wow installed under wine and it does have sound was its wine entry was set to OSS.  Its progressing but the system sounds and ETQW's no sound issue has me stumped.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-08
Run this dmesg command and attach the .txt file it produces. ```
dmesg >> dmesg_out.txt
``` Also lets tell ubuntu to manually load the driver by putting ctsound in /etc/modules ```
echo 'ctsound' | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```

### Post by PRGUY85 on 2008-05-10
**ADDENDUM2: Sound in Flash**
1. Run:
```
file /usr/lib/libflashsupport.so
```
If this returns info on the file, skip ahead to step 4. If the file isn't found...
2. Save the attached libflashsupport.so.gz to your Desktop
3. ```
cd ~/Desktop; gunzip libflashsupport.so.gz; sudo mv libflashsupport.so /usr/lib
```
4. create symbolic links to /usr/lib/firefox/plugins & /usr/lib/mozilla/plugins:
```
sudo ln -s /usr/lib/libflashsupport.so /usr/lib/firefox/plugins; sudo ln -s /usr/lib/libflashsupport.so /usr/lib/mozilla/plugins
```[/QUOTE]

### Post by AndyJS on 2008-05-13
> **NullHead said:**
> I'm not actually sure if the mic works in OSS :confused: I couldn't get mine to work, but I though it was user error :lolflag:

So I'd say it doesn't work if there are two people here who are saying their microphone doesn't work.

Just grabbed this from the OSS announcements post from the introduction of the XFI beta drivers..

> We're happy to release OSS 4.0 Build 1013 (binaries and sources under GPL/CDDL/BSD) 

Major new features: 

- Aded Beta driver for Sound Blaster XFi - playback working, record still problematic. Source code also released. 
- Full source code to MAudio Revolution and Delta series soundcards is now open sourced. 

Changelog: 

- Changes to mixgen.c 
- Contribution to ossxmix. Now there are tabs for each mixer device unless the -d parameter is given on command line. 
- Added the patches for Juli@ (not tested yet). 
- Minor changes to the HDA dedicated mixer for Abit AA8 motherboard. 
- Rewritten the HDA dedicated mixer for Ferrari 5k. 
- Some changes to the hdaudio driver related with the above. Also some hacks to enable the HDA modem codec (not functional yet). 
- Added plattform=i86pc to sbxfi/.config. 
- Renamed mixer.c to mixer_core.c. 
- Minor change in vimx_core.c. Default recording source make is now "locked" instead of "default" if there are more than one applications using the device. 
- Added dedicated mixer (same as ferrari5k) for Acer Aspire 5052. 
- Changed ossmix and ossxmix to use getopt. 
- Fixed a bug in getopt() handling in ossmix.c. 
- Changed Solaris/ossdetect and ossdevlinks to use getopt(). 
- Updated envy24ht/.devices to support Juli@.


You should note it says recording is still problematic and I have seen nothing in the announcements for the later builds to indicate that the situation has improved. Unless anybody can verify that they have recording/mic input working then I would say it probably doesn't work yet!

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-05-14
zooLiz, try the OSS 4.1 build.

```
sudo apt-get -y install linux-headers-`uname -r` build-essential gawk libtool libgtk2.0-dev libesd0 esound esound-common linux-sound-base gcc-multilib libesd0-dev mercurial libssl0.9.8 libssl-dev
cd /usr/src
sudo hg clone http://mercurial.opensound.com/ oss-devel
cd ~/
mkdir oss41build
cd oss41build/
sudo sh /usr/src/oss-devel/configure
sudo make
sudo make install
```

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-16
> **alonecity said:**
> I installed the new drivers and now Rhythmbox plays music full blast and I can't change the volume.

Use the guide located on the front page: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2) that one. Download the patch for your architecture and follow the simple instructions located inside the patch. The patch is located at the end of the guide that I just linked to. 

If that doesn't work you can add an applet to your task bar (right click>custom launcher>) and put in the name" oss mixer" and for the command "ossxmix" and you can manually turn down individual applications (like rhythmbox).
