### Post by amtam on 2008-05-17
For those people who are still interested in the Alsa-driver under 8.04, I have made some changes to my little [howto](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675), especially where the ALSA-section is concerned.

A few days ago, *kugel* asked me if I could do a fresh install and follow my own howto to the letter, because he thought I might have forgotten something in it. I fired up WUBI (which is great for testing) and made a fresh install and tried the howto. To my suprise, it failed... ](*,)

I got the same errors a few other people here got: "Unknown symbol bla bla bla" when trying to load the ctalsa-module.
So I took another good look at my own customized, working config and saw that there were some ALSA-options enabled that weren't in the howto.
So I did yet another fresh install, modified the aforementioned ALSA-settings, compiled and tada, it worked again! \\:D/
Also, I removed the part of the SLAB/SLUB issue, because it seems that's no longer relevant.

If you really have nothing else to do, you might give it another shot... ;)

---

I might try a fresh install to test this and if I get it working in one architecture I'll be sure to try it on the other.

### Post by amtam on 2008-05-17
Yes there was a problem with the ReiserFS, I read that on the Gentoo-forum.
User *the_root* had a fix for it:

> If you are using reiserfs you can expect kernel opps, it can be fixed, just open:
/usr/src/linux/fs/reiserfs/file.c , go to "const struct file_operations reiserfs_file_operations = {"
and add this line: .llseek = generic_file_llseek,
if should look like this:
const struct file_operations reiserfs_file_operations = {
.llseek = generic_file_llseek,
.read = do_sync_read,

So you have to hack the sources, which is rather 'ugly'... ;-)
Why don't you just use EXT3?

edit: Damn mnnn, you beat me to it... ;)

Hans Reiser was convicted of murdering his wife a few weeks ago, so I guess we won't be hearing from him anytime soon...

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-05-17
It is time for OSS4 to be included in the Linux kernel. OSS is compatible with a lot more apps than ALSA, which often has to emulate OSS function calls. Almost everything in the OSS4 catalog is GPL'd now, including Creative's X-fi code and VIA's Envy24(ht/pt) code, two of the worst companies when it comes to open-sourcing. I'm not saying that E-MU or ICEnsemble hardware is bad, quite the contrary. It's just unfortunate that two technologically elegant chipsets were picked up by two extremely monopolistic, proprietary entities.

OSS has been maintained by a small, but very dedicated and knowledgeable community of enthusiasts. It often receives criticism for "selling out" or being tainted by corporate money, but I find it hard to blame two guys (Hannu Savolainen & Dev Mazumdar) for trying to make a living doing something they love. They have honored NDA's and GPL'd where they could, making the majority of their earnings from large UNIX vendors (Sun, IBM, HP, etc.). The result of their work is very promising and technologically superior to ALSA, but it needs support from kernel developers for "full compatibility".

I'm not blaming anyone for buying Creative hardware, even though I may have in the past. Unfortunately, I bought an Envy24HT-based product myself before I got interested in the Linux community and open-source suppport. However, I'm extremely grateful for the patience that 4front has had in dealing with VIA and M-Audio on proprietary disclosure and crafting the Envy24HT driver. I'm currently listening to part of my music collection with foobar2000 (my audio player of choice because of DSP plugins) and it sounds wonderful. OSS4 allows me to use the hardware mixer on my card, so I can play CD's at native 16-bit, 44.1KHz format, with no resampling, no skipping, and no processor throttling. I can also enjoy my Neil Young Audio DVD's at 24-bit, 96 or 192 KHz playback.

For more specific technical info on the difference between OSS and ALSA, I suggest reading this blog article, keeping in mind that it'a about a year old and a lot of OSS4 drivers have been GPL'd since then: [http://insanecoding.blogspot.com/2007/05/sorry-state-of-sound-in-linux.html](http://insanecoding.blogspot.com/2007/05/sorry-state-of-sound-in-linux.html)
Thanks for reading.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-17
Temüjin, thank you very much for your feelings about OSSv4 and ALSA. I try to be as impartial as I can be, but for simply "convenience" I tend to lean  towards ALSA ... when the ALSA team releases their take on the driver into the next release, I do understand that we'll have to go through a compilation, make procedure witch probably won't be any better than what OSSv4 will offer.

I do have to say that until OSSv4 supports the x-fi to it's fullest I can't truly appreciate all the work that 4-front has done. There are advantages to both sound systems. I actually would like to have both of them installed at the same time; and have both with working x-fi drivers for supreme sound support for any application that ever comes my way.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-18
Did you get alsa-base re-installed? If you're trying to use the ALSA sound system again you should have alsa-base installed. ```
sudo apt-get install alsa-base
```

Well, that didn't help at first. But asoundconf unset-pulseaudio helped.

### Post by amtam on 2008-05-18
mnnn, try rebooting to recovery-mode (root prompt) and from there, uninstall the drivers first:

```
cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/
./configure
make uninstall

```

Remove the entire Creative-dir and then try to install again.
That is, if you haven't removed Ubuntu already... :lolflag:

sudo make
sudo make install

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-22
> **das letzte einhorn said:**
> There is indeed sound which came out from the X-FI when it tested the card. Can't test it with Banshee yet however because the program tells me that there is no mp3 codec coming with the program. Do you know which package I must get for that?

Well if you're building from source using their beta, you can issue a ```
sudo apt-get build-dep banshee
``` and then you *should* have all you need to build banshee from source, but if all else fails, there is a good thread here where some people have built .debs for the new banshee beta ... I haven't tried these debs yet because I built from source, but feel free to. The support for banshee should be re-directed to that thread please. 
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=724337](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=724337)

> **greg-hao said:**
> Got it "working" or not, well the Creative Driver compiled with a charm etc on hardy 8.04 64Bit.

Buut, the Sound is noisy, like under Windows if played with a to high khz and rate (example : 24bit/192000 )

is there maybe a setting to lower that, or to fix the noise ?

i can hear ppl speaking etc on youtube as example, jut with noise.

I don't know if a setting to lower those things. If you're having problems with playing your high def music or movies then all I can tell you is that the Creative driver is in beta stage and probably doesn't support those high end features yet.

``` in a terminal. Doing so, however, does not change the situation in the least....

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-05-25
Henri, you may have an x86-64 CPU, but it appears you're running the x86 version of Ubuntu. The current x86-64 Ubuntu kernel is version 2.6.24-16, and you're using version 2.6.24-17 (which I believe is the latest x86 Ubuntu kernel).

To proceed, follow the instructions here: [http://www.4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054](http://www.4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054)
Then, try again with this .deb package: [http://www.4front-tech.com/release/oss-linux_v4.0-1015_i386.deb](http://www.4front-tech.com/release/oss-linux_v4.0-1015_i386.deb)

### Post by mhenriday on 2008-05-25
**Temûjin**, thanks for your speedy reply ! On my machine, a **HP Pavilion d4595se, AMD Athlon 64 X2 5000+ Dual Core**, I do indeed boot by default into the **2.6.24-17-generic** kernel, but I am surprised to read that this kernel is not available for **x86-64** boxes. I upgraded to **Hardy** from a 64-bit **Gutsy** setup, and all kernel changes that have been made since then are a direct results of updates from the Update Manager. Besides, whenever I attempt to install an **i386.deb** file (e g, the **OOo 3.0 beta**), I get the standard warning to the effect that the architecture is wrong (I am aware that **i386** architecture can be forced, but generally I choose to abstain from doing so and wait for native 64-bit builds). Moreover, the impression I get from, e g, the *_[page]("https://lists.ubuntu.com/archives/hardy-changes/2008-May/011429.html")_* posted by Tim Gardner is that the **2.6.24-17** kernel, at least in its most recent version, **2.6.24-17.31**, is available for both **x86** _and_ **x86_64** machines. But this is (obviously !) not my field of expertise, so I may easily have misinterpreted something here....

Two unsolved problems remain to me in using **Ubuntu** : the sound problem (thank you, **Creative** !), which sometimes forces me to boot into my **Windows** OS in order to do things like listening to audio presentations online, and another with which I can easily live, i e, an inability to use the «Ctrl + Shift + u, hexcode, spacebar» workaround to enter **Unicode** glyphs directly from my 105-key Scandinavian keyboard (I suspect that it might be my **SCIM 1.4.7** installation that is interfering here). I've been considering saving my documents, music, videos, etc to an external hard disk and performing a clean install of **Hardy** from a 64-bit ISO CD, to see whether I might then have better luck in resolving them. That would naturally be a bit of a pain where I sit, but if there's a chance it would help, I'm willing to try. Or do you think there's something else I can do to resore my setup to the *status quo ante* ?...

Let me say how much I appreciate your help and that provided by **Nullhead** and other posters on this valuable thread. I believed Shakespeare referred to this sort of altruism as «the milk of human kindness»....

Henri

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-05-25
That's interesting. I wonder how you got a version 2.6.24-17 kernel. Have you added any repositories other than the standard Hardy ones (or do you have it set to show proposed and/or pre-released updates) in the Software Sources dialog? I run the amd64 version myself and with the latest updates, I only see 2.6.24-16 available. Perhaps there is a difference in the English and Swedish versions? What does *uname -a* show?

Let's try some things before you do a full reinstall. First, use the first link I gave in my last post to completely purge the failed install. Then, make sure ALSA is unloaded and not interfering. The following command should run and return no output:
```
lsmod | grep snd
```

You can also try installing from recovery mode. Press 'Esc' after you start your system and the BIOS/POST procedure is finished to access the GRUB menu. Choose recovery mode and you'll be prompted with a few more options. Choose 'drop to root terminal' and follow the install guide again, though you're already in a "failsafe terminal" and you shouldn't need to restart in the middle of the procedure.

gksudo gedit /var/lib/dpkg/status

Tomish,

### Post by mhenriday on 2008-05-26
After I entered ```
gksudo gedit /var/lib/dpkg/status
``` the .deb package list opened ; it was nearly overwhelming to see how many I had installed ! At any rate, after some searching I was able to to find the oss-linux package and remove it from the list. I then did```
dpkg -- purge oss-linux
```with the following result:> dpkg: behöver en åtgärd att utföra [a measure to execute is required]which, if I understand the matter aright, should be taken to mean that oss-linux has already been purged....

Should I now go back to the beginning of [Temüjin's tutorial]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4874981") and follow the steps outlined there (including the addenda) ?...

Two reflexions : Firstly, the instructions found for recovering from a failed .deb installation on the [*_4Front Technologies page_*]("http://www.4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054") begin as follows :> On Ubuntu or Debian, if you don't have the necessary packages listed in the "Installing on Ubuntu 6.10" notes then OSS will likely fail to install and
you will need to do the following to recover:

1) cd /var/lib/dpkg/info
2) rm oss-linux*
3) edit /var/lib/dpkg/status and look for oss-linuxThe first two instructions are examples of code which should be entered as is in a terminal. The third element, however, is a combination : «edit» is a meta-instruction to the reader, while «/var/lib/dpkg/status» is code to be entered into a terminal. The unwary and less knowledgeable reader, like myself, easily becomes confused by this hop from instructions on code to be entered in a terminal to a meta-instruction _about_ such code. Had the instructions been written in the form
> 1) cd /var/lib/dpkg/info
2) rm oss-linux*
3) gksudo gedit /var/lib/dpkg/status and look for oss-linuxthe misunderstanding would not have arisen. A word to the wise - when writing tutorials, take into consideration the capacity of your lesser brothers and sisters to misunderstand....

Secondly, as I have attempted to make explicit, I am very grateful for all the help that I have received from other users, like **Temüjin** and **NullHead**. The latter writes :> The poll on the top of this thread is left over from an older method that was very hard to get working for most people. Fell free to vote on it ...But when I log on to the thread I am greeted with the following statement :> Hello, mhenriday you are browsing the ARCHIVE section at Ubuntu Forums. You are able to reply/post to threads that are ALREADY created, however you cannot create NEW threads in the archive.followed by :> View Poll Results: Did this work for you?
Yes 		48 	44.04%
No 		61 	55.96%
Voters: 109. You may not vote on this pollWhy can't I vote on this poll ? And I may not be the only one so disenfranchised ; the total number of voters that I see, 109, has not changed over the course of the last month, during which time some twenty-odd pages have been added....

Henri

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-26
The poll must be locked because this thread is actually in the archived threads forum. When there was a huge software update a wile back they moved a bunch of threads into the archived section ... when they moved this one the poll must have been locked. 

Did you actually follow the steps you are nicely clarifying?>  1) cd /var/lib/dpkg/info
2) rm oss-linux*
3) gksudo gedit /var/lib/dpkg/status and look for oss-linux 
I gather that you have probably followed them, but if not just try them again.

I suspect you have to use sudo to properly run the command "rm oss-linux*" so perhaps try doing that commands again with sudo (?)```
cd /var/lib/dpkg/into
sudo rm oss-linux*
``` 

Also if I may ask .... could you translate your next terminal dump with [babel fish]("http://babelfish.yahoo.com/translate_url?fr=avbbf-us") so us English only speakers can read it.

### Post by mhenriday on 2008-05-26
Hullo, **NullHead**, and thank you for one of the most interesting - and most useful - threads on this forum ! One of the consequences of having a thread removed to the archives, it seems, in addition to users being unable to vote in a poll, is that they cannot use the «thank-you» button to thank other users from whose help they have benefitted ; I am sure that many of us who have participated here subsequent to the removal who would have liked to be able to express their appreciation in that manner. Please take the thought for the deed !...

You are, of course, quite right - the «rm» command requires a superuser «sudo». That I was able to figure out for myself ; it was rather my failure to observe that «edit» was here not a command to be entered directly in a terminal, but one to me which required that I enter «gksudo gedit» in the former which led to the confusion. In any event, I did carry out the steps outlined above. My question to Temüjin, which I can also address to you, is if, having done so, it would now be appropriate for me to go back and try to follow the procedure for downloading and installing the oss-linux package....

### Post by mnnn on 2008-05-28
jukingeo: No driver available today for X-Fi in Linux has more than 2 channels output. Don't even think about DSP or things like that (I guess the card is running in some compatibility mode, where there aren't many things available, just pure audio playback and record).

> **jukingeo said:**
> 
However, I did notice on the OSS app that the main stereo output is on the level controls labeled "Totem".  Then there are outputs for PCM 3 through PCM 9.  What do these all do.

That's interesting, because when I had Ubuntu 7.10 and using ALSA method (from Creative) I also noticed that there are volume level controls for rear, center, sub., channels. But none of them actually did something (except front). I guess it's just some kind of compatibility thing.

Anyway, if you use 2 speakers or headphones for playback of sound, you can use normally this driver in Linux. However, if you require something more like 5.1 surround sound (like me), you won't find it in Linux **yet**. This should be available either in OSS drivers sometime, or in ALSA driver (from ALSA). I guess Creative will just blow off their driver, it's not their priority.

---

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-05-28
jukingeo,
The outputs you see in the mixer are virtual outputs (that's why it's called vmix).
[http://manuals.opensound.com/usersguide/index.html](http://manuals.opensound.com/usersguide/index.html)

dpkg = Debian package

dir = ls (stands for list) in Linux/UNIX. Use ls -la for more detailed output. Pipe the output into more (ls (-la) | more) to see one screen at a time, using the spacebar to get to the next screen and 'q' to quit.

[http://manuals.opensound.com/usersguide/index.html](http://manuals.opensound.com/usersguide/index.html)

dpkg = Debian package

dir = ls (stands for list) in Linux/UNIX. Use ls -la for more detailed output. Pipe the output into more (ls (-la) | more) to see one screen at a time, using the spacebar to get to the next screen and 'q' to quit.

On the opensound.com link, what am I looking for?  There are quite a few sublinks on those pages and I don't know which one will how me how to use the mixer.

The command-line is extremely powerful in Linux/UNIX, and I recommend you do a search on UNIX basic commands.

> jukingeo: No driver available today for X-Fi in Linux has more than 2 channels output. Don't even think about DSP or things like that (I guess the card is running in some compatibility mode, where there aren't many things available, just pure audio playback and record).

> That's interesting, because when I had Ubuntu 7.10 and using ALSA method (from Creative) I also noticed that there are volume level controls for rear, center, sub., channels. But none of them actually did something (except front). I guess it's just some kind of compatibility thing.

They don't seem to do anything in the OSS version either...unless there is a control I don't know about.

> Anyway, if you use 2 speakers or headphones for playback of sound, you can use normally this driver in Linux. However, if you require something more like 5.1 surround sound (like me), you won't find it in Linux **yet**. This should be available either in OSS drivers sometime, or in ALSA driver (from ALSA). I guess Creative will just blow off their driver, it's not their priority.

I guess I will have to see what the future brings though.  So I guess I am just stuck with 2 channel sound for now :(.  

Ok Ok...it is better than nothing.

Oh!  I got YouTube to work as well, but it does cause hang ups every so often.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-05-30
> **macuser2000 said:**
> I'm running Ubuntu 8.04 64-bit. How do I install the creative sound blaster x-fi driver for my system?

Well as of now I don't recommend the Creative driver to anyone that isn't willing to do some work. You can use amtam's guide here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675j](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675j) Witch some say it works, but I have yet to test it; as I've been really busy lately. I do, however, recommend the guide on the first post of this thread. There is a link there to another guide that will help you install a driver from the OSS Team. 

It's a good guide and it does work, ***but*** if you really want the creative driver than use the guide I linked to.

### Post by amtam on 2008-05-31
> **goofeyfoot said:**
> cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18
michael@michael-desktop:/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18$ 
michael@michael-desktop:/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18$ ls
drivers  XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18-installer.log
michael@michael-desktop:/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18$ ./configure
bash: ./configure: No such file or directory
michael@michael-desktop:/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18$ make uninstall
make: *** No rule to make target `uninstall'.  Stop.
michael@michael-desktop:/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18$

Michael, it looks like you're trying to uninstall from the wrong directory. Uninstalling should be done in

/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/**drivers**

and not

/opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18


Try this:

```
cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers
./configure
make uninstall
```

### Post by amtam on 2008-05-31
Michael, try this:

Goto /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18

and then type this:

```
chmod -R 777 drivers
```


Then do the sudo su thing again and try to uninstall again.

### Post by amtam on 2008-05-31
Michael, you say you boot from the Live-CD. That complicates things. You have to 'chroot' into your Ubuntu-install first to get the job done. But that is rather complicated.

Why don't you just boot into recovery-mode? You really don't need the GUI to uninstall the drivers.
Boot into recovery-mode, choose 'root-prompt only' (or something like that, can't really remember) and then try the chmod -R 777 drivers and uninstalling again.

sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg
```after it fails to give you your gui on a normal boot.

Sorry but I do not know of a mixing program to do what you want to do. I would like one also, but I'm afraid you're going to have to try to use the software mixers that come in applications. BMPx has a mixer, but it fails to "mix" wile using OSS as its output. 
> **Penguin Power said:**
> How do i tell applications such as Rhythmbox to use OSS? mplayer seems to have no trouble doing so, but VLC, Rhythmbox and the rest only work (strangely) part of the time. Sometimes and not others.

Unfortunately Rhythmbox does not have a selection for changing the sound system. I'm using banshee and bmpx and I actually really like them! I will instruct on how to install banshee. 

Install dependencies for banshee: ```
sudo apt-get build-dep banshee
``````
sudo apt-get install build-essential automake
```Download banshee:```
svn co http://svn.gnome.org/svn/banshee/trunk/banshee

```Move to the banshee directory for configuration and installation: ```
cd banshee
```Now configure banshee:```
./autogen.sh --prefix=/usr

```Make and install it: ```
make && sudo make install
```

That should do it for banshee; BMPx you can get from here: [http://getdeb.net/app/Beep+Media+Player](http://getdeb.net/app/Beep+Media+Player) That website also has banshee, but it is rather outdated and my method of doing it gives you the most up-to-date version of banshee.

### Post by mhenriday on 2008-06-06
A week or so ago, after posting my latest distress calls (see pp 86 & 87 above), I decided to re-install **OSS4** and see what would happen. I got the **Ubuntu** startup sound, and could once again listen to music and videos that I had saved to my HDD, but still found myself unable to listen to, eg, **YouTube** videos directly from the web. Then, after installing a slew of updates a couple of days later, I decided to try again and, lo and behold/hear, I could open a **Youtube** video and enjoy not merely the moving pictures, but the sound as well ! Shades of *The Jazz Singer* ! And heartfelt thanks to **NullHead**, **Temüjin**, and all the others who have tried to steer me in the right direction !...

But I wouldn't be the person I am if I had been satisfied to leave it at that - I decided to try installing **Skype**, even though the only packages available are for 32-bit builds, which requires forcing the architecture, something which for reasons I am unable to articulate I dislike doing. **Cappy** has provided us with a most useful [_*thread*_]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=432295") on this topic, so I chose to follow the instructions there. Alas, as the screendump attached hereto shows, no joy - at least as yet. I can't help wondering if the problem is that, as I'm running 64-bit **Hardy**, I've installed the 64-bit **OSS4** deb package. Can it be that the forced **Skype** i386 deb architecture fails to work on such a setup ? Does this mean that in order to be able to use the service, I am going to have to wait until **Skype** vouchsafes us a native 64-bit package or might  it be something else entirely which is causing the difficulties ? Grateful as always for any suggestions !...

Henri

### Post by mhenriday on 2008-06-06

I would disable your motherboard sound mixer. This could either be, 

1: OSS is confused to which sound device is supposed to be used.

2: Your onboard sound device and your x-fi are conflicting. 

Also make sure that the speakers are plugged into your X-Fi and not your onboard device. 

Once you've accomplished those things, test out your sound card; ```
osstest
```

### Post by NullHead on 2008-06-14
You can learn how to disable your onboard sound by poking through your bios. If you're unfamiliar with this, then you better leave it alone. 

Most applications need to be manually set to use the OSS sound system. A good program I use to play my music library is banshee. It is a rather good program witch, if I remember, will automatically detect OSS and play through it rather than ALSA. Other programs like audacious need to be changed over to OSS, but it's really easy to do. You can go into the options, or preferences, and change the sound output to use OSS.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-06-15
> **PRGUY85 said:**
> Hey there, I installed Ubuntu Hardy 32-bit on my desktop (had 64-bit, wanted a more stable solution).  I installed the X-FI driver from the site and did the OSS solution as well (then noticed I didn't have to install the X-FI Driver) and got sound.  Yet, I am having several problems with my computer that I am not having on my laptop also running 32-bit and ALSA (cannot compile Pidgin with MSNP14 support, Avant Window Navigator works but has issues...). I want to go back to using my onboard sound until ALSA releases their drivers...

I deleted the OSS package, removed the blacklisted HDA INtel and ALSA modules, uninstalled the XFI Driver...and yet I cannot get the test sound to work on Ubuntu.  I get the following:

audiotestsrc wave=sine freq=512 ! audioconvert ! audioresample ! gconfaudiosink: Failed to connect stream: Invalid argument



Any ideas?

> **PRGUY85 said:**
> Also, I did sudo soundon and it says:

sudo soundon
OSS is already loaded.


So, even if I uninstall OSS and I am putting ALSA as the default option, I am getting OSS running?

^ - I'm having a hard time understanding your exact problem/question here :( 

I'll take a shot at it. 

It seems that you tried to use the 64bit version of ubuntu but had problems with it, so you switched to the 32bit version and now have problems with the X-Fi sound drivers; both Creative's and OSS. 

For the removing OSS; try using the link on the OP that explains how to uninstall OSS. For the broken Creative driver. You should: ```
sudo cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers/
sudo ./configure
sudo make uninstall
```
I guess it sounds, overall, that your install of ubuntu is rather screwed up. 
> **minibeardeath said:**
> is there a method for disabling (or re-enabling) the onboard soundcard w/o using the BIOS?
(this is a loaded question. i know there is a file that lists which drivers for OSS to load on boot, but i cannot remember where the file is so that i can re-enable my onboard audio because i am trying to setup a 5.1 system, and the x-fi doesnot have more than 2.1 support)

You can try using the **"*lsmod*"** command in a terminal and see if you can't locate the modules that load your sound; locate them and add them to the file /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist like so: ***echo "blacklist <module name here>" | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist" ***

Likewise, if you wish to "un-blacklist" your sound module you can always edit out the module added by the command I gave earlier, ***"gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist"*** .

### Post by minibeardeath on 2008-06-16

### Post by NullHead on 2008-06-16
> **minibeardeath said:**
> thanks, but that is not quite what i need. oss currently works fine with the x-fi card. what i am trying to do is to eanble my second sound card (the "on-board" audio came on a removable riser card). when  i first installed OSSv4, i went into a text file where i disabled the secondary sound card from being loaded on boot. the problem is that i cannot remember where that file is. currently, my computer will not recognize that i have the second sound card. (i know for a fact that it is enabled in the bios) also, sometimes on boot, i will get a screen w/ text and it says that open sound system has failed to load "#hdaudio". i am trying to find the file where i can remove the "#" from infront of the hdaudio so that it will load.

Oh I understand now. I believe the file you're looking for it, /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist . You can open the file like so: ```
gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```That is the file where you disabled your onbord hd-audio card.

---

### Post by minibeardeath on 2008-06-16
this is the contents of etc/modprobe.d/blacklist:
> # This file lists those modules which we don't want to be loaded by
# alias expansion, usually so some other driver will be loaded for the
# device instead.

# evbug is a debug tool that should be loaded explicitly
blacklist evbug

# these drivers are very simple, the HID drivers are usually preferred
blacklist usbmouse
blacklist usbkbd

# replaced by e100
blacklist eepro100

# replaced by tulip
blacklist de4x5

# causes no end of confusion by creating unexpected network interfaces
blacklist eth1394

# snd_intel8x0m can interfere with snd_intel8x0, doesn't seem to support much
# hardware on its own (Ubuntu bug #2011, #6810)
blacklist snd_intel8x0m

# causes failure to suspend on HP compaq nc6000 (Ubuntu: #10306)
blacklist i2c_i801

# replaced by p54pci
blacklist prism54

# replaced by b43 and ssb.
blacklist bcm43xx

# most apps now use garmin usb driver directly (Ubuntu: #114565)
blacklist garmin_gps

# replaced by asus-laptop (Ubuntu: #184721)
blacklist asus_acpi


the file im looking for contains a list of audio devices that are detected (or detectable but disabled) by the computer and OSS

The outputs you see in the mixer are virtual outputs (that's why it's called vmix).
[http://manuals.opensound.com/usersguide/index.html](http://manuals.opensound.com/usersguide/index.html)

dpkg = Debian package

dir = ls (stands for list) in Linux/UNIX. Use ls -la for more detailed output. Pipe the output into more (ls (-la) | more) to see one screen at a time, using the spacebar to get to the next screen and 'q' to quit.

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-06-17
> **NullHead said:**
> Perhaps you should try "**sudo apt-get install -f"** or "**sudo dpkg-reconfigure timidity**

Ok,

I did both items and the second part came back with an error message saying that timidity was only partially installed.  So with that in mind I went into Synaptic and fully removed timidity.

After removing timidity, I was able to reinstall OSS successfully.

However this time around things were different:

First off, OSS looks different.  The window is A LOT smaller and instead of the multiple output sliders there are only two sets; one for playback and one for record (I did indeed verify that the window does say Sound Blaster X-Fi).

Second, many applications that didn't work on my first foray are now working.  And applications that were working are no longer working.

Let me explain:

Initially after my installation of OSS, I didn't have sound with any of my emulators (GENS, NES, ZSNES, DosBox, Stella).  Also none of my native Linux games had sound either (Neverball, Super Tux).

Some applications such as Audacity & Cinelerra did work along with streaming audio on the internet.  Totem Movie Player works when playing audio files that I have stored on hard disk.

The system sounds within Ubuntu didn't work either.

I had to use a sound layer called Pulse Audio in order to get sound working on the other applications, which I did, however, certain applications such as GENS and DosBox were distorted sounding and I couldn't fix that problem no matter what I did with the settings within the programs.

However, Pulse Audio knocked out my streaming audio/video on the internet and supposedly there was a fix for this, but the fix was aimed at Pulse audio overlaying ALSA and I didn't know that.  So naturally the streaming audio fix, blew my entire sound out completely and I was forced to delete everything (sound related) and reinstall.  Also prior to reinstalling OSS, I did upgrade Hardy to Ubuntu Studio.

Fast forwarding to now. 

I have ONLY OSS and not Pulse loaded on and these are my findings:

1) Totem is working again.
2) Ubuntu sounds are working, which they never did before.
3) Linux games do have sound, but now they have a bit more distortion than before.
4) ALL of my emulators work with the exception of GFCE (NES Emulator).  DosBox still is a bit distorted at times, but it is MUCH better than with my first combination of Pulse & Oss. GENS in which I had so much trouble getting to work, is working beautifully on the recommended settings. The audio is the right speed and it is crystal clear!

That is the good news.  Now comes the bad.

1) NONE of my Ubuntu Studio applications have sound.  So that means Audacity, Ardour, Hydrogen, Cinelerra, Open Movie Editor

2) My internet doesn't have sound either.  Streaming audio from sites such as YouTube do not work. Now here is the kicker.  When first started this 2nd installation of OSS, I DID have audio at first on the internet, however, when I went to check Audacity for sound, it completely locked up to the point I had to reboot.  Since that reboot, my internet doesn't have sound.  

3) As stated above out of my gaming programs for some reason only the NES emulator has no sound.


Now comes the ugly.

1) Naturally, all the items that are not working, I would like to be working.  Which really as of this stage would be my NES emulator, streaming internet audio, and of course ALL my Ubuntu Studio applications.


For some reason, this time around, most of the game applications ARE working properly, but the native Ubuntu Studio programs are not working.  By any chance has OSS been updated in the past couple weeks?  If so, that would make sense considering the program does look different from the first time I installed it.

Pulse Audio is what knocked out my streaming audio (and also the video) the first time.

I was really happy this time when my game programs were working AND the internet as well, but as I said, when I went to check out Audacity, something got blown out of whack and now, once again, I have no internet audio.

So being that I brought everyone up to speed as to what is going on, I am hoping that there is a solution in which I can get the rest of my audio back up.

For now I am going to hold off on installing the Pulse Audio layer as it appears that just about all my games are working with OSS without it.

Well, time to get to some gaming!

Geo

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-06-17
PulseAudio is not fully compatible with OSS4. If you need a low-latency audio server, use JACK.

Did you download the libflashsupport.so file attached to the guide and create the appropriate symbolic links?

Can you post the output from these commands?:
```
cd /dev
ls -la | grep dsp
```

Then I just would have to set up software synths only to work through JACK. ### Post by AndyZ on 2008-06-18
> **amtam said:**
> Below are the steps I made to get the Creative Alsa-driver working under Ubuntu Hardy 8.04 AMD64.
Disclaimer: just because it worked on my machine, doesn't mean it will on yours. YMMV.

Basically, you need to do 2 things:

1. [Download](http://www.xs4all.nl/~tachyon1/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz) this patched Creative-installer to your Home Folder.

(if you downloaded it before, please do so again; i fixed some permissions in it)

I patched it with several fixes I found in this and other threads.

2. Recompile your kernel. It needs some changes, mostly ALSA-related. 


If you don't recompile your kernel, the driver will still compile and install, but it will lock up your system or segfault.
At least, it did so on my system.

A little warning upfront: a recompile of the kernel will almost certainly break your Nvidia-driver.
You can download and install it manually afterwards from [here](http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_amd64_169.12.html) (64-bits driver).
Or you can check out the [NVIDIA](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=301499) or [ATI](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=515573) HOW-TO's... ;-)

So here we go. I use the root-account when compiling stuff, so I don't have to sudo all the time. 
If you don't, remember to put sudo in front of all the commands! 

Or just type
```
sudo bash
```
in a terminal so you only have to sudo once.

Be sure to update the system first:

```
apt-get update && apt-get upgrade
```

Install some necessary packages:

```
apt-get install kernel-package libncurses5-dev fakeroot bzip2 build-essential
```

Download the 2.6.24 kernel-sources from the Ubuntu-repository

```
apt-get install linux-source-2.6.24
```

It will download to /usr/src. There, you have to unpack it and make a symlink to it:

```
cd /usr/src
tar jxvf linux-source-2.6.24.tar.bz2
ln -s linux-source-2.6.24 linux
cd /usr/src/linux
```

You are now ready to start compiling.
Use the .config from your working, default kernel as a basis for the new kernel:

```
cp /boot/config-`uname -r` ./.config
```

Or, if you're really feeling lucky, you can download my own customized, lean-and-mean kernelconfig [here](http://www.xs4all.nl/~tachyon1/config-lean.txt) ;-)
Be warned though, I ripped out a lot of stuff. There is only one network driver for instance, the sky2-module for Marvell Yukon.

Then run:

```
make menuconfig
```

Go to Load Alternate Config File and choose .config (in the lower part of the main menu).

Then change the following (if not already checked):

```
Kernel hacking -> Enable unused/obsolete exported symbols [*]
```

(thanks to BigMichi1 on the Gentoo-forum)

And make sure ALSA is build as follows:
(ALSA = Advanced Linux Sound Architecture)

```
Device Drivers -> Sound -> Sound card support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> Advanced Linux Sound Architecture <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Sequencer support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Sequencer dummy client <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS Mixer API <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS PCM (digital audio) API <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS PCM (digital audio) API - include plugin system [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS Sequencer API [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> RTC Timer Support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Use RTC as default sequencer timer [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Verbose procfs contents [*]

```

Untick "Support old ALSA API".
That's all you have to do for this particular HOWTO, but you can make other changes if you want.
I always change stuff like processor-architecture and I remove a LOT of unused stuff (like ISDN, PCMCIA and other hardware I just don't have.
It wil shave a LOT from your compiling time... ;-)
But if you don't really know what your doing, leave everything untouched because you can seriously screw up things... ;-)
Exit the main-menu and save the new kernel-configuration (it will ask you to). Then build the kernel with the following two commands:

```
make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

This can take a while, depending on the speed of your system. It takes about 10 minutes on my Core 2 Duo@ 4GHz.

Installing the new kernel.
There are now 2 .deb-packages in /usr/src.

```
cd /usr/src
ls -l
```

They are called something like:

linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb and
linux-headers-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb

If you have the i386-version of Hardy, the filenames will be slightly different, but you get the idea.
Install them with these commands:

```
dpkg -i linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
dpkg -i linux-headers-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.23.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
```

Check /boot/grub/menu.list to see if the new kernel is present.
Reboot the system with 'shutdown -r now' and after that, check with 'uname -r' if the new kernel is active.
If yes, you can proceed with compiling the X-Fi driver.

First, make sure you uninstall it first, in case you already tried to compile but it failed. I found that if I just left it there, it would still give errors, so this is important! 

```
cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers
./configure
make uninstall
```

Then delete the entire Creative-dir.
Now unpack the patched driver you downloaded to your Home Folder earlier:

```
cd ~
tar zxvf XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz && cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18
```

And run the installer:

```
./installer
```

It *should* compile and install. If not, then maybe you did something wrong, it just won't work on your system or you're simply out of luck... ;-)

Then reboot. I had to reboot first or the driver wouldn't detect my X-Fi.
After the reboot, check if you have sound in a terminal:

```
alsamixer
```

A terminal-mixer should come up now. Ofcourse you can try the default mixer (right-click on the volume-icon --> Open Volume Control) too.
On my system, the drivers works just fine. Headphones work and sound in Flash-apps like YouTube too.
I used the Adobe-Flash package.

```
apt-get install flashplugin-nonfree
```

But like I said in the beginning, it may or may not work on your system.
Don't give up if it doesn't work right away. I spent almost two days getting it to work, but the satisfaction when it finally did made it all worth it... ;-)

Good luck!

P.S. Nullhead: do whatever you want with this little, probably very imperfect HOWTO... ;-)

I have to say, that after trying all various methods this is the only one that worked on my system. Wasn't an easy way but certainly was worth it. Running Hardy amd64 with X-Fi Fatal1ty SB (ALSA) working :D.

### Post by NullHead on 2008-06-19
From what you've said, it sounds like you really **need** JACK. I'm not familiar with JACK and I can run a google search just as easily as you can. I recommend you try installing ESD. That is what gives us system sounds in regular ubuntu and I suspect it might prove useful in ubuntu studio too. 

I'll test recording with the new version of OSS for you then; if you can't. 

I recommend you try installing ESD like suggested in the first post guide.

> 1. If you want the ability to hear system sounds, install ESD (Enlightened Sound Daemon) with the commands below. Note that this will remove libesd-alsa0 if installed. Remember to reinstall that package if you ever switch back to ALSA for some reason.
Code:
```

sudo apt-get install esound esound-clients esound-common libesd0 libesd0-dev gstreamer0.10-esd
```

I wish not to (rank) the users on this forum from "expert to n00b" here, but if you're uncertain with this then don't try it. It would seem that you want functional sound at an easy price. OSS is the answer for you then; the official driver isn't what you want to attempt. There is always a risk involved when you're messing with kernels, but if you really don't know much about the procedure, then I strongly suggest you stay away from the heavy kernel compiling and tweaking of the official driver package. 

If you're feeling interested or simply lucky you can read through [this]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Kernel/Compile") page. I suggest you get to know the terminal a little bit better and go through the commands involved with some of the steps in that guide I linked to. For example, I can see they use apt-get to fetch the kernel source. I would go through and read some of the man pages on apt-get. To read the "man" pages you can type **"man <command name goes here>"** so you'd want to put in "**man apt-get**".

sudo apt-get install libbio2jack0
```

### Post by nsx241 on 2008-06-24
> **amtam said:**
> Below are the steps I made to get the Creative Alsa-driver working under Ubuntu Hardy 8.04 AMD64.
Disclaimer: just because it worked on my machine, doesn't mean it will on yours. YMMV.

Basically, you need to do 2 things:

1. [Download](http://www.xs4all.nl/~tachyon1/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz) this patched Creative-installer to your Home Folder.

(if you downloaded it before, please do so again; i fixed some permissions in it)

I patched it with several fixes I found in this and other threads.

2. Recompile your kernel. It needs some changes, mostly ALSA-related. 


If you don't recompile your kernel, the driver will still compile and install, but it will lock up your system or segfault.
At least, it did so on my system.

A little warning upfront: a recompile of the kernel will almost certainly break your Nvidia-driver.
You can download and install it manually afterwards from [here](http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_amd64_169.12.html) (64-bits driver).
Or you can check out the [NVIDIA](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=301499) or [ATI](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=515573) HOW-TO's... ;-)

So here we go. I use the root-account when compiling stuff, so I don't have to sudo all the time. 
If you don't, remember to put sudo in front of all the commands! 

Or just type
```
sudo bash
```
in a terminal so you only have to sudo once.

Be sure to update the system first:

```
apt-get update && apt-get upgrade
```

Install some necessary packages:

```
apt-get install kernel-package libncurses5-dev fakeroot bzip2 build-essential
```

Download the 2.6.24 kernel-sources from the Ubuntu-repository

```
apt-get install linux-source-2.6.24
```

It will download to /usr/src. There, you have to unpack it and make a symlink to it:

```
cd /usr/src
tar jxvf linux-source-2.6.24.tar.bz2
ln -s linux-source-2.6.24 linux
cd /usr/src/linux
```

You are now ready to start compiling.
Use the .config from your working, default kernel as a basis for the new kernel:

```
cp /boot/config-`uname -r` ./.config
```

Or, if you're really feeling lucky, you can download my own customized, lean-and-mean kernelconfig [here](http://www.xs4all.nl/~tachyon1/config-lean.txt) ;-)
Be warned though, I ripped out a lot of stuff. There is only one network driver for instance, the sky2-module for Marvell Yukon.

Then run:

```
make menuconfig
```

Go to Load Alternate Config File and choose .config (in the lower part of the main menu).

Then change the following (if not already checked):

```
Kernel hacking -> Enable unused/obsolete exported symbols [*]
```

(thanks to BigMichi1 on the Gentoo-forum)

And make sure ALSA is build as follows:
(ALSA = Advanced Linux Sound Architecture)

```
Device Drivers -> Sound -> Sound card support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> Advanced Linux Sound Architecture <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Sequencer support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Sequencer dummy client <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS Mixer API <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS PCM (digital audio) API <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS PCM (digital audio) API - include plugin system [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> OSS Sequencer API [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> RTC Timer Support <M>
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Use RTC as default sequencer timer [*]
Device Drivers -> Sound -> ALSA -> Verbose procfs contents [*]

```

Untick "Support old ALSA API".
That's all you have to do for this particular HOWTO, but you can make other changes if you want.
I always change stuff like processor-architecture and I remove a LOT of unused stuff (like ISDN, PCMCIA and other hardware I just don't have.
It wil shave a LOT from your compiling time... ;-)
But if you don't really know what your doing, leave everything untouched because you can seriously screw up things... ;-)
Exit the main-menu and save the new kernel-configuration (it will ask you to). Then build the kernel with the following two commands:

```
make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

This can take a while, depending on the speed of your system. It takes about 10 minutes on my Core 2 Duo@ 4GHz.

Installing the new kernel.
There are now 2 .deb-packages in /usr/src.

```
cd /usr/src
ls -l
```

They are called something like:

linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb and
linux-headers-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb

If you have the i386-version of Hardy, the filenames will be slightly different, but you get the idea.
Install them with these commands:

```
dpkg -i linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
dpkg -i linux-headers-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.23.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
```

Check /boot/grub/menu.list to see if the new kernel is present.
Reboot the system with 'shutdown -r now' and after that, check with 'uname -r' if the new kernel is active.
If yes, you can proceed with compiling the X-Fi driver.

First, make sure you uninstall it first, in case you already tried to compile but it failed. I found that if I just left it there, it would still give errors, so this is important! 

```
cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers
./configure
make uninstall
```

Then delete the entire Creative-dir.
Now unpack the patched driver you downloaded to your Home Folder earlier:

```
cd ~
tar zxvf XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz && cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18
```

And run the installer:

```
./installer
```

It *should* compile and install. If not, then maybe you did something wrong, it just won't work on your system or you're simply out of luck... ;-)

Then reboot. I had to reboot first or the driver wouldn't detect my X-Fi.
After the reboot, check if you have sound in a terminal:

```
alsamixer
```

A terminal-mixer should come up now. Ofcourse you can try the default mixer (right-click on the volume-icon --> Open Volume Control) too.
On my system, the drivers works just fine. Headphones work and sound in Flash-apps like YouTube too.
I used the Adobe-Flash package.

```
apt-get install flashplugin-nonfree
```

But like I said in the beginning, it may or may not work on your system.
Don't give up if it doesn't work right away. I spent almost two days getting it to work, but the satisfaction when it finally did made it all worth it... ;-)

Good luck!

P.S. Nullhead: do whatever you want with this little, probably very imperfect HOWTO... ;-)

/usr/lib/libflashsupport.so: ELF 32-bit LSB shared object, Intel 80386, version 1 (SYSV), not stripped
geo@geo-desktop:~$ file /usr/lib/firefox/plugins/libflashsupport.so
/usr/lib/firefox/plugins/libflashsupport.so: symbolic link to `/usr/lib/libflashsupport.so'
geo@geo-desktop:~$ file /usr/lib/mozilla/plugins/libflashsupport.so
/usr/lib/mozilla/plugins/libflashsupport.so: symbolic link to `/usr/lib/libflashsupport.so'
geo@geo-desktop:~$ 


From the look of it, it seems like it is there.  The "not stripped" thing does bother me, but I am not getting a "file not found" error..

Anyway, I also went into Jack Control and upon starting it I got an error message.

It says "Could not open ALSA sequencer as a client.  Alsa Midi patchbay will not be available".

GREAT!  More good news.  So I guess right off the bat I know I will have a problem with Midi.

Anyway, Jack Control came up as "Stopped".  I went into SETUP and saw there was a setting for "driver".  It was set to Alsa and I put it on OSS.  I turned off Jack Control and then turned it back on.  Now I get the same error message as above, but no instead of "Stopped" it comes up "Inactive".  I can no longer get into setup as Jack Control completely locks up and have I have to do a force quit.

I tried to start up Ardour, but it still crashes. I am going to try out some more applications to see if I get anything.

So, it looks like Jack CAN do OSS, or at least it supposed to.

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-06-24
jukingeo, see if this does the trick:
```
sudo ln -s /usr/lib/libflashsupport.so /usr/lib/firefox-3.0/plugins
```

If not, try compiling your own version of libflashsupport.so.

I've attached flashsupport.c to this post. Extract and save it somewhere and:
```
sudo cc -shared -O2 -Wall -Werror -fPIC flashsupport.c -o libflashsupport.so
sudo mv libflashsupport.so /usr/lib
```

> The "not stripped" thing does bother me
It simply means that there are comments in the library. A few strings of text don't take too much disk space :P

### Post by nsx241 on 2008-06-25
Back again. Amtam's instructions work perfectly as long as you're willing to compile your own kernel. My issue was caused by installing the nvidia display driver (173.14.09) AFTER installing the creative x-fi driver. I removed both drivers, then installed the nvidia driver first, and now sound works perfectly.

I don't know why this happened. Possibly some strange overlapping module dependencies or depmod's that get run when the video driver is loaded.

### Post by amtam on 2008-06-25
> **jukingeo said:**
> Who is Amtam? 


That would be me :)

A few months ago I wrote a small [HOWTO](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675) on installing the Creative Beta (or really Alpha) driver on Ubuntu 8.04 x64.

The 'OSS-way' is more mainstream and more suited for most people because it's (much) easier to install, but if you go the 'Alsa-way' the results can be quite satisfying also :)

But I'm not trying to 'promote' it or anything, it's just an alternative to OSS.
Sound-support for the X-Fi remains very shaky, for both OSS **and** ALSA.

Don't be put off by the compiling thing though; you don't have to actually program anything; it's just changing some stuff and waiting for the compile to finish....:lolflag:

I myself still have a working (with X-Fi sound) Ubuntu using WUBI; my main Ubuntu-install had to make room for OS-X Leopard  for the PC :)
Got that working too, but, of course, no X-Fi support there either... :(

Wow! Someone has hawk eyes.  That was fast :).

I don't have a 64 bit system, nor do I have an AMD.  It was only a couple of days ago that I noticed that in the subject heading, but the funny thing is that this thread seems to have the most information and the most solutions in regards to issues with the X-Fi card.  So I am sticking around. 

> The 'OSS-way' is more mainstream and more suited for most people because it's (much) easier to install, but if you go the 'Alsa-way' the results can be quite satisfying also :)

Oh, I am not doubting that.  but as it is being a beginner, naturally I am going to do what is easiest.  But it is also true that the easiest way is not necessarily the best way.  BUT if I am having all this trouble with OSS and trying to get it to work with my sound card and applications, I could only imagine the fiasco I would have trying to get the ALSA drivers to work.

> But I'm not trying to 'promote' it or anything, it's just an alternative to OSS.
Sound-support for the X-Fi remains very shaky, for both OSS **and** ALSA.

Yes, I am finding that out the hard way.  I know I am not going to part with my X-Fi because Windows is set up with it perfectly and I mainly use it for surround audio on my games.  While it is a bummer that I don't have surround with Ubuntu, all I am really asking for is a couple of audio channels in and out.  It looked like the OSS driver would do that for me.  However, with all the problems I been having I have been thinking about alternate solutions.

1) Get a USB sound device that is compliant with Ubuntu/Alsa
2) Perhaps try again with an older version of Ubuntu (Version 7.10) as I am reading much about the problems with Hardy right now.
3) Abandon Ubuntu altogether and go with another distro such as Jack Lab (Open SuSE) or Musix.  Both distros are meant for audio recording work like Ubuntu Studio.  Maybe I would fair better with something else.

> Don't be put off by the compiling thing though; you don't have to actually program anything; it's just changing some stuff and waiting for the compile to finish....:lolflag:

Programming and compiling are new to me as well.  Last year around this time I got bitten by the programming bug and ventured into the realm of the microcontroller.  So I bought a Basic Stamp programming kit and have been having a ball with it.  However, it is easy to program and compile because it uses the Basic programming language.  Compiling is a straightforward and pretty much automatic.  I been thinking about upgrading and learning the SX controller which is a controller that runs assembly but can be programmed in basic.  Overall it is a good learning tool to get into assembly programming.

So as you see, I am very much a beginner when it comes to programming.  This is all very much new ground for me.

But one day as my knowledge expands in regards to Linux, I sure would like to learn to do programming work with in it.  But that is far off from where I am now.

> I myself still have a working (with X-Fi sound) Ubuntu using WUBI; my main Ubuntu-install had to make room for OS-X Leopard  for the PC :)
Got that working too, but, of course, no X-Fi support there either... :(

Well, I am entertaining the idea of using an external sound card that is more compliant with Linux and ALSA, however, it is going to have to be something inexpensive. 

The thing is that over the past 3 years or so I have bought two expensive sound cards:

1) The Creative X-Fi which I bought at the then going price of $279.99
2) The Alesis IO-26 which is an 8 In / 8 Out professional audio interface which I purchased for $399.99.

As it turns out, both work fantastic in Windows, but not so for Linux.  While the X-Fi is at least partially supported, I am dead in the water with the Alesis IO-26.

I am aware that the RME Hammerfall cards are high end cards that work with Linux...however, they run on the order of $500 to $800.  I will more then likely use one of these on a dedicated Linux system for professional use. But as of now, that figure is not in pocketbook right now.

So I am looking into other sound device recommendations that will not have me tear into my machine and potentially disturb my Windows settings with my X-Fi card.

Thanx for chiming in.  Hopefully I can resolve this issue or perhaps either find an alternative sound device...or even an alternative distro.

Geo

It sounds like you're well equipped for compiling a kernel. Amtam's How-To makes it easy for a confident linux user to compile a kernel. By confident I mean willing to do some setup on their own with out the need for utilities like the restricted drivers manager. For example, when you compile a new kernel, the drivers that *were* installed now nolonger work with your new kernel and you'll either have to compile new drivers or try another version of the driver. In my case, I would have to re-install the madwifi drivers and the Nvidia drivers to be used with the new kernel that was just compiled. 

Also it is **NOT** a guarantee that after you compile your kernel that it will even work! The driver could not compile or the kernel could fail to boot. There is a number of things that could go wrong, but honestly if you're desperate to get sound, compiling a kernel is the way to go. 

I followed Amtam's first version of his guide and it worked for ubuntu 7.10 and I also tried his guide a few months ago when he tried to patch the driver the first time; it didn't work for me. I have not put much effort into it because I'm too laze to take the time to compile a kernel and play around with these stupid drivers from Creative. When I can use a ***almost*** as good driver from a free source and also support an organization directed at free usage, then I will. It's also allot faster and easier. :rolleyes:

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-06-25
> **NullHead said:**
> It sounds like you're well equipped for compiling a kernel. Amtam's How-To makes it easy for a confident linux user to compile a kernel. By confident I mean willing to do some setup on their own with out the need for utilities like the restricted drivers manager. For example, when you compile a new kernel, the drivers that *were* installed now nolonger work with your new kernel and you'll either have to compile new drivers or try another version of the driver. In my case, I would have to re-install the madwifi drivers and the Nvidia drivers to be used with the new kernel that was just compiled.

Sounds messy.  Anyway the will is there.  But the question is the feasibility.  The past day or so I have been reading about the multitude of problems with this version of Ubuntu (8.04).  There are many that complain about getting things that were not working to work and then a simple update blasted everything out of whack.  So I don't want to go through what may be a very long process and then all my work gets whacked out by an update.

> Also it is **NOT** a guarantee that after you compile your kernel that it will even work! The driver could not compile or the kernel could fail to boot. There is a number of things that could go wrong, but honestly if you're desperate to get sound, compiling a kernel is the way to go.

This is what I was worried about.  I have a funny feeling I have more than one problem going on here and even if it did work with one problem, who is to say that trying to fix the next problem will put things out of whack again.  So far that seems to be happening a lot with sound issues in Ubuntu.

> I followed Amtam's first version of his guide and it worked for ubuntu 7.10 and I also tried his guide a few months ago when he tried to patch the driver the first time; it didn't work for me. I have not put much effort into it because I'm too laze to take the time to compile a kernel and play around with these stupid drivers from Creative. When I can use a ***almost*** as good driver from a free source and also support an organization directed at free usage, then I will. It's also allot faster and easier. :rolleyes:

So I see even you are hesitant to compile unless you are more sure of the driver you are going to use.  So from the voice of your experience, it does sound like this compiling thing is not a good idea.

Given the situation that my sound card is partially supported AND the Hardy version of Ubuntu appears to be buggy, I think I am probably going to head down one of these paths:

1) Put Ubuntu aside for now, go back to Windows until a more stable version of Hardy comes out.
2) As others have advised me...install an older version of Ubuntu known to be good and stable.  That would be version 7.10 (Gutsy).
3) Install a USB sound device known to work with Linux and ALSA.
4) Abandon Ubuntu altogether for something more geared for audio production such as JackLab for Open SuSE or Musix.

Now obviously the first choice is the easiest.  Just wait.  Perhaps both a fix for the X-Fi and Ubuntu would be released in time.  The downside is that I don't get to play around with Ubuntu.  I just go back to my Windows applications and wait. 

The 2nd choice would have me delete everything I have on my Ubuntu hard drive right now and start everything over from scratch using an older version of Ubuntu.  The problem I have with this is obvious...I would be moving backward and not forward.  The upside is that I don't have to wait and I can play around in Ubuntu.  This is probably the least likely choice I would go with.

The 3rd choice, to find a USB External Sound device that is compliant with Linux/ALSA is also a good choice because I can leave my X-Fi alone for Windows and then just plug in the external sound device for when I use Ubuntu.   This seems like a an easy alternative to #2 and I don't have to wait as with #1, but I can see it being a pain in the neck constantly having to plug and unplug my speakers to switch sound devices when switching operating systems.

The 4th choice is kind of like the 2nd choice in which I have to go through all the work of uninstalling Ubuntu Studio and trying out one of the other distributions.  However it has merit in that I would be moving forward and not back.

I think overall I would give it a go in this order, but I am open to everyone's opinions on what I should do.

I would choose #3 first, #1 next, #4, next, and #2 last.


There is a 5th choice that I didn't take into consideration until a few moments ago.

Perhaps I could pull out both my Alesis IO-26 (a $400 sound device) AND my X-Fi (a now $150 device)...Sell them on Ebay and then buy an RME Hammerfall card.  Expensive yes, but with the money I would make from selling the other devices I could end up with something that would work in both Windows AND Ubuntu.  

But this one is a tough call.  It would mean messing around with the sound in Windows as well...which is something I was trying to avoid.

At any rate I am open to any other ideas anyone may have.

Thanx,

Geo

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-06-26
Hi peoples,
I thought I might join in on this thread of problems with creative X-Fi drivers.

Nullhead, when I read through so many posts and I see that you have patiently help and advised many along the way I feel appreciative to you.
Also to Amtam, who again seems to have gone through quite a struggle to get these drivers into some sort of working condition.

Like you Jukingeo, I too am very new to Linux, having only installed ubuntu in the last week. I tested it first in VMWare running in Vista Home Premium on my DELL Demension thingy. It worked pretty good, I was impressed, even the sound worked.

Yes, the sound worked, coming out of my Creative X-Fi card that I specifically ordered to have installed in the DELL desktop.

NullHead, I know you wont like me saying this, but I am a Creative fan. I do sound recording and wanted an economical portable unit to record with, so the Nomad Jukebox and Jukebox 3 seemed a great deal. With top audio specs for a consumer product. 98 dB S/N. So, yes, I do like the hardware, even my ZEN 40G Extra is totally lovable. But, alas, I do agree with you in that Creative are lacking in the driver and general support department. And like you may already know, it is not that they don't have good programmers, it's a management problem. Take a look at recent troubles with one clever cookie, Daniel K, who on his own wrote drivers for the X-Fi card for MS XP/Vista better than Creative's if in deed Creative had a driver at all. I know I had many problems with getting to understand and get the correct X-Fi driver installed so that I could get Vista to install SP1!!!

I digress... but I am sure you guys understand the situation and how frustrating it has been and still is to at least get a simple method or procedure to having our X-Fi cards making sound.

Then I downloaded the LiveCd and ran that for a few minutes before my eagerness urged my in hitting the install icon on the desktop. And now Vista has had to make room and share the hard drive with ubuntu Hardy.

So all seems to be real good, even my ATI drivers, but no sound at all, in fact a fat red cross on my volume icon!!

Ubuntu knows that there is the creative card there, it just chooses not to want to use it for lack of suitable driver. I did something suggested I think on this thread to prove that the card existed and was at least seen by the Hardy one.

So, then I downloaded from creative the latest driver, which is XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18.tar.gz sitting nicely in a folder awaiting installation.

This is where I need help kind peoples.
There is the install file, which when I take a peep at it, seems to be a script file. When I say do a sudo ./installer it eventually pops out an error message as follows:

Installation is in progress. Please wait...
checking for gcc... gcc
checking for C compiler default output file name... configure: error: C compiler cannot create executables
See `config.log' for more details.
make: *** [all] Error 2
make: *** [install] Error 2
Installation Unsuccessful
paul@paul-desktop:~/Desktop/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18$

Where does 'config.log' live these days??
Do I need to install a compiler or give access to the complier??

Is it that I need to unpack the XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18.tar.bz2 file first?
Well I can open it with File Roller 2.22.3 ok, and when I tell it, actually ask it really nicely to extract the file, it would appear to go through the motions, but when i inspect the folder there is zilch apart from creating a folder called 'driver'!

So, then I spend more time reading the forums which is educational but time wasting. It is now nearly 1:50 in the morning and sleep would be good as it was later than this last night.

Like you Jukingeo, I too am new to this, but with a solid background in programming microcontrollers through to high level DB apps and industrial PLC/SCADA stuff I am seeing myself as a babe in the woods with my lack of knowledge with Unix!!

Maybe it's my memory of issuing a Kill or delete command on a SCO system many many years ago, thinking I was deleting the contents of the folder, only to be confronted with the screen streaming of line after line of file deletion successful messages. It was a large bakery who's complete operational system I had just managed to wipe. It too was a long night! Another story of course, and have stayed away from Unix based systems till now.

I like my ubuntu install, and I do want know a sure and safe and understandable method by which I can get this card to work?

Amtam, have you used the latest driver from creative??
And what is my next step??


I do appreciate any advice, any at all.
King regards,
mountain goat [http://ubuntuforums.org/images/icons/icon6.gif(oh](http://ubuntuforums.org/images/icons/icon6.gif(oh) no, not the next version of ubuntu!!)

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-06-26
> **mountain_goat said:**
> Hi peoples,

Like you Jukingeo, I too am very new to Linux, having only installed ubuntu in the last week. I tested it first in VMWare running in Vista Home Premium on my DELL Demension thingy. It worked pretty good, I was impressed, even the sound worked.

Yes, the sound worked, coming out of my Creative X-Fi card that I specifically ordered to have installed in the DELL desktop.

Hello MG,

The X-Fi has a VERY impressive spec reference for a consumer product.  It is a very good all around piece for casual listening as well as intermediate to semi-pro audio editing work.  Given the on-board Soundfont synth, 7.1 channel output and surround sound...well it is a great card.

I don't have Vista and nor do I want it (as of now).  I have had many problems with Windows after version 98.  But then XP came along and finally Microsoft got it right.  However there are other issues that bothered me as well.  The constant updates (bloating up the Windows folder on my hard drive), the constant need for virus protection and worse...spyware.  So I got sick of it.  I did some research a while back with Linux and thought it would be good to investigate further, but I didn't pay it too much mind until Ubuntu came out.  That caught my attention.  So when Vista was released that prompted me to take the dive into Linux.

My first impressions were good and for the most part I was happy to see that Ubuntu (GNome) did have somewhat a similar operation.  But it was the problem with the sound that I noticed right off the bat.  When I saw that the Linux drivers for the X-Fi were only in a Beta state, I made the generalization that Linux (or at least Ubuntu) is way behind Windows and is chasing it's tale, after all, the X-Fi has been out for quite a while now and it is gaining popularity as much so as the famed LIVE card.  But then I found out later that it was Creative creating the issues in their lackluster desire to finish the drivers for Linux.  But in one aspect, they are not being paid, so why rush?  It just leaves us here with X-Fi cards twiddling our thumbs.  

Now I could use the Soundblaster Live, which is ALSA supported, but it really would only be for general use and an output source to see how the Ubuntu Studio applications work.   However, the Soundblaster Live isn't even 24 bit.  So I know I couldn't do any serious recording work with it.

Now...Say ello to mah lil' friend:

[http://www.sonicftp.com/news/images/alesis_io26.jpg](http://www.sonicftp.com/news/images/alesis_io26.jpg)

Pretty sweet, huh?  That is my OTHER sound device, the Alesis IO-26.  This is a professional recording piece at 24bit 192khz. 8 ins 8 outs.  Works great in Windows...but Ubuntu?  Nada, Iksnay.  No drivers for it.

Sure there are the RME Hammerfall cards that are supposedly Linux compliant...but they cost upwards of $500 and they don't have anywhere near the nice desktop interface like my Alesis unit does.

> I digress... but I am sure you guys understand the situation and how frustrating it has been and still is to at least get a simple method or procedure to having our X-Fi cards making sound.

It is downright awful.  Either you make a go with it and attempt to use the Creative Beta drivers for ALSA and do ALOT of praying, or you use OSS and get 'some' sound.  Naturally that isn't good enough.  Sure I have sound with my games & mp3's, but no internet sound and nothing works in Ubuntu Studio.

> So all seems to be real good, even my ATI drivers, but no sound at all, in fact a fat red cross on my volume icon!!

The OSS driver does work...but not with everything.  Most of the really nice Linux applications such as Ardour use Jack Rack...and that is mainly set up for ALSA.  Supposedly there is a "wrapper" or overlay I can use to interface Jack Rack with OSS, but I yet to learn how to do it, or worse, I don't know how it will affect what sound I have currently.

> Ubuntu knows that there is the creative card there, it just chooses not to want to use it for lack of suitable driver. I did something suggested I think on this thread to prove that the card existed and was at least seen by the Hardy one.

Yeah, I was told the same thing and there is a Terminal command that you execute and it will return all the hardware installed on your system.  But as you said, knowing it is there is only half the battle.  Without a good driver for it, it is useless.

So I see you are going to attempt the compiling?

> Like you Jukingeo, I too am new to this, but with a solid background in programming microcontrollers through to high level DB apps and industrial PLC/SCADA stuff I am seeing myself as a babe in the woods with my lack of knowledge with Unix!!

Well, you have several up on me in the programming department and probably would be more successful at it.  But the truth be told that even compiling the kernel yourself is no guarantee (as I been told).  So for me, I am not sure if it is worth the trouble.   Thus I am looking into a Linux/ALSA compliant external sound card (because I don't want to muck with my Windows settings of the X-Fi card.  I had a beeotch of a time getting this card installed properly in Windows and now it is running perfectly in that OS.  So I don't want to touch it.

> Maybe it's my memory of issuing a Kill or delete command on a SCO system many many years ago, thinking I was deleting the contents of the folder, only to be confronted with the screen streaming of line after line of file deletion successful messages. It was a large bakery who's complete operational system I had just managed to wipe. It too was a long night! Another story of course, and have stayed away from Unix based systems till now.

Ahhhh, sounds like one of the dreaded SUDO -rm commands.  Luckily I learned a simple die hard and valuable rule early on when it comes to the Terminal. WATCH OUT FOR -RM!  I guess that is the Linux equivalent to Windows/Dos' FORMAT C:.

> I like my ubuntu install, and I do want know a sure and safe and understandable method by which I can get this card to work?

Oh I like it too.  But there are quite a few issues with the Hardy version to begin with.  So I am only a month on it.  If I found another distro that I would have an easier time in setting up my sound, then that is the way I would go.  Already I been looking into Open SuSE's JackLab and a new Linux distro called Musix.  The guestion does remain though that the problem with the X-Fi is across the Linux board.  So I probably will have problems, but I guess the difference would be in how fast could I get those problems rectified.

I don't know, it is a tough call.  I probably will wait until a more stable version of Hardy is released and or better ALSA drivers released for the X-Fi.  That is unless those other distros really have an edge over Ubuntu, then I would use either one of those instead.  It could be too that Ubuntu Studio is very new and perhaps all the kinks haven't been worked out of it either.

Perhaps just my timing is off.  I may be ready for Ubuntu and Linux, but maybe it isn't ready for me.

Bottom line is that I do want to find a solution to my problem.  But if there is no clear cut path, then perhaps I may just stick to Windows for a while longer.  ....We will see.

Geo
