### Post by NullHead on 2008-06-26
[FONT="Tahoma"]It sounds as if you two have rather large needs for recording and high quality sound. I'm glad you two found a solution with the X-Fi, but the fact of the matter is: the Linux drivers for the Creative X-Fi sound card just aren't mature enough for usage like you two are asking for. You two would probably get the best results using ubuntu **7.10** and the Creative driver. Yes it's the older version of ubuntu, but the nice thing is that you don't need to compile a new kernel to use it! Here is my simple instructions for installing the Creative driver in Ubuntu 7.10, Gutsy: 
[SIZE="5"][CENTER]***Installation***[/CENTER][/SIZE]

Start off by installing the files needed to compile the Creative driver. To achieve this feat, run this command witch will install the files needed: ```
sudo apt-get install build-essential
```Then download the Creative driver, move to the driver's directory and run a "sudo ./install ... it's been a long time since I've done this on a gutsy install, so this might be incorrect. Give it a try anyways. I'm expecting you to do the work of extracting and navigation by your self ;) [/FONT]

---

> Jukingeo, wow, that was a quick reply. I'm with you totally.

> That sure is a cute looking piece of kit, the Alesis IO-26, I only wish my Roland BR8 would hook in with FireWire or USB..

Cute??? The thing is a beast.  I researched it for a LONG time.  However, now that I have a good sound device, the Windows programs I like are far too expensive and while there may be alternatives here in Linux, Linux doesn't support that piece at all.

> Now that you mention it, about the SB Live, I have something in my bottom draw here, what is it, ah ha a Sound Blaster USB 24bit 7.1 do hicky.
I might give that a go in the new day.

I am not 100% sure that piece is fully compatible with Linux, but it was made around the time of Live and Audigy so more then likely it is based on the same chipset.  So there is a good chance that it is supported.  However the question remains if that piece is truly 24 bit or not.  That was a reason why I didn't jump for the Audigy when it first came out.  I held out a bit more and then I hit pay dirt with the X-Fi. 

So I really would love to get this card going because a) I don't have to mess with my Windows settings and b) I would have a sound device that works in both Windows and Linux.  So in that aspect damn Creative for not getting us working Linux drivers.  But they are a business, so whatcha gonna do. The paying OS people have to come first.

> ubuntu Studio??? Sounds intriguing and I am interested, as I use Audition in Windows and will be looking for some audio program to do editing in.

Yup!  I am surprised you haven't heard of it yet.  I heard about it in my first week with Hardy loaded on.  It was recommended to me because I did mention right off the bat what I wanted to use Ubuntu for.

Just to whet your appetite this is what Ubuntu Studio offers (I am only going to list the popular stuff):

Ardour, Audacity, Beast Freebirth, Genpo, Hydrogen, Jack Rack, Patchage, Qsynth, RoseGarden, Superlooper, Terminator X.

That isn't even half the applications for audio.  

For video I have:

Kino, Open Movie Editor, Cinelerra, and Stopmotion.


Oh! Almost forgot.  Ubuntu Studio uses a different kernel than regular Ubuntu.  The kernel is designed to run in real time...hence it is referred to as the RT kernel.


At any rate I do want to get something done with my sound because just looking at all these cool apps and not being able to do anything with them is making me foam at the mouth!  Talk about torture!

Geo

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-06-27
Hi peoples,
Ah, another quite evening contemplating......

Nullhead
> It sounds as if you two have rather large needs for recording and high quality sound. I'm glad you two found a solution with the X-Fi


I wouldn't say I have a large need, just a simple need to get this great X-Fi card to perform its function. I do play around with software synths and would like to do more with surround soundscape creations. I wouldn't called this a large need. Just take a look at what video cards are capable of, in terms of blazing graphics capabilities for serious gaming apps. It seems the soundcard is the one that has been left on the shelf unattended and alone for too long now.

I haven't got any solution as yet, I think Jukingeo has something with OSS that he may not be entirely happy with though

Yes, I agree with what Jukingeo is saying
> That is the end goal...yes, but for now, I will take ANY sound I can get. I just want to get my streaming audio back and use those Ubuntu sound applications.


It should really be a simple task, surely for creative to write a decent driver. I don't take it that it comes down to money and that why should they support or even produce drivers for a OS where they wont make money on it. Well, for me, it is very easy to see the effects of not providing complete product support. People will become disenchanted and even resentful towards such a company, vowing not to have any more to do with such a company. This is not the answer kind peoples, not at all. I know it would seem an impossible task for any individual to have any sort of convincing with creative to write their drivers, weather it be for XP, Vista or Linux based systems. They only shoot themselves in the foot if they don't, and I don't understand why such a company such as creative's management people don't understand this from a business point of view.
Surely, with creative's knowledge and understanding of the hardware, in this case the X-Fi card, they are in the best seat to develop the software drivers for it. And, you know what, for the kudos, it would cost them nothing in real terms. A small team for a few weeks to sort out for once and all a fully tested driver that can be deployed into the Linux/whatever flavour system. Even if they say, hey guys, we've made this driver that works in your OS, and we want to give it to you to maintain for that point on. Such a greater gift creative could not exceed at this point in time I believe.

I'm at exactly the same point as Jukingeo, can go backwards, but very hesitant to do this, as I know what this means for the future. I don't really what to move to other systems either. I'm a believer in the ubuntu philosophy and what it stands for, (as I understand it). I would rather slog it out some way. Maybe the talented Daniel K has written drivers for Linux too, I will try to find out. I know there are many many clued up peoples here in ubuntu land that would certainly posses the knowledge to get existing creative drivers to operate in the Hardy one.

It would seem that I don't want to give up or to at this moment, to take an easier but retrograde path to getting sound from my X-Fi card. It's still so new, and hasn't seen any real work at all.

Oh, Jukingeo, that unit I have, the Sound Blaster Audigy 2NX worked first up with providing sound, but it currently doesn't understand the device controls, such as volume, remote control commands and so on. I'm happy that it does see it as an Audigy 2NX though. One small step for me to at least enjoy some sound. It is not the answer that I eventually want.

Amtam - the cheeky one!

Jukingeo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-06-27
> **mnnn said:**
> Temüjin: I have X-Fi for over 1,5 years now ;-( Anyway I use XP till there would be 5.1 support in Linux. Sorry :-)

I am in the same boat as well and if you been following my entries you know what I mean.  I bought my card when it first came out.  At that point in time I wanted something better than the Live sound card.  Audigy looked to be promising was wasn't quite there.  But luckily I heard about the release of the X-Fi and I got it when it first came out.  So it was pretty expensive back then.

As of now, because I got the X-Fi to work so well with everything in Windows, I don't really want to part with it.  It does have a semi-pro spec and in a pinch I could record with it.  However I also have an Alesis IO-26 which is an 8-In 8-Out firewire breakout box.

However, just my luck the Alesis unit is NOT supported in Linux and due to OSS or the Beta ALSA drivers for the X-Fi, it is only partially supported.

Still with the idea of having to juggle around with another internal sound card and possibly messing up my Windows settings getting just getting two channels in and two channels out to work on my X-fi is good enough for me.   As it stands this is my main computer and I don't intend on using it for heavy professional recording work.  But since I am new to Ubuntu (Studio), I wanted to check out the applications and play with them to see if they really can hold a candle to my Window's counterpart programs.

However the difficulty in setting up the X-Fi card is beginning to become unbearable.  It is over a month now I been struggling to get this card fully functional with my Linux games, my game emulators, streaming internet and of course the Ubuntu Studio applications.

With OSS I managed to get to play my mp3's, the system sounds work, and most of my games and console emulators work.  However, the most important thing...the Ubuntu Studio programs are not working and neither is my streaming internet audio.

So that kind of puts me between a rock and hard place.  I it is like should I wait until there is better support for the X-Fi and a more stable version of Hardy is released?  (But who knows, I could be waiting a very long time).  Or should I invest in a cheap external sound card that is fully compatible with Linux and just use that when I am in Ubuntu (this way I don't have to mess with my Windows settings with the X-Fi)?  Should I try a different distribution for audio recording (like Open SuSE's JackLab or Musix)?  Or should I just bite in the sour apple and pull the X-fi card and sell it and my Alesis IO-26 off so I could get one of the nice RME Hammerfall cards which is fully supported by both Windows AND Linux.


So while we may be in the same boat, I don't know what class you want to sail by.  I am shooting for first class, but thusfar it doesn't seem that I am getting anywhere fast with the X-Fi.

So if you want to do quality recording work, thus far the only high end cards that I seen that are supported are the Hammerfall cards.  BUT they are extremely expensive.

If you only need basic sounds for your applications and games, then the OSS driver may be enough to get your X-Fi working.  However, I do warn you that there is a glitch in the new OSS driver release and Hardy in that streaming internet doesn't work.  I know for most people not having audio on the internet is unacceptable (raises hand).

Finally, if you are shooting for a dedicated Linux box AND you are not doing serious audio recording work (but still want to do a bit of it), the perhaps you should sell off your X-Fi and get an older Sound Blaster Live (or Audigy) card.  From what I heard the Sound Blaster Live does have a complete supported Linux driver for ALSA and many people do have a fairly easy time to get get that card to work.

Another alternative is M-Audio.  Some of their cards are supported as well.  But I do have to warn you, making my living as an audio tech pretty much rules out most M-Audio products as pure junk.   For home use, you should be fine. But DO NOT rely on an M-Audio product for pro use.  There are exceptions though.  The Midiman USB midi interfaces are fine.  Get the older ones in the Yellow box, they are better built.

Oh!  Welcome to Ubuntu and the "I can't get my X-Fi to work in Linux" club.   Assume the position like the rest of us ](*,)

### Post by amtam on 2008-06-28
> **jukingeo said:**
> Hello Amtam,

So up basically what you are saying that up to now there isn't a successful case of getting the X-Fi ALSA drivers to work in Hardy?  But yet, there were success cases with getting the drivers to work in Gutsy?


No, I'm not saying that ;)
It **is** possible to get the Creative X-Fi Alsa-driver working in Hardy, but not with the **default** driver. It needs some patches in order for it to work in Hardy. And the Alsa-modules need to be recompiled into the kernel. For some reason they were left out in Hardy while they were still included with Gutsy.

(BTW, you have post #1000, a milestone for this thread! :) )

Hello Amtam,

\\:D/ For getting post #1000

Sadly though that is the only happy news I been getting out of Ubuntu lately.  The more I am digging on these forums the more I am starting to realize the problems with this distribution.

You were not the only one to suggest to go back to Gutsy and recompile the ALSA drivers for my card.

But I am not sure if I am up to that level.  Essentially it is reprogramming Ubuntu's kernel, right?

What would be easier in your opinion?  Should I unload everything and reload an older version and try to recompile the ALSA drivers into the Ubuntu kernel?  Or should I just bite in the sour apple and change my sound card.

Still even with a new sound device I could be looking at problems with this version of Ubuntu, right?   There don't seem to be too many who are thinking highly of Ubuntu Studio right now.

So far the big contender is OpenSuSE's JackLab.  BUT there are a couple issues there as well.  For one I most certainly would have to get a different audio card that is supported with Linux/ALSA as JackLab is also ALSA based.  The next thing is that the support over there is not as good as here.

Yet, there seems to be many many more success stories with being able to just get things going very quickly.   One guy even said that he set up two computers for audio, one with Windows XP and one with JackLab.  He said the full installation took about 7 to 8 hours of his time with Windows and less than two hours to get JackLab running.  He only had a video driver issue with his video card with JackLab.  A quick driver update and he was making music!

Another problem is that OpenSuSE needs a DVD iso burned AND I don't have a DVD burner in my machine.  But that is not a big issue because I want to get a DVD burner soon anyway, so I can make videos.

So right now I don't know what to do.  Should I revert to 7.10 and try a recompile with my present X-Fi sound card?  Should I buy a new sound card that is ALSA compliant?  Should I switch to a different distro altogether.

I am very confused right now.

Geo

### Post by amtam on 2008-06-28
Again, you don't have to recompile if you revert back to Gutsy.
Gutsy = 7.10 :)

This is what Creative has to say about the driver:

> This beta driver has only been tested on the following 32-bit / 64-bit Linux distributions: Ubuntu® 7.10, openSUSE® 10.3 and Red Hat® Enterprise Linux® 5.1 (64-bit only).

Using one of these distro's, the driver will work out of the box.
On Hardy and many others, you will need a recompile and a patched driver.

I really can't comment on the Ubuntu Studio / JackLab apps, because I have no experience with them. Good luck!

### Post by NullHead on 2008-06-28
jukingeo: 

I suggest you downgrade to ubuntu studio 7.10 edition. I'd try downloading it from [here]("http://linux.softpedia.com/progDownload/Ubuntu-Studio-Download-27041.html"). 

According to [this]("http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/ubuntustudio/releases/7.10/release/"), the software on the iso is vulnerable to a [very serious security vulnerability]("http://www.ubuntu.com/usn/usn-612-1"). Make sure to install **all** of the updates after you get it installed. 

Download and install the creative driver after you get everything updated and setup to your liking and let us know how the creative driver works with the rt kernel. 

All that is, of course, if you downgrade to 7.10. For you needs/wants I'd downgrade; for the simple reason of the audio driver.

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-06-28
Hello all again,
Congrats Geo on the 1K milestone, yea!!!
I'm still in the same boat as you my friend, and the boat seems to be small and being tossed around in heavy seas. But I do see land and i do see a way towards the land. When I read what you are saying regarding the possible lack of support on other distro forums and the fact that I like the compactness of ubuntu, I would really like to stick it out with ubuntu. Again, for me, it has a good feeling about it, and I'm learning more and more, albeit small bits of learning :)

I have been digging and digging the various sites, including creative, alsa and reading up on oss, and know that I don't even want to go down the oss path, with all the issues it gives. Alsa for me is definitely the path I am wanting to take.

Now, on the forum site for [creative driver]("http://connect.creativelabs.com/linux/Lists/Driver%20Issues/AllItems.aspx") you can see the troubles people are experiencing. And there is a lot to do with Hardy, and in particular, to do with an error at the make stage.

So then I find on  [Gentoo wiki]("http://gentoo-wiki.com/HARDWARE_Creative_Labs_X-Fi") a method that may shine some candle light on where the problems are in the driver install files.

Amtam, I'm wondering, did you have anything to do with the info on this site??

So then I found out where my first problem was with the driver install, I don't think the LiveCD comes with the gcc (C compiler ver 4.2) so I did a 'sudo  apt-get install build-essential' and that seemed to download files ok. But I haven't as yet gone the next step.

I would like to ask Amtam (the cheeky one) if doing the hack as described on the wiki site will have any chance of being successful on ubuntu 8.04??

Also, on the creative forum site, I do see signs that the creative team are starting to take the driver issue more seriously in recent weeks. For instance, they have made available an updated WIN XP/Vista driver at the beginning of June. And it appears that they may well be taking in the frustration of their customers who have bought their hardware, in terms of keeping informed more frequently. Hopefully, this is not false hope on their part, and that they are really putting in the needed work to get drivers available. Then there is the alsa team, who I think are also getting the picture that they would be much thanked if they can develop and make available the alsa driver.


From the [creative driver forum]("http://forums.creative.com/creativelabs/board/message?board.id=soundblaster&thread.id=31220&view=by_date_ascending&page=117") a user wrote

> PGHammer, I do not agree with your argument. As all the package management systems basically achieve the same goal, it does not really matter which one a specific distribution uses (they are a bit like different archive formats - .rar, .zip, .7z and whatever - you can put files into them and then extract them again). Besides, every distribution supports "manual" installs using shell scripts or makefiles. So, when you have a software that runs under Linux, there shouldn't be a problem with getting it installable on all major distros.

I do not know any details about Linux driver models and whether there are any common standards (I would guess so) or not. But writing Linux drivers cannot be that hard: Firstly, look at the ALSA team, a bunch of volunteers that support hundreds of soundcards without even being employed by their manufacturers. Secondly, look at graphics cards, whose drivers surely are a lot more complicated than those of a soundcard. Both ATI and nVidia manage to produce Linux drivers that work reasonably well on any distribution.

So, in conclusion, there is no reason whatsoever why Creative should not be able to write working drivers within a few months after the release of a new soundcard model - except if they think that "that handful of Linux users out there" just is not worth being supported, in which case the best we can do is sell our X-Fis on eBay and buy replacement cards made by manufacturers that think differently (which I did and recommend to anyone).

Which is pretty well what I was saying a couple of posts ago, that it would not be at all difficult for creative to get it sorted.

So, for me, I have want to look at two options,
1. Use my Audigy 2NX unit which is recognised and does work reasonably ok for the present. And wait for creative and/or alsa to provide tested drivers and installer. This may be a good option for me, to wait and concentrate on my work more than on being challenged by getting sound going. I Know I have allowed my real work to suffer as a result.

2. Go for it and go the hack path knowing that in the end this will give either great satisfaction or frustration, but also better learning and understanding.

Geo, I would encourage you to stay and together have the opportunity to form a small group that will be able to share and support each other on this exciting path. By the way, not sure what sort of music you are into making or what programs you use, but if you are interested in modular synths, have you checked out a program called [AudoMulch ]("http://www.audiomulch.com/")??

I download Audacity yesterday, but at first glance it seems like an early early version of Audition aka old Cool Edit. Will be looking into more of the available sound editing programs over time.
My needs for running under ubuntu are reasonable sound file editor, software synth, additive syth like Native Instruments Absynth ver 3.0,
modular synth like AudioMulch. I have downloaded gAlan and Spiralsynth but they seem to me to be far from useful other than just playing around.

Geo, What are your thoughts?

Amtam, what do you think of the hack path??

As always, I am very grateful to you for your efforts.


mountain goat - grazing on the slopes on the mountain :biggrin:

~~ Hey Amtam having stampot tonight :) ~~

It's not quite the same as the 2.4.24-19-generic from Gutsy, but it's close. You just gonna have to try. There are no guarantees.

> Amtam, I'm wondering, did you have anything to do with the info on this site??

So then I found out where my first problem was with the driver install, I don't think the LiveCD comes with the gcc (C compiler ver 4.2) so I did a 'sudo apt-get install build-essential' and that seemed to download files ok. But I haven't as yet gone the next step.

So it is possible to run Ubuntu 8.04 and Ubuntu Studio 8.04 and the rt-kernel with sound from Creative's ALSA-driver.

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-06-29
> **mountain_goat said:**
> Hello all again,
Congrats Geo on the 1K milestone, yea!!!

Hello again MG.

Yeah, I guess that is one bit of good news. To bad it is irrelevant to solving my situation right now.

> I'm still in the same boat as you my friend, and the boat seems to be small and being tossed around in heavy seas. But I do see land and i do see a way towards the land. When I read what you are saying regarding the possible lack of support on other distro forums and the fact that I like the compactness of ubuntu, I would really like to stick it out with ubuntu. Again, for me, it has a good feeling about it, and I'm learning more and more, albeit small bits of learning :)

Overall, I would say as an OS for general application use, it is hard to beat Ubuntu which is why I thought I made a good choice to go with it...especially since I found out about the Studio variant.  However, that was just an icing on the cake and I really didn't delve into other audio recording applications.  In terms of Hardy, Ubuntu Studio does rank low on the list.  It seems most are favoring OpenSuSE's Jacklab right now, followed closely by Musix and if you have a 64 bit system, 64-Studio.

After being enticed by the initial spiel for JackLab, I signed up to their forum to get more info.  Sadly while in the forum I noticed that it was no where near the amount of action that is going on here.  Also, here you almost get a response right away to your post.  Over there at least a day goes by before you get a response.   Many of the X-Fi issues (posted by previous people) have simply gone unanswered.  So I became very unsure about JackLab because of the support.  However, while I was in the site yesterday I found a link to another forum. Check this out:

[http://linuxmusicians.com/index.php?sid=73a8dc7205c18b744c27527dbf5bf2e5](http://linuxmusicians.com/index.php?sid=73a8dc7205c18b744c27527dbf5bf2e5)

It is a forum for those who make music with Linux.  I was so happy to find this forum through the JackLab forum.  Naturally I found a thread on preferred distributions and low and behold, JackLab seems to get the most action.  So despite a slower support base than Ubuntu Studio, many people like it as a distro to get into when just starting to work with Linux. Supposedly it also has been around a bit longer than JackLab or Musix, so I think the OpenSuSE people may have the edge on audio...for now.

But if one leaves the door open to Ubuntu Studio and they do get all the problems fixed, it could very quickly become the top audio distribution.  Just for now...I don't think so.

I saw some screenshots of 64Studio as well and this has been getting alot of play with those that have 64 bit machines.  Sadly I don't have a 64 bit machine, but the distro looks VERY good.  With my findings they say that it is harder to set up than both JackLab or Ubuntu Studio, but once you get it going, it is very good.  64Studio is also Debian based, so it shouldn't be much different to operate from Ubuntu Studio.

JackLab/OpenSuSE I think are Red Hat distributions...so I believe a different learning curve would be in order.

But in terms of my findings earlier...Ubuntu has a WAY stronger support base.  NO comparison.   So it is a tough decision.  JackLab seems to be a stronger application overall, but with a smaller support base, problems could take a while to resolve.

But in the LinuxMusician's forum, almost everyone is switching over from Ubuntu Studio.  So that should tell you the problems that they are having with the current Ubuntu distribution.  Again, there are those there that were happy with version 7.10, but changed over when Hardy was released.

Needless to say, this isn't a very easy decision to make for me.


> I have been digging and digging the various sites, including creative, alsa and reading up on oss, and know that I don't even want to go down the oss path, with all the issues it gives. Alsa for me is definitely the path I am wanting to take.

OSS is improving, but I think over time support will slow down because just about every new Linux distribution is favoring the ALSA project.  A core program needed to do audio work is Jack Rack and we all know what that runs on.  So for us in audio recording, there is no question, we must get ALSA to work and Jack.

> So then I found out where my first problem was with the driver install, I don't think the LiveCD comes with the gcc (C compiler ver 4.2) so I did a 'sudo  apt-get install build-essential' and that seemed to download files ok. But I haven't as yet gone the next step.

I would like to ask Amtam (the cheeky one) if doing the hack as described on the wiki site will have any chance of being successful on ubuntu 8.04??

I just answered Amtam's post and he says he got it going.  Naturally I am very surprised about that...but if he got the X-Fi to work WITH ALSA on 8.04, naturally I am all ears.  

> Also, on the creative forum site, I do see signs that the creative team are starting to take the driver issue more seriously in recent weeks. For instance, they have made available an updated WIN XP/Vista driver at the beginning of June. And it appears that they may well be taking in the frustration of their customers who have bought their hardware, in terms of keeping informed more frequently. Hopefully, this is not false hope on their part, and that they are really putting in the needed work to get drivers available. Then there is the alsa team, who I think are also getting the picture that they would be much thanked if they can develop and make available the alsa driver.

Yeah, but as you said, that is for Vista.  Despite problems with Vista, there are many that are upgrading.  So if someone wants to buy a sound card in the future and right now the X-Fi is one of Creative' strongest products they very well better put the shoulder to the grindstone to make sure their card works with Vista.  I think the problem boils down to the fact that Creative doesn't realize how many people are thinking about, or already have made the switch to Linux.  They are unfortunately not taking the Linux OS seriously and they are effectively cutting themselves off of a good market share of purchases.

If they had Linux drivers for their CURRENTLY popular X-Fi card, they would have many more people buying it. 

I see it time and time again that there are those that have an X-Fi card or want to buy one because their current card will not work in Linux.  However, they are warned not to buy the card because of the support.

So that is a shame on Creative's part.  

> 

So, for me, I have want to look at two options,
1. Use my Audigy 2NX unit which is recognised and does work reasonably ok for the present. And wait for creative and/or alsa to provide tested drivers and installer. This may be a good option for me, to wait and concentrate on my work more than on being challenged by getting sound going. I Know I have allowed my real work to suffer as a result.

2. Go for it and go the hack path knowing that in the end this will give either great satisfaction or frustration, but also better learning and understanding.

Well, you have one thing going for me that I don't.  You have a card already that will work.  So to me that is a simpler option.

Up to this week I have been VERY against switching out my X-Fi card because I was afraid it would muck up my sound in Windows.  However, I did find out that I could reconfigure my pro-audio device (an Alesis IO-26) to work with my Window's sound).  So now I am not so hesitant on pulling out the card and buying something that is Linux compatible.  I have already looked into the M-Audio Delta 44.  It isn't the greatest card, but it has 4 ins and 4 outs and the connections are balanced so it would at least allow me to try out the Linux applications as it is fully ALSA compliant.

If I really really like the Linux applications and they will do what I want, then when I build a new computer, it will be fully Linux based and then I can splurge for an RME Hammerfall card.

I also hear that the Echo line of cards are also supported by Linux.  If you use FreeBob, you can also use the Firewire Echo audio devices as well.

I could kick myself in the a$$ now because prior to the Alesis IO-26 purchase I made, Echo was one product line I did check out.


> Geo, I would encourage you to stay and together have the opportunity to form a small group that will be able to share and support each other on this exciting path. By the way, not sure what sort of music you are into making or what programs you use, but if you are interested in modular synths, have you checked out a program called [AudoMulch ]("http://www.audiomulch.com/")??

Well, I would like to stay, but JackLab is looking pretty good right now.  But my goal is to get the Linux sound applications to work and Jack Rack to work...with sound.  So that is my priority.  If I have to switch OS's then so be it.

At any rate, in terms of music I mixed...well, years ago I had a full hardware studio (no computers) in my parent's basement and I mostly did remix work.  I mainly worked with editing music for dance studios and I also set up live audio work for their recitals.  Back then I mostly had analog equipment, but I did spring for a digital rig and spent alot of money on it.  Back then the best audio recording frequency was 48k.  I didn't like it.  The sound wasn't quite right.  My clients noticed too and my clients noticed too and it cost me my business.  It took me a VERY long time to figure it out and the problem was the sample rate.  With a very high sample rate and tube processing post digital processing, I could get that sound that I missed.  However, I let 96k pass me up as it was better, but not quite there.  However, the new 192k systems are fantastic.  So this time around I want to go all computer.  Saves me a bunch in buying hardware.  If I can go Linux then I would save even more without having to pay the high cost for applications.  

At any rate,  I did go to school for audio recording, so I know how a studio operates.  I just chose (at the time) not to deal with recording, but only remixing.  I am still going to go the remix path, but I do want the capability to do live recording work as well.

Yes, I am very much into old equipment and I do tend to fancy the old Synths.  However, I also find there are now finally good emulations of all those nice old pieces of equipment for the computer.  Sure they are not fully the same thing, but they get close.

I do love old keyboard instruments though and my all time favorite instrument is the pipe organ.  Yeah, I think BIG.  But there is no other instrument that can make my hairs stand on end like a pipe organ can.  Next in line is another organ...the Hammond B3.

I do have Native Instruments B4 program and I love it.  It is VERY close to the real thing and I have tried quite a few hardware Hammond clones before.  They are getting close too, but the B4 program is still my favorite.  What more is that you can completely midify B4 and effectively take a set of manuals and drawbars from a busted up old Hammond and make your own organ controller for B4.  Cool huh?  Sure it isn't a real B3...but it doesn't weigh 425lbs either.

> I download Audacity yesterday, but at first glance it seems like an early early version of Audition aka old Cool Edit. Will be looking into more of the available sound editing programs over time.

Audacity does have an open source Windows variant and I did load it on for Windows, but I didn't use it much because it was very much like Soundforge (which I already have on Windows).  So it just sits there quietly.  I been thinking about using it now instead of Soundforge so this way it is at least one Linux program I can get a feel for because of the fact that I have the Windows variant and it is working.


> My needs for running under ubuntu are reasonable sound file editor, software synth, additive syth like Native Instruments Absynth ver 3.0,
modular synth like AudioMulch. I have downloaded gAlan and Spiralsynth but they seem to me to be far from useful other than just playing around.

Geo, What are your thoughts?

I have not been playing around too much with Windows based soft synths because I have Reason.  Reason has samplers and softsynths all in a nice package.  Plus it uses ReWire, so it can patch into Ableton Live.

For the most part Ableton Live and Reason are my favorite sound creation programs in Windows, with Soundforge for physical sound editing.  So naturally if I can find Linux counterparts for these programs I am a go for being mostly on Linux.

I have heard of Absynth though and I did look into it a bit.  It does seem like a popular program.  If you don't have Reason already, I would go for it.

Have you ever messed with Antares' Kantos program?  Oh boy!  That one is really cool.  It is kind of a vocoder type program and it does the Cher thing with your voice as well as the Daft Punk type sound.  It is more or less a plug-in rather than a full application, but it is way cool.  I still haven't fully figured it out though.

So that is my scoop.

So hopefully we can 'weather' this storm and pull our little boat through and a solution presents itself.

Anyway, keep your ear to the ground and let us know if you find out more.

Thanx,

Geo

### Post by amtam on 2008-06-30
jukingeo and mountain_goat, if you want to run Alsa on 8.04, just use this [howto](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675). It may seem rather long and perhaps difficult, but in fact the only thing it does is make the patched X-Fi driver from Creative available and recompile the Alsa-modules back into the kernel. That kernel can also be the Real Time Kernel from Ubuntu Studio 8.04, I already tried that and it works. Just make sure you use the .config from the rt-kernel as a template for the recompile.

You're just gonna have to try.

---

### Post by mnnn on 2008-06-30
amtam: Hey, but in your howto is said to compile ALSA as modules, not into kernel? What's right then?

---

### Post by amtam on 2008-06-30
mnnn, you're right, it's a little confusing. Alsa must be compiled as modules and not directly into the kernel itself.

I'm thinking there must be an easier way to just compile the modules and not the kernel itself. That would make things a lot simpler. Something like building the vanilla nvidia-driver / module. That also leaves the kernel itself untouched, but every time you change kernels you have to recompile the driver.

Still a bit of work, but a lot less and a lot faster too than recompiling the kernel itself (with attached Alsa-modules).

So if someone knows of an easy way to to this, it would be great! :D

---

Hello MG,

I looked over Amtam's guide and I see that it is directed towards an AMD64 based system.  As I mentioned earlier, I have an Intel x86 based machine.  So why am I here?  Well lets just say this is where the Ubuntu Forum search engine dropped me and I came along for the ride.  I realized where I was about a week ago but I didn't find anywhere near this elaborate of a discussion on the X-Fi then here under the AMD64 thread.  The funny thing is that MOST of the solutions here worked on my system as well.  But Amtam's guide looks clearly aimed at the AMD64, so I am not in a position to start the procedure without clearing it with him that if indeed it will work or not, and if it will not work, if there is a work around for my system.

As for what I been doing in the meantime...Yes, I have signed up for both the JackLab forum and also the LinuxMusicians forum.   I found LinuxMusicians through Jacklab and I figured I would go there first.  I wanted to talk to those who are using Linux for recording work and what it is they prefer.

I didn't get too many answers yet, but one fellow introduced me to 64Studio and I been checking that out as well.  Yes they do make a 32 bit variant and I was told that because it is Debian based the learning curve will possibly be easier than with JackLab.  But clearly many are in agreement that both JackLab and 64Studio are better than Ubuntu Studio.  So I may still make the switch.

I also found out that JackLab is based on OpenSuSE 10.2...so the ALSA driver for the X-Fi will not work in JackLab (nor 64 Studio).

So switching over to a different distribution will NOT solve my problem.

So as of last night I did make the decision that if I don't get the X-Fi working here in Ubuntu that I am going to pull it.

In that manner with a new Linux supported card, I can at least set up Jack here in Hardy and try out some of the applications.  If I like what I see I can always change distributions later (because I would have a supported card).

The more I look at it, the more I am now seeing the X-Fi as a cage holding me back from exploring and moving forward with Linux.

I already have been looking at cards by M-Audio as well as Echo.

Echo does have some cool firewire cards which work to 192khz, they are supported in Linux, but via FreeBob and not ALSA, so I am not sure how FreeBob will work with Jack.  So I have to double check that.


Amtam...Would you know the answer to that question?  Does FreeBob work with Jack?

Geo

> **jukingeo said:**
> Hello Amtam,

I do believe I mentioned it earlier, but I do NOT have an AMD64 machine.  I have a 32 bit Intel machine.  I ended up here by via a search on the forum.  It seems like all talk of the X-Fi ends up here.  So I came along for the ride.

At any rate I did look over your guide and it seems like you are specifically aiming towards the AMD64 system.  So I am guessing that more than likely it will not work on my system.

So I have two questions 

1) Will it work, or are there simple changes that can be made so it will work on a 32 bit Intel system?

Yes, it will work for Intel and 32-bits too. The howto was originaly intended for 64-bit systems only (both Intel and AMD) because the first driver from Creative was 64-bit only. But that has changed with the latest driver. BTW, 'AMD64' doesn't mean you have to have a AMD (x64) CPU. It's just the 64-bit extensions that are called that way.
AMD64 is basically the same as EM64T, the Intel-version.
My system has a Intel Core2Duo.

> 
2) Does OSS have to be removed in some way, shape or form before performing any of those tasks?  I would worry that a prior OSS installation would interfere with the procedure.

Not really sure, because I based the howto on a clean install.

> Amtam...Would you know the answer to that question? Does FreeBob work with Jack?

Oh!  I answered my own question there.  Since I had the errors with Jack I decided to go into Synaptic and do a reinstall just for the hell of it.  Ok, so now I do still get an error message but I was able to go into Jack Control's setup and switch it off of the OSS driver.   While I was there I looked at all the selections on the drop down list and FreeBob is listed.  If it works, I don't know.  OSS is on the list and we saw what happened with that.


[QUOTE]Here is a screenshot I just made running Audacious with the Jack-plugin activated and several other, euh... Jack-things... To keep you motivated.... ;)

Nice 'pretty' destkop you got there.

Well, I am willing to give it a go, but I have to get rid of OSS first because I don't want it to conflict.


EDIT:

Ok, I did a bit of digging and this command will remove OSS:

sudo dpkg --remove --force-depends --force-remove-reinstreq oss-linux

As of now my sound desktop icon is gone and I have NO sound.  In Synaptic the only thing that is OSS that is left behind is the ALSA-OSS driver...so I believe that should be OK.

Well, it is dinner time now but after dinner I am going to start to get to work on this.  I am crossing my fingers that it works.  If not then I am going just have to buy a Linux supported card.

I will be back tomorrow with a report (unless I run into problems tonight).


Thanx,

Geo

The .config file you need is in /boot. You can use the command that amtam gave in his guide. Don't worry, it should work with out a hitch. Use ```
cp /boot/config-'uname -r' ./.config
```to copy the config and continue from the next step in amtam's guide. I'm away from my desktop computer right now, so I can't get around to testing amtam's guide. 

This process is similar to what we used to do for ubuntu 7.10, gutsy gibbon. I am familiar with this process though. Don't worry, I'll check in as much as I can to help you. 

If you're familiar with IRC, you can join #creative and, if I'm at the computer, I can help you. There will be other people in there that can help you in realtime; instant chatting. 
> **FlyingWV said:**
> I could be mistaken, but I just copied and pasted the command

cp /boot/config-`uname -r` ./.config

And it copied over the correct config file. In the process of rebuiling the kernel right now.

You are correct.

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-01
Hello Geo and all,
I came then went and now back again. Call me yo yo!
OK, so last night I followed Amatam's instructions down to the bone, simply by copying and pasting each line of code.
I kept a text file log in GEdit which I kept saving to my Vista partition which ubuntu can see ok.
I did this as I was fearful something would break along the way, and so I wanted to keep a record so I could backtrack and work out the problem.
But no problems at all with Amtam's How To at all, (with as far as the new kernel patch install part).
I did get a little confused where he says check the alsa lines of code, but I then realised this was in the config editing section.
Here is a copy of the command and then a copy of my my settings making sure I set the same as how Amtam suggested.
The menuconfig command brings up a basic window interface for editing your config.

root@paul-desktop:/usr/src/linux# cp /boot/config-`uname -r` ./.config
root@paul-desktop:/usr/src/linux# make menuconfig
""------------------------------------------------------------------------
""
"" .config - Linux Kernel v2.6.24.3 Configuration
""
"" Geo, goto the section headed sound where you will see alsa and oss
"" select the alsa and list below will be available to edit
""
""
<M> Advanced Linux Sound Architecture
<M>   Sequencer support
<M>   Sequencer dummy client
<M>   OSS Mixer API
<M>   OSS PCM (digital audio) API

[*]   OSS PCM (digital audio) API - Include plugin system (NEW)

[*]   OSS Sequencer API
<M>   RTC Timer support

[*]   Use RTC as default sequencer timer (NEW)
[ ]   Dynamic device file minor numbers (NEW)
[ ]   Support old ALSA API

[*]   Verbose procfs contents (NEW)
[ ]   Verbose printk (NEW)
[ ]   Debug (NEW)
      Generic devices  --->
      ISA devices  --->
      PCI devices  --->
      SPI devices  --->
      USB devices  --->
      PCMCIA devices  --->
      System on Chip audio support  --->
""
""------------------------------------------------------------------------

At the end where you need to check the grub file, just go to /boot/grub and view "menu.lst" file and check in there that you do in fact have an additional jump entry, and that it has the same kernel number as what you have just compiled. Then do the shutdown sequence and grub should allow you to select the new install.

My white screen is a known issue, especially with ATI cards. I have done a little bit of a fiddle, but haven't as yet had the chance, time wise to nut it out.
I am currently back on the previous install, which initially also did come up white, but I selected gnome failsafe on the login screen.
Check at the bottom left of your screen where it says options, select, I think, new session. I will need to recheck that.
I just re-enabled the ATI drivers in Hardware Drivers under Admin.

But for the new install I still need to fix the white screen issue.
I hope I haven't confused you or anybody in the above??

I will need to fix my white screen issue before I install the next stage of the How To, the X-Fi driver itself.

So, if any of you do know the answer or suggestion to fix this, I would be so grateful.
But I do see lots on the board, but a lot of it does seem to me that people are just trying all sorts of things, some work while others not so well.

Then I will hopefully be able to spend some more time looking into the sound apps and experimenting somewhat
Geo, you like big and heavy and old, I wonder if you have ever had the opportunity to explore and play a Kawai K5000S, it's an additive synth, solid build too!!
For me, it was an instant love affair when I spotted it in the store, secondhand some years back.
It's one of those units where you know there is quality electronics ans low noise op amps in it.
I have spent many many hours late at night creating all sorts of wonderfully rich sound textures that I think are lusciously warm and full bodied.
Though you seem to need a degree in the complexities of FFT to really explore this beautiful beast.
Take a peeps [here]("http://www.vintagesynth.com/index2.html") and [here]("http://www.sonicstate.com/synth/kawai_k5000s.cfm").
For me, it it a truly amazing piece of engineering that I am fortunate to have come to own. It will be a sad day if it ever gives up it graceful ghost :(

Question for Amtam or Nullhead:
The install is a lower number than what I already had, for instance, from the grub automatic kernels list section I now have;
Ubuntu 8.04, kernel 2.6.24.3-custom
Ubuntu 8.04, kernel 2.6.24-19-generic
Ubuntu 8.04, kernel 2.6.24-16-generic

what is the .3 part of the number mean?
what will this mean in terms of the normal upgrade process?
and the next release of ubuntu, the goat thing???
will the patch just travel through the upgrade, or do we need to re-compile at each new release?


OK, so the sun doth shine here at the moment.
Time to graze ~ mountain goat  :grin:

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-01
mountain_goat and FlyingWV: Great, you got it both working! :)
jukingeo: just copy the commands, like Nullhead and FlyingWV said. The howto is 'designed' to be followed to the letter. I found that if you leave too much room for interpretation, people will always do things slightly different, often resulting in failure.

Of course you can copy the driver to an other location than your home-dir, the Desktop for example, but then the next command in the howto won't work anymore and you have to edit it to reflect the right path. Nothing wrong with that, but you have to know what your doing... ;)

A tip for people with screwed up screens and nvidia cards: don't use the restricted driver from the repositories. It won't work anyway with a custom-kernel. Just download the driver directly from nvidia's site and install it manually in recovery-mode using a root-prompt only. Works everytime for me and only takes a few keystrokes. I've done it so many times now I can almost do it with my eyes closed....:cool:

It does it with wav and mp3 encoded files.

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-01
Hello MG,

Wow! Looks like you made some progress last night.  Seems like you have a lot going on there.  I heard about the white screen before as well.  I hope I fair better because I have an nVidia card and that all I will get is bumped to a lower resolution, then I can still see what I am doing and can install the video driver.

At any rate, let us know how you got around the white screen problem.


> **mountain_goat said:**
> 
Geo, you like big and heavy and old, I wonder if you have ever had the opportunity to explore and play a Kawai K5000S, it's an additive synth, solid build too!!
For me, it was an instant love affair when I spotted it in the store, secondhand some years back.
It's one of those units where you know there is quality electronics ans low noise op amps in it.
I have spent many many hours late at night creating all sorts of wonderfully rich sound textures that I think are lusciously warm and full bodied.
Though you seem to need a degree in the complexities of FFT to really explore this beautiful beast.
Take a peeps [here]("http://www.vintagesynth.com/index2.html") and [here]("http://www.sonicstate.com/synth/kawai_k5000s.cfm").
For me, it it a truly amazing piece of engineering that I am fortunate to have come to own. It will be a sad day if it ever gives up it graceful ghost :(

Nope...never seen one come into my shop.  Looks like a nice unit though.   Usually I am after units that have sliders on it as well as knobs.  The need for sliders should be obvious as I like organs :).  Anyway, a word of advice if you still can get parts for that unit, stock up.  Get an octave of keys, the key contacts and some of the tact switches and push buttons.  As the unit gets older, parts for it will get harder and harder to come by.  It is sure going to be a bummer if you wear through your keypads and can't redo something that simple.

This is why lately I am being turned off by hardware pieces.  Its getting the parts that is a problem.  I, personally have been favoring computer based synths and using a standard USB keyboard controller.  If my controller goes bad, I just get a new one, and plug it in.  Lately the computer modeling has become very accurate and while the computer isn't still the old analog deal...it is getting VERY close.  Now with the new 64 bit machines, it will even get closer.

But I do still have that old analog bug in me and if I found a Mini-Moog for a good price...I would still buy it.  The Mini-Moog is a fairly easy unit to keep operational.  So you will be good to go for a LONG time with one.  


> OK, so the sun doth shine here at the moment.
Time to graze ~ mountain goat  :grin:

Grazin' in the grass is gas, baby can you dig it!

Sorry...you left yourself open for that one :).

Well, nice to hear you are coming along.  I am going to finish tonight after I watch Hell's Kitchen.  Finale tonight!

Geo

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-01
Geo,
After I did a little digging around about white screen issues I was able to get around somewhat.

White Screen as I experienced it:
The white screen only appears after login on, when it wants to display the desktop. You will be able to login as normal. the white screen is like a whit sheet in front of your screen, the mouse will be visible and will move ok. And I found that by clicking in the area where I thought a menu icon would be, I notice the hard drive go busy. So that told me that the screen was behind the white. In fact I found out via another user that if you ALT CTRL BACKSPACE you will briefly see your normal desktop, before taking you to a command line screen if I remember correctly.

So, while in white screen you can do ALT CTRL F1 through to F7 to bring up an alternate display device, which will be a plain command line interface. There you can do what is needed in terms of issuing commands to get around. But read below for what I did.

Firstly if you find after you finish the install of the kernel patch and reboot like Amtam says to do, that you do get a white screen, then you will at that point simply press the soft power button on the PC to reboot it. Then when it boots up into grub again, take the same new option as before. At the point where you enter your username, you will notice at the bottom left of your screen the "options" icon. Select "failsafe session" as I recall. This will reboot apparently without using what is called a Xclient script file. So straight into plain vanilla system I guess. this is where we have seen some talk about low res screen. Don't worry, I didn't get this. And I expect you wont either, as I think the config has many res options in it. old vga is the last thing I think it will use.

Right, so if all that works for you, it will appear to be a normal system. And this is where you can look into reloading your video driver. Maybe use the synaptic package manager or use FoxyFire to get the driver and install details. ATI pointed to an ATI wiki site that appears to be quite informative to re-install in varying situations.

OK, enjoy your work, heheheh,
Oh it's 11:11 a magic number if you know anything about Mayan Culture??

mountain goat ~ looking at the stars

Audacity has a problem, in that I am getting ticks in the playback sound.
It is directly related to the preference setting for buffer for latency.
When set low, say, 10ms I get buzz, when I set to 500ms, I get tick tick tick.
I think it is some other driver that Audacity is using.
It does it with wav and mp3 encoded files.

Tick Tick Ticky, Tick Tick Ticky. BOOM! Chicka Chicka BOOM! :D

Actually if you got the latter part of that...we have a MAJOR problem.  Hmmmm, I had that problem with sound on video games.  Could be you are out of the range of the audio card? (you are playing a 96k file when the card is doing 48k).  Buffer is always a good thing to play around with.  I do remember when I was on OSS that one particular game console emulator, Gens, gave me an awful lot of trouble.  There were SO many audio settings to play with.  I had to play with them all until I found something that I could deal with.  The Tick Tick Ticky never fully went away, but I did minimize it.  Granted that was fine for a game, but I wouldn't tolerate that for audio recording at all.

Another thing, does the ticking sound come and go?  If so then it could very well be that the computer is trying to do more than one task and if you have everything on one hard drive that could be a problem.  Usually it is a good idea to have the program on one hard drive and a workspace on another hard drive this way the computer can access the drives simultaniously.  If everything is on one drive, what you could be hearing is seeking error because the drive head has to zoom back and forth.  Generally that is what the buffer is there for.

Let us know how you make out with it.  I know I am going to be using Audacity.   

> I have just installed SWF and java and can now play media from YourMightyTube, which is good.
When you get it going, check out [this]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dvTe1-a6Pdo&amp;feature=related").

I hope you peoples like it.

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-01
jukingeo, you can continue at the point where you left of, copying the .config-file.

Just make sure you are in the /usr/src/linux directory like it says in the previous step of the howto. The .config is supposed to end up there.

> Finally, how to get into 'recovery mode using a root prompt'?

At the Grub boot-menu, you can choose recovery-mode. It then asks you if you want to repair a X-config, or boot into a root prompt only. It's selfexplanatory really ;)

I'll try this evening when I return from work.

Where did you get that from?

If you feel up to it, you can try this:

Download this (64-bits) driver: [http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86_64/173.14.09/NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-173.14.09-pkg2.run](http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86_64/173.14.09/NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-173.14.09-pkg2.run)

Boot to recovery-mode, then root-prompt only and type:

```
sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-173.14.09-pkg2.run
```

at the location where you downloaded the driver
Then follow instructions. That's it! :)

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-07-01
> **dadec said:**
> for some reason my intel sound won't blacklist. heres the output of ossinfo...I've tried blacklisting hdaudio, can anyone help? (running hardy heron 32 bit)

dadec, if you're not using your onboard audiio, it is best to disable it in the BIOS, and then run (sudo) soundoff, ossdetect -v, soundon.

You can also remove the OSS driver for it by deleting the appropriate line in /usr/lib/oss/etc/installed_drivers : i.e.
```
gksudo gedit /usr/lib/oss/etc/installed_drivers
```

Also, make sure /dev/dsp points to your X-fi output. Let me know if you need more assistance.

Ok, I got up to this part in your tut:

<<<NOTE: If you have the i386-version of Hardy, the filenames will be slightly different, but you get the idea.
Install them with these commands:

Code:

dpkg -i linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb
dpkg -i linux-headers-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.23.3-custom-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb>>>

The first .deb package for the linux-image was there, the only difference was the extension and obviously for some reason the package was highlighted in red so I found it quickly 

However, the other .deb package for linux-headers was NOT there.

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-07-01
FlyingWV, copy and paste this into a terminal to get the headers for the kernel you're running:
```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-`uname -r`
```

### Post by NullHead on 2008-07-02
To clarify exactly what that mysterious dpkg command does, let me explain it. 

dpkg is a different program to handle programs installed through a .deb file. The xserver-xord was already installed by default when you installed ubuntu. So "dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg" rescans your video display device and puts the monitor and video card settings back to default. It also resets your keyboard, mouse and wacom devices to their defaults too.

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-02
Wow, you guy's really have been busy.... ;)

And it seems you are busy at a time when I'm sleeping, being on the other side of the planet and all... ;)

There's a lot of questions, I'll try and answer (some of) them.

> The first .deb package for the linux-image was there, the only difference was the extension and obviously for some reason 

the package was highlighted in red so I found it quickly 

However, the other .deb package for linux-headers was NOT there.

Are you really sure it wasn't there? Because it should be. And you do need it. The kernel itself is not enough, you need the headers too if you want to compile stuff like the X-Fi driver or the nvidia-driver.

```
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

This is the command in the howto that builds the header-package (the last part). Sure you didn't ommit that?

There's no point in continuing and trying to install the driver if you don't have the right header-files, so make sure you got the command right and check for the package in /usr/src.

> Note: I did drop in every now and then to see how the compiling code was coming along. Every so often this "warning" 

message came up. I don't recall what it said. Is that anything to worry about?

Warnings are more or less 'normal'.

> Another question. Is this new custom kernel real time?

That depends on which kernel you based the recompile on. If you want the rt-kernel, you first have to install it from the repository and then use it's .config as a template for the recompile of the howto. It has several options ticked related to low-latency stuff. If you do a uname -a, it should say PREEMPT at the end for instance. Take a look at the screenshot I made earlier.

About the video-driver issues many people experience: that is to be expected. A recompile just breaks it. All I can say is you have to find a way that suits you best to fix it. I've pointed out several times now how I do it and that works 100% of the time for me. But it's not the only way to do it and you can try others if you want. There's lots of howto's related to video-card issues. But don't be put off if it doesn't work right away; a LOT of people have trouble getting that drivers to run properly... ;)

> Alas, I've uninstalled Ubuntu again. I got the procedure to work, but I noticed something. At least for now, my audio quality is much better with OSS 4.0 than with ALSA/Creative Driver  I'm not sure why that is per se. But most of my players were crackling with ALSA output... and actually sounded good with OSS output, except it would skip at times. That's another thing, my sound would skip whenever I browsed web pages, and such. It didn't do that to me under OSS, hmm.

So for now, going to migrate back to OSS 4.0, and sit on the fence till the next driver release by OSS and ALSA and see which would be better then 

Thanks much for the guide, I DID learn a lot through this process. 

That's OK FlyingWV. No 'hard feelings'...:lolflag:
For me, it's just the other way around: apart from the issue with Audacity, the sound from the X-Fi using the ALSA-driver is crystal-clear in all apps. Does Audacity work OK with the OSS-driver? Let me know.

Yes, the alsa-driver still remains less than stellar at best and I just can't wait until they (Creative or ALSA) release something better.

But then again, I actually LIKE messing around with stuff like this too.... :lolflag:

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-02
> **amtam said:**
> Wow, you guy's really have been busy.... ;)

And it seems you are busy at a time when I'm sleeping, being on the other side of the planet and all... ;)

Well, we just keep toiling away trying to squeeze some sound out of the X-Fi.  So far this is the best lead any of us had!

> 

Are you really sure it wasn't there? Because it should be. And you do need it. The kernel itself is not enough, you need the headers too if you want to compile stuff like the X-Fi driver or the nvidia-driver.

```
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

This is the command in the howto that builds the header-package (the last part). Sure you didn't ommit that?

Absolutely.  I do remember invoking that command because I remembered laughing at the "fakeroot".  I will say that there ARE header files within that same folder.  There just wasn't a .deb package.  Could it be that that command automatically unpackaged the .deb file?

I wish there was a way I can send a screen shot of the file list to you.  

> There's no point in continuing and trying to install the driver if you don't have the right header-files, so make sure you got the command right and check for the package in /usr/src.

Ok, but in the event that it automatically unpackaged itself, what files should I look for?



> Warnings are more or less 'normal'.



That depends on which kernel you based the recompile on. If you want the rt-kernel, you first have to install it from the repository and then use it's .config as a template for the recompile of the howto. It has several options ticked related to low-latency stuff. If you do a uname -a, it should say PREEMPT at the end for instance. Take a look at the screenshot I made earlier.

I don't remember a preempt, but I will say that I started the recompile from within Ubuntu Studio and that has the RT kernel.  The new custom kernel also boots up into Ubuntu Studio.  I will double check with uname -a when I get home tonight.

> About the video-driver issues many people experience: that is to be expected. A recompile just breaks it. All I can say is you have to find a way that suits you best to fix it. I've pointed out several times now how I do it and that works 100% of the time for me. But it's not the only way to do it and you can try others if you want. There's lots of howto's related to video-card issues. But don't be put off if it doesn't work right away; a LOT of people have trouble getting that drivers to run properly... ;)

Well, what I did was go into recovery mode and then when I get the blue screen with the gray select box, I selected the X-server rebuild file first.  It said I was supposed to see flickering and I didn't.  So I clicked on the next item which was the dpkg button.  That sure did something and after it was done, I rebooted and Presto!  I was back up and running.  Thusfar though, the only side effect I see is that my login screen is set to a very very high resolution and the type is tiny.  But after I login the screen resolution is fine.  So I would like to fix the login screen to a resolution I can see.



> That's OK FlyingWV. No 'hard feelings'...:lolflag:
For me, it's just the other way around: apart from the issue with Audacity, the sound from the X-Fi using the ALSA-driver is crystal-clear in all apps. Does Audacity work OK with the OSS-driver? Let me know.

Yes, the alsa-driver still remains less than stellar at best and I just can't wait until they (Creative or ALSA) release something better.

But then again, I actually LIKE messing around with stuff like this too.... :lolflag:

Well I guess I will see how I fare later on.  As of now I still didn't install the X-Fi drivers, so hopefully in the meantime we can straighten this thing out with why that second .deb file didn't appear.  I am wondering if it is an ix86 32 bit thing.

Oh well.  I am going to hope for the best and expect the worst.  I am already preparing to purchase an M-Audio Delta series card if this doesn't work.

But thanx for the trip thusfar, Amtam.  And a thanx to the rest of you guys that also pitched in with help.

Geo

Yeah, guys, I am up...but at work now.  So I can't do anything until 9 hours from now.  So you think Audacity is a toy?  Hmmm, I guess if you haven't used Soundforge before I guess you may have different thoughts on it.  Audacity isn't a DAW, it is an editor.
For example.  Say you record someone and you have one shot at it.  That someone gets too close to the mic and pops his/her p's and t's. Now you have the mess on your recording.  What do you do?  Well whip out Audacity (or in my case Soundforge), and bring up the file.  All the pops would appear as huge spikes on the waveform.  You just can go in and zoom into the pop and highlight it. Then you just deleted it right out!

That is what Audacity can do on a basic level, but I am sure it has it's share of noise filters, normalizing and other effects that you can do to "prepare" your file for a DAW program.  In other words, Audacity "fixes" your files, but Ardour would be used for the actual recording. 

> I may have it all wrong, and I hope I do, but it seems sluggish and clunky to use Audacity. I guess it is lacking in fineness. Sorry to be negative about at the moment though

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-02
Hey Geo and Amtam

Geo> 
So you think Audacity is a toy? Hmmm, I guess if you haven't used Soundforge before I guess you may have different thoughts on it. Audacity isn't a DAW, it is an editor.
For example. Say you record someone and you have one shot at it. That someone gets too close to the mic and pops his/her p's and t's. Now you have the mess on your recording. What do you do? Well whip out Audacity (or in my case Soundforge), and bring up the file. All the pops would appear as huge spikes on the waveform. You just can go in and zoom into the pop and highlight it. Then you just deleted it right out!

That is what Audacity can do on a basic level, but I am sure it has it's share of noise filters, normalizing and other effects that you can do to "prepare" your file for a DAW program. In other words, Audacity "fixes" your files, but Ardour would be used for the actual recording.

Yes, I agree and do understand what yo are saying. And this is where I think comparison between Audition SoundForge and Audacity can be made. I use Audition for any post processing after I have recorded. I can view listen, edit, process, mix down, re-format to master the end section. And as you say being able to clean up audio right down at the sample point for those once in a while spikes or transients is a blessing.

Just playing with Audacity now and noticing a few things that I don't like. There is very little in the way of options. Options to set the application in the way you would like, eg, layout and colour .... Very little option in the way you want the application to process your files.
For example, I am at the moment zoomed right in and when I pick up a sample point and move it, it does so in a non spline curve method, so in a very direct straight line way. Now, you are supposed to be able to smooth out a section by ALT left mouse click, but I can not make it happen. It does have a good selection of mouse and keyboard operators.


_____________________________
> That is what Audacity can do on a basic level, but I am sure it has it's share of noise filters, normalizing and other effects that you can do to "prepare" your file for a DAW program. In other words, Audacity "fixes" your files, but Ardour would be used for the actual recording.

In terms of effects and how you can edit, that is basically down to the plugins you have. I downloaded a good ubuntu scoop full and so Audacity does have many many ways available to mess your sound. So that is good!!

_____________________________
> I had no choice I had to fix my video before I could move on. I am wondering now if you do find a fix for the driver that it may knock out your audio again. I think Amtam does mention this in his tut. So hopefully if you fix your video driver, you will not loose audio.

I noticed when I installed the ati driver, by following instructions on the wiki site for ati driver in ubuntu, the driver was a little stubborn but what did suprise me was to find the creative X-Fi driver gone, yes gone, walk-about totally!!

So I thought that something in the ati driver installation knocked the driver out a bit, as you say, the X-Fi.

To fix this I thought I would go back over Amtam's instructions at the point of the X-Fi install. I carefully re-issued the commands, with some not working, but in the end after re-booting I found that both the ati and creative alsa were back with me, so all is good.

OK, so Audacity uses this thing called "portaudio" and there is a site about it [[COLOR="Blue"]here[/COLOR]]("http://www.portaudio.com/"), and oddly enough if you look you will notice that portaudio was proposed by Ross Bencina who developed that amasingly fantastic sonic program I talked about, Audiomulch!!! What a surprise.. Another great Australian. Ross lives here in Australia. And yes I am on zee little island of van deiman land called Tasmania. Van Deiman was a dutchy, just like Amtam, and my family too. I think Amtam must be somewhere near Den Bosch or Maastricht? My cousin lives in Den Bosch, some fantastic and architecture and art to see there.
Amtam have you ever seen the [[COLOR="Blue"]Pleinmuseum[/COLOR]]("http://www.pleinmuseum.nl/ekunstenaars.html") ??
I saw it in Utrecht and videoed the same as this person did in this [[COLOR="Blue"]youtube video[/COLOR]]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rXkVFstjUjY&feature=related") 

It was totally awesome, the sound from this artist blew me away with it's deep lows. The sound was synced to the video as you can see. Watching it on youtube just does it no justice at all, totally amasing.

Geo, the thing is a foldable piece of kit for showing art based media. It uses pneumatic drives to set most of itself up and uses all sorts of high tech audio and video wizardry and travels around parts of Europe, but is mostly based in the Netherlands I think.

OK, I digress again, I know. So much to explore and to share.
I didn't get a thing done for work today, so maybe tomorrow I will try a little harder to make progress with the program I am working on.
Maybe you should take your computer to work so that you can work on it there hehehe :mrgreen:

### Post by NullHead on 2008-07-02
Wow, you guys are fast! Who ever, I can't remember, is having the problems with not having kernel-headers should boot back into the old kernel, delete the custom kernel in synaptic, delete the .debs from /usr/src/ and re-compile a kernel. You are going to NEED kernel-headers to compile the X-Fi drivers and just about anything else you want to compile. 

In my opinion, OSS is the best way to go for just about everyone needing basic sound in linux using an X-Fi. The creative driver is, also in my opinion, for developers/experienced users. I do not mean that to turn off any of you attempting to use the Creative driver, but I just want to state that, if you fail to get Creative's driver working in 8.04, that would be my explanation for the situation.

As for video drivers, I'd go to [envy's home page]("http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html") and download the newest version of envy-ng. This program will help you setup both nvidia and ati graphics cards very easily. 

The thing is ... on ALL linux distros, when you make a new kernel or install an upstream update from your distro's update system, this will break any drivers you have installed for you old kernel. It is normal that your video drivers don't work in your new kernel. 

As for the dpkg command and the question of it's safety, it is absolutely safe to run! It even makes a backup of your old .config file. As my explanation explains, this rescans your hardware and sets up your config accordingly. The problem with this command is that, it will not put in a proprietary in the config for you. I suggest that you run "sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg" and **then** run envy-ng to get your video drivers working. 

NullHead ~ enjoying his camping trip away from his bothersome X-Fi :lolflag:

---

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-02
> **FlyingWV said:**
> Just reading around... I don't think you're going to want to use OSS geo. OSS doesn't have MIDI support, I don't think. It's been disabled for the time being.

I also doubt I'd be able to get Jack running, after I read your whole thread regarding it with OSS. 

But on the other hand, you should be able to get the X-Fi to work with ALSA. My problem is probably that I didn't spend enough time with ALSA trying to fix the poor quality of sound (config files and maybe conflicting software).

Hello Fly,

OSS?  I just came from that camp.  With OSS I only had HALF the sound on my system.  The X-Fi worked with my games, mp3's, and the system sounds.  It didn't work in Jack (in fact it locked Jack up), no internet either.  OSS has a HUGE problem with streaming internet audio.  There is a huge thread on it.

True, it also doesn't work with Midi.  I was forced to remove a program called Timidity in order to install OSS.

I spent A LOT of time today trying to research other cards as well as external firewire and USB interfaces.

For the most part USB devices are a nightmare.  Even though ALSA says they are supported, I been hearing nightmare after nightmare both here and in the Linux Muscians forum.

Firewire is touch and go.  However, it uses a different system.  You have to use Freebob which IS supported in Jack, but nothing else.  So you cannot use a firewire device to get system sounds, internet streaming or what have you.

In terms of internal sound card support it seems most people have luck with the RME, M-Audio, and Soundblaster Live (or Audigy) internal cards.

I was pretty much set on an M-Audio Delta 44 but just realized that it doesn't have midi support either.  I would have to either go with the audiophile or the mammoth Delta 1010 for that support.  The 1010 is pretty expensive for me right now, but the Audiophile card only supports 2in and 2out.  For me bare minimum is 4in 4 out & midi.  SPDIF is optional, but I would like it.  Another problem with the M-Audio stuff is that none of the inputs are pre-amps...so no mics.  You have buy another thing called an "Audio Buddy" if you want pre-amps.  So by the time you get the 1010 interface and the audio buddy, I am already 3/4 the way there to a Hammerfall card!  Same would be true if I would go with the Delta 44, and get a separate midi interface and then hope it all works together properly.

I been just now came back from looking at some of the Audigy stuff from Creative.  Hopefully something work in that line without costing a fortune.

You know thing that irks me the most is that BOTH firewire & USB have been out for a while...how come the head Linux developers are SO far behind?  Now mind you, that is not with everything.  Video support seems to be OK.  But video is much harder than audio and that seems to be working fine.  Why is it so hard to get a darn sound card to work?  That is what irks me.

I have a funny feeling I am going to run into the same thing with the distorted audio on the X-Fi card ad you have had.  We will see though.

As you can tell I am a bit ticked off that I been on this single sound issue for over a month now and there is no easy solution.

I am going to finish trying to get the X-Fi to work tonight, but if it doesn't work I am not sure what I will do next.

Geo
