I use Audition for any post processing after I have recorded. I can view listen, edit, process, mix down, re-format to master the end section. And as you say being able to clean up audio right down at the sample point for those once in a while spikes or transients is a blessing.


Audacity is really just a tool.  It isn't something you make part of a DAW, but if you have problems with an audio file, chances are you can fix those problems.  Now mind you, when it comes to Windows, I still do prefer Soundforge, so it has been a while since I been in Audacity last.


> In terms of effects and how you can edit, that is basically down to the plugins you have. I downloaded a good ubuntu scoop full and so Audacity does have many many ways available to mess your sound. So that is good!!

Yeah, Audacity isn't an effects processor in that manner.  It is what it is, an audio editor.  But you can bend and shape your file to get a desired effect and then drop that into your DAW.


> So I thought that something in the ati driver installation knocked the driver out a bit, as you say, the X-Fi.

To fix this I thought I would go back over Amtam's instructions at the point of the X-Fi install. I carefully re-issued the commands, with some not working, but in the end after re-booting I found that both the ati and creative alsa were back with me, so all is good.

Yup, I have heard that several times here in Linux that if you don't do the video BEFORE audio, you will knock it out.

Oh! As for the video clip you sent with the 'light art'.  I have seen something similar on a TV program here in the US called "Dancing with the Stars".

Here, take a look:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tOdpMXooBAw&feature=related](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tOdpMXooBAw&feature=related)

Don't pay attention to the dancers while you are watching, but instead focus on the rear panels where the band is.  Pretty cool huh?

I know what the material is, but how to do the projections themselves I still haven't fully figured it out.

Anyway, I will work on this nightmare when I get back home. 

Yeah, while it may seem cool to be able to work on my machine here at work...I would never get anything done!  At any rate my machine is even bigger than the one I have here at work.  It isn't a laptop.  It also has a massive 17" CRT monitor...no flat screen as of yet.  To be honest with you, the picture quality blows any flatscreen away (It is a Sony monitor based on the Wega technology.  It is crystal clear).  There is a lot connected to it...including a multi-speaker sound system and I am not even going to go into the USB stuff and firewire things I have hooked up to it.  It's an octopus!!  So there is no way I am going to move it! :lolflag:

Hafta run.

Geo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-02
> **mountain_goat said:**
> Geo, Amtam and FlyingWV,
I have just come across a program called sweep, ver 0.9.2 which on first glance is what I think Geo and I might be after.
I have loaded up a audio file and it seems the play back is clean.
Have a look [[COLOR="Blue"]here[/COLOR]]("http://www.metadecks.org/software/sweep/") 

mountain goat ~ ZZZzzzzz......

Hmmm, impressive.  Looks nicer than Audacity.  I would almost say this program might be closer in function to Soundforge than Audacity.  Nice to hear you are getting clean audio from it.

You know...you got me to thinking something, MG.  Audacity was around for a long time.  It could be more optimized for OSS.  Before I upgraded to Ubuntu Studio and when I had the old OSS driver on my machine Audacity DID work in Ubuntu and it was clear too...no distortion.

So methinks that Audacity could be more optimized for OSS, Ya!

Well, anyway, when I upgraded to Ubuntu Studio and tried the updated OSS driver, it didn't work anymore.  But since everything in Ubuntu Studio is set up for ALSA, that could be an issue.  Something to look into.

Sweep could be a newer program that could be more optimized for ALSA, so perhaps that is why it works better.

That is just a guess, mind you.

Geo

### Post by FlyingWV on 2008-07-02
Hello all (geo, amtam, mountain goat, nullhead)

Yeah, Audacity works with OSS, from a sound level, if you recompile it from source with the option to force it to use the OSS driver. I noticed if I try to use any effects from menus and then cancel, it locks up the program though. I doubt it would be useful for you, since you need those effects and such.

On another note, I broke down and bought a cheap sound card, until the X-Fi support is better. I haven't installed it yet, but later I plan to take my computer apart and throw it in there, I need to use some compressed air inside there anyways.

I bought (Ugh) a Creative Blaster Audigy SE, as I think the Audigy is supported better in Linux for now. I really wanted an M-Audio Revolution 5.1, but NOWHERE around here carried anything except creative really, plus a cheap $30 card is probably enough for now, I really don't want to replace my $300 card long term :lolflag:

So after I clean my computer out, switch cards, reinstall Ubuntu (for like the 8th time in 3 days), I hope that setup will be a little more seamless and smooth.

Keep up the hard work

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-02
> **FlyingWV said:**
> Hello all (geo, amtam, mountain goat, nullhead)

Yeah, Audacity works with OSS, from a sound level, if you recompile it from source with the option to force it to use the OSS driver. I noticed if I try to use any effects from menus and then cancel, it locks up the program though. I doubt it would be useful for you, since you need those effects and such.

On another note, I broke down and bought a cheap sound card, until the X-Fi support is better. I haven't installed it yet, but later I plan to take my computer apart and throw it in there, I need to use some compressed air inside there anyways.

I bought (Ugh) a Creative Blaster Audigy SE, as I think the Audigy is supported better in Linux for now. I really wanted an M-Audio Revolution 5.1, but NOWHERE around here carried anything except creative really, plus a cheap $30 card is probably enough for now, I really don't want to replace my $300 card long term :lolflag:

So after I clean my computer out, switch cards, reinstall Ubuntu (for like the 8th time in 3 days), I hope that setup will be a little more seamless and smooth.

Keep up the hard work

Hello Fly,

Home now, so I am probably going to try to take off where Amtam said and see if I can get the missing .deb file back.  Since I didn't compile the X-Fi drivers, I should still be good.  So at least that can be fixed.

That isn't good news I am hearing with Audacity.  But you did verify what I was saying in regards to OSS working with it.

I think I may end up like you and buy another sound card myself.  I did get set on the Delta 44, BUT I overlooked one important thing.  The Delta 44 & 66 do not have midi ports.  Normally (in Windows anyway) that isn't a big deal because most of my controllers and control surfaces use USB.  BUT I already know that my main Kord controller keyboard...the nice one with the sliders on it...the one I use for my organ patches, I cannot use with USB in Linux.  Luckily it also has Midi In/Out jacks.   So those USB controllers that are not supported, I am going to have to do it the old fashioned way.  At any rate there was a similar card that M-Audio had called the Audiophile 2496 and that is supported and it does have SPDIF and Midi...BUT it is also only 2 in and 2 out.  That is no good.

Idealistically as a BARE minimum I COULD get by with just two inputs. But I need four outputs.  The reason for that is when I do work with my DJ mixing programs.  Two channels are needed for the main audio output and another two channels are needed for the headphone monitoring system.  4 inputs would be better because then I can grab two outside sources (such as my Pioneer CD mixing turntables) and mix them into the computer audio.  That is why I was looking into the Delta 44.  But now that is bummed out for me.  But then I found out that the inputs of the entire Delta series cannot be used for mic level...you have to buy the "Audio Buddy" for that.   So what I thought would be inexpensive would quickly become fairly expensive overnight!   And I KNOW the M-Audio products are not that great.  

So I guess you are in the same boat huh?

The Audigy does have a couple of different inputs to work with and they do have multiple outputs.  The only bad thing is that most of those outputs are 1/8" jacks and they are notorious for developing loose connections.

For the most part I am hoping that I can get some kind of audio to work with out of the X-Fi, then I can try out the Linux programs.

Hopefully the X-Fi would receive better support and/or maybe even firewire has better support.  But for now this is like pulling teeth.

Anyway, MG delivered some good news.  He found a program that is called Sweep (scroll up to his post).  He says he isn't having ANY kind of stuttering or skipping problems with it at all.  I took a quick look at it myself and it is another editing program like Audacity.  It looks even more so like Soundforge to me.  So THAT is good news that he got that working.  My guess is that it is probably a more ALSA compliant program and thus it works better with the X-Fi's ALSA drivers.

Anyway, I am going to go back to Amtam's posts and see if I can get things going tonight.

EDIT:

I think Sweep will work out for all of us.  Take a look at this:

[http://www.metadecks.org/software/sweep/news.html](http://www.metadecks.org/software/sweep/news.html)

now scroll down to Wed Feb 7, 2007.  What does the first line say?

JUST as I suspected :).


EDIT:

I did a bit more digging and I think I may have found another lead on a sound card.  As it is the famous Echo product line IS supported in Linux.  And this applies to the new stuff as well as the old.  They are moderate priced.  Definitely costs more than M-Audio, but no where near RME. 

I am looking at the Echo Gina-3G right now.  It has a pretty good spec.  Here is the info:

[http://www.echoaudio.com/Products/PCI/Gina3G/index.php](http://www.echoaudio.com/Products/PCI/Gina3G/index.php)

So while it is more expensive than the M-Audio line, it has built in preamps, it has midi, it has SPDIF AND it has 6 audio outputs.  The short coming is that it only has two inputs.  It sells new for around $279.  BUT there is always Ebay and there are always older variants of the Echo product line.


Geo

I read with interest what you are saying re the different audio cards, and can appreciate what you are saying about the many pros and cons for each setup.
I am hopeful you will get your X-Fi card going, as for me, it is a hard choice to better unless one spends many more dollars.

Yes, the video clip you mention. To me it looks to be that they have sprinkled a few projectors about to display the plasma vids on. The rest would be installed lighting of various types.

Talking about projection, have you ever seen or been interested in sky and or building projection. I haven't done this, but it can be pretty spectacular.
What I am toying around with is working within a dome. Involving a team of people, both technically and creatively to project from within a semi translucent dome to display moving image together with sound. The domes would be somewhere in the order of around 6 to 8 metres in diameter with all the equipment concealed inside the dome. Then place three of these domes in a field out in the country some distance away from say a major road. Can you imagine how that could look and the reaction of the motorists?? Just to get your creative juices going huh!!

I need to go and a few things, but will be about.
Unfortunately, I think you will need to hear from Amtam or Nullhead re the missing deb package

Keep smiling, you will be pleasantly pleased when sound doth exude from thy card.

mountain goat ~ I am the wizard of Oz  :cool:

PS. I understand you may be using the real time kernel, and this may have something to do with the issue, so Amtam or Nullhead might need to consider this.
I had a quick look at ubuntu studio last night, and will look more into it as I think it may be more prudent for me to use a rt kernel as well.

Cool how the world turns but yet these computers put everyone in touch.  Ahhh, the World Wide Web.  Nice to get caught up in it from time to time.

> I read with interest what you are saying re the different audio cards, and can appreciate what you are saying about the many pros and cons for each setup.

Yeah.  It is tough because as of now I am anticipating the worst, so it is a hard trade off to find something that is inexpensive, but yet has some kind of usability.  As I said earlier, the M-Audio Delta 44 looked like a good inexpensive solution, but when I really delved into the card's features it really didn't have much.  Sure it was 4in and 4 out which is my minimum requirement, but it doesn't even have mic pre's.  Then no SPDIF and no midi.  Heck, my X-Fi can do WAY more than that.

In one aspect I don't mind having to buy an extra midi interface because I do forsee needing a 4 in 2 out because of the organ projects I want to work on.  But I know without supported USB capability on my favorite controller keyboard...I definitely need some kind of midi capabilities.

The next contender I am looking at right now is the ECHO Gina.  ECHO products are good and I only recently found out they are Linux supported.  The Gina has the short coming of only 2 inputs, but they are mic pre capable.  It has 6 outs and completes the package with SPIDIF and Midi.  Sure it cost $100 more than the Delta 44, but the Audio Buddy preamp for the Delta 44 is another $100 and I am sure the Midi interface will be another $50 to $100.  

> I am hopeful you will get your X-Fi card going, as for me, it is a hard choice to better unless one spends many more dollars..

Well, if I were able to get the X-Fi 100% supported, I CAN get the breakout box option and it would make the X-Fi a pretty powerful contender.  It does have the low noise preamps like the pro cards do.  So for basic futzing around, it WOULD have been a really good card.  But unfortunately without a good driver, it is useless here in Linux.

> Yes, the video clip you mention. To me it looks to be that they have sprinkled a few projectors about to display the plasma vids on. The rest would be installed lighting of various types.

Actually on that TV show, they can display pictures and really complex patterns.  They aren't plasma TV's behind there, that is for sure.  BUT they could be computer based video projectors...more than likely.  But the trick is how they filled the whole space for the projection without any light spillover or anything and that is the hard part to figure out.

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-03
jukingeo, mountain_goat correctly identified the problem:

> ====== making target install/linux-headers-2.6.24.3 [new prereqs: ]======
This is kernel package version 11.001.
echo "The UTS Release version in include/linux/utsrelease.h"; echo " \"2.6.24.3-custom\" "; echo "does not match current version:"; echo " \"2.6.24.3\" "; echo "Please correct this."; exit 2
The UTS Release version in include/linux/utsrelease.h
"2.6.24.3-custom"
does not match current version:
"2.6.24.3"
Please correct this.
make: *** [install/linux-headers-2.6.24.3] Error 2

There's a mismatch in the version. This happened because at some point, you probably rebooted **before** the completion of the compiling and/or installing of the deb's. The new kernel became then active (2.6.24.3-custom) while the matching headers were not compiled yet. The script expects to see 2.6.24.3 but is presented with 2.6.24.3-custom and aborts.

I suggest you reboot back into the *original* kernel (the one you used before you started the howto, could be the generic or the rt, not sure which one you used), uninstall the custom-kernel using Synaptic (or apt or dpkg) and repeat the command of building the kernel_image and kernel_headers. 

```
make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
```

Don't forget the make-kpkg-clean. And ofcourse make sure you are in the right dir, but you probably know all that by now :)

That's one of the advantages of problems; you actually learn from them. No pain, no gain... ;)

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-03
It would probably be possible to only build the kernel-headers and not the kernel-image (since you already done that), but I don't recommend it. Headers and kernel are a 'team' and should be compiled and installed together right after one another.

But if you don't want to wait for the long kernel-compile you could try building the headers seperately, but it has to be done from the same running kernel you used for the kernel-compile! Otherwise you get this version-mismatch.

Again, I don't really recommend it, but it could work if you do it right and it would save you a lot of time.

On my own machine, compiling takes now less then 5 minutes, because I removed almost everything I don't use. And having a really fast machine helps too... ;)

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-03
> **jukingeo said:**
> Hello Amtam,

Unfortunately I have a 3 year old machine, so no core duo or anything like that.  The compiling took about an hour on my machine.

I don't want to take any short cuts. So, no, I would like to do it the right way. 

So what do I do to remove everything.  You mentioned that the kernel is in listed in Synaptic.  What should I search for?  Or should I do everything in the Terminal?

Just search for 'linux-image'. You can use the terminal too if you want, or a root-prompt (no X at all), like I do myself.

```
dpkg --list | grep linux-image
```

This will list your installed kernels. You can then remove the already installed kernel with:

```
apt-get remove --purge linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom
```


> 
Is there stuff that I can remove to speed things up?  I did go through your recommendations the last time, however, nothing was ticked off.  I was a bit surprised at the length of time it took it to compile.  I have a 2.8ghz machine with 1.5gig of ram on board.  I figured that if it took only 10mins on your machine, I was good for a half hour.  Wrong! It was twice that.


You can disable entire sections at a time. For instance if you don't have ISDN-card or PC-Cards or some other rarely used stuff, you can tick off ISDN-support and all other options below it will disabled too. But be careful; sometimes things aren't what they seem. Kernel-tuning is an art you know... ;)

You can't really mess up like in the old days though. If your machine fails to boot, just select another installed kernel.
But anyway, don't spend too much time on the kernel-tuning right now; your goal was to install the X-Fi, remember? ;)

mountain goat ~ wanting another cup of FairTrade organic coffee


...And the moment we been waiting a month for.


[SIZE="7"]SOUND!!!![/SIZE]

Ahhhhhhhhh FINALLY!  

Oh, gee...lemme see where to begin.  Not everything is all roses mind you, but this is what I have:

1) [COLOR="black"][SIZE="4"]Streaming INTERNET!!![/SIZE][/COLOR]

Yah!  I have my YouTube back!

2) Ardour plays perfectly, crystal clear.  There is a setting now that you can play it outside of Jack and go to ALSA directly.  I didn't know that you can do that.

3) Terminator X:  This is a cool DJ function that I have to really check out later.  You can scratch mp3's pitch bend them add effects to them and play around with them just like you could a record on a turntable.  This is also crystal clear.

4) Hydrogen: A drum machine.  Normally another one that goes through Jack. Works fine, but the sound is a bit grainy, even with the buffers set all the way up, but it isn't bad.  Cool to play around with but it needs to be fixed for recording.

5) Cinelerra:  (A video editor) BACK IN ACTION

Sorry, but this is all I tested for now.

MOST of my games have sound, but there are some problems.


Ok now for the things that are NOT working, but would like to fix.

1) Audacity:  Sounds like Rice Krispies cereal.  Definitely not usable (MG, you have this same problem.  I am going to try to download and use Sweep later and see what happens).

2) Two of my game console emulators are not working.  GFEC (a Nintendo emulator) makes a hideous noise and will not play the game.  GENS (a Sega Genesis emulator) will not even boot up.  Surprisingly two Linux games (Neverball being one) stopped working altogether and like GENS will not boot up.

3) Some applications such as Genpo (an organ simulator) have problems like some of my games in which I click on them and they will not boot up.

4) Open Movie Editor (another video editing program) refuses to work at all. But it didn't work good under OSS either.  I may just remove it and stick with something better and that I know works...Cinelerra.\

5) Jack doesn't seem to be working. As prior to my change over to ALSA I am getting the "Could not open ALSA sequencer as a Client. ALSA Midi patchbay will not be available."

That is not good and it is also unacceptable as it more then likely means that Midi applications such as Rosegarden probably will not work.

I did double check the Jack Control set up and it is set to ALSA.


Ok, now come the questions.

1)  Could it be possible that when I installed the programs that they somehow "automatically" configure to the sound driver that is available at the time of installation?  And now that I am on ALSA the programs are corrupted?  Would simply removing and reinstalling the affected applications fix the problem?  Would I need to check configuration files or something?

2) Can someone help me fix Jack?  I need that to work...obviously.


So all in all, I finally feel that I am getting somewhere and that I am inching along in making a dent in some of the Ubuntu Studio applications of which when I had OSS NONE would work.

I do have my streaming internet back so that is another major plus.

I have to run...it is getting late.  But I figured I would give you all a heads up and let you know that the X-Fi ALSA driver is working.

Thanx again for everyone's help thusfar...but I am still not out of the woods yet.

However, I am going to call it a night in the forums for now and go play with some more of those applications!

G'night

Geo

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-07-03
Geo, I'm glad to hear the Studio apps are working for you with the ALSA driver. I'm not sure which version of my guide you followed, but let me give you some tips on getting back to ALSA.
```
sudo apt-get install alsa-oss libesd-alsa0 alsa-utils gstreamer0.10-alsa
```
If you reconfigured linux-sound-base, configure it for ALSA.
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure linux-sound-base
```

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-04
> **jukingeo said:**
> ...And the moment we been waiting a month for.


[SIZE="7"]SOUND!!!![/SIZE]

Ahhhhhhhhh FINALLY!

Way to go! I knew you could do it :)

> 1) Audacity:  Sounds like Rice Krispies cereal.  Definitely not usable (MG, you have this same problem.  I am going to try to download and use Sweep later and see what happens).

Audacity is not really usable at this time it seems. Both you, MG and myself have the same problems with it. I think I'm gonna try compling it from the latest source, see if that helps.

Sweep is already much better, but I still get small 'drop-outs' there; the sound itself is OK, but occasionally, it just 'skips'.


> 
5) Jack doesn't seem to be working. As prior to my change over to ALSA I am getting the "Could not open ALSA sequencer as a Client. ALSA Midi patchbay will not be available."

That is not good and it is also unacceptable as it more then likely means that Midi applications such as Rosegarden probably will not work.

Jack *should* work. It works just fine on my system.

Maybe not all of the sequencer-modules are loaded. Try this:

```
sudo su
modprobe snd-seq
echo snd-seq >> /etc/modules 
```

You need te reboot after these commands. A search for ""Could not open ALSA sequencer as a Client" on Google give many hits, so it's a common problem.



> 
Thanx again for everyone's help thusfar...but I am still not out of the woods yet.

And you probably won't be for some time.... ;)

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-04
[FONT=Trebuchet MS][SIZE=3]Hey creative peeps,
Do you want to hear some good news,

Make sure you have a Jack Patch do hickey installed

OK, if you have Audacity, then run it up and load a file, a nice audio file is what you will require.

Then go to the preferences and set the playback device to : Jack Audio Connection Kit:system. or even the vu bridge-6062

The make sure you have a decent sound amp hooked up to your creative outputs, as you would usually have anyhow, and hit the play.

Tell me you hear cleanness and the quality you are expecting??
[/SIZE][/FONT][FONT=Trebuchet MS][SIZE=3]
Great isn't it ~ :mrgreen:[/SIZE][/FONT]

I want to say a big thanks to Amtam and it seems Temüjin as well and NullHead.
I really appreciate all the effort in you being so helpful and assistive and supportive over the past days.
We are all the more happier with getting our Creative X-Fi cards to work in the Hardy One.

I don't think the discussions or indeed the thread will close at this point as I'm sure there will be many other issues 
to be looked at, but it does appear to me that at last there is a proven HowTo method to configure these cards to function.

For me, I'm only at the end of my second week with hitting the deck and running something other than a MicroSoft OS.

I don't know what to make of this, if is good, bad, ugly or what have you.  But I figured I'd post the results here.


EDIT:

[SIZE="6"]IT'S WORKING!!!!![/SIZE]

I am a dumb dumb.  I kept closing out Jack Control.  Just by accident I left it on and pulled up Audacity to try a different setting out and Jack Control "Twitched".  Then I pushed the "Start" button on Jack Control and the Play button on Audacity.  BOOM! All of a sudden it was playing! (AND CLEAR TOO!!)  So while both Jack Control and Audacity were running I pulled up the meter bridge.  It came up and stayed!  The meters were continually pegged, but then again, the sample I used was a very loud normalized one.

So I closed out of everything but left Jack Control up and started.  I went back to my aps menu.  I pulled up Aeolus (an organ program)  BOOM! It came up.  I pulled up Genpo (another organ program) BOOM!  It came up.

BOOM CHICHA CHICHA BOOM CHICKA CHICA BOOM!!!!

[SIZE="5"]YAAAAAAHOOOOOOOOOOOO!![/SIZE]


Ya!  Now I am happy as a pig in you know what.

\\:D/\\:D/\\:D/\\:D/\\:D/\\:D/

[Doin' The Cabbage Patch Dance followed by the Mashed Potatoe]

Well, guys, it is going to take a while for me to figure this all out now and I am still not 100% sure if Midi will work, but Rosegarden is coming up now WITHOUT an error message.

So this means that up to now my audio is about 85% there.  I can live with this...

At least now I can PLAY!

Well, that is going to be it for me today.  Going to a house party today at my parent's place...Not for getting Ubuntu Studio to work, but it is the 4th of July!  But still equally both a good reason to set off some fireworks!

THANX A HUGE BUNCH GUYS!



Geo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-06
> **FlyingWV said:**
> Congrats you guys on getting it to work :) My downgrade to the Audigy went smoothly as far as sound working, was straight out of the box. I guess i'll stick with this until the X-Fi is supported a bit more, as I don't need all of the gadgets that you guys do. 

Again, it's great to hear that you got it up and running and can test your applications, hope they're up to your expectation geo :)


Hello Fly,

I guess you are up and running now too? That is great news.  The Audigy is a nice system as well.  I am not sure if it has 96k capability, but I know that the newer Audigy 2 & 4 systems with the "pro" breakout box are pretty close to the X-Fi.

As for myself, yeah, I am MOSTLY running fine now.  Some apps are still a bit 'twitchy' and some applications do have the 'crackling' sound problems (such as the Ubuntu System sounds), but when it comes to the Ubuntu Studio applications, I found that setting everything to work with Jack and it's ALSA driver solves many problems.

Both MG and my self have found out that Audacity WILL work crystal clear, but not through it's own ALSA driver.   It could very well be that Audacity (being an older program) could be patching sound to ALSA via it's OSS driver and that could be causing problems.  However, if you set Audacity (or any application that can use Jack) to the "Use Jack" setting, everything becomes clear.

I am still going to check out Sweep though because it looks interesting and perhaps more capable than Audacity.

Everything isn't 100% though as I said above.  Some of my games are not working now.  I may attempt to reload the ones I am having a problem with to see if there is any change in performance.  But as of now only four Linux games my NES emulator and my Sega Genesis emulator isn't working. But overall, now I have 85% of my sound working and the two main requirements of getting my Ubuntu Studio applications and streaming internet audio to work have been fulfilled.

As of now I have the arduous task of figuring all of these cool Ubuntu Applications out.  So I have a lot of ground to cover.  I have to first start off by learning Jack as everything ties through there.



Amtam, Nullhead.  I have noticed a few things regarding the installation of the new kernel and subsequent performance:

I took this from my grub menu.lst:

title		Ubuntu 8.04.1, kernel 2.6.24.3-custom
root		(hd0,0)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24.3-custom root=UUID=b554ae58-fba0-4ae0-a405-0361be180aa3 ro quiet splash
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.24.3-custom
quiet

title		Ubuntu 8.04.1, kernel 2.6.24.3-custom (recovery mode)
root		(hd0,0)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24.3-custom root=UUID=b554ae58-fba0-4ae0-a405-0361be180aa3 ro single
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.24.3-custom

title		Ubuntu 8.04.1, kernel 2.6.24-19-rt
root		(hd0,0)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24-19-rt root=UUID=b554ae58-fba0-4ae0-a405-0361be180aa3 ro quiet splash
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.24-19-rt
quiet

title		Ubuntu 8.04.1, kernel 2.6.24-19-rt (recovery mode)


Ok, now first off I noticed that the custom kernel's version number ends in .3 where as my last kernel was version .19-rt.

So now two things are coming to mind:

1) The custom Kernel is based on an older version, correct?  If so, how old is it?
2) Regardless if the build was from my .19-rt kernel I am getting the funny feeling that this new customer kernel is NOT a real time kernel.  Why am I saying that?

Well for one, going into Jack Control--> Setup has a tick mark for "realtime operation".  If I tick that off it causes Jack to crash and produce an error message.  

Here are the results:

15:10:57.098 Patchbay deactivated.
15:10:57.240 Statistics reset.
15:10:57.359 Startup script...
15:10:57.359 artsshell -q terminate
15:10:57.422 ALSA connection graph change.
15:10:57.787 Startup script terminated with exit status=256.
15:10:57.788 JACK is starting...
15:10:57.789 /usr/bin/jackd -R -dalsa -dhw:0 -r48000 -p1024 -n2 -m
jackd 0.109.2
Copyright 2001-2005 Paul Davis and others.
jackd comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY
This is free software, and you are welcome to redistribute it
under certain conditions; see the file COPYING for details
JACK compiled with System V SHM support.
cannot use real-time scheduling (FIFO at priority 10) [for thread -1210952016, from thread -1210952016] (1: Operation not permitted)
cannot create engine
15:10:57.804 JACK was started with PID=6243.
15:10:57.807 JACK was stopped successfully.
15:10:57.807 Post-shutdown script...
15:10:57.808 killall jackd
jackd: no process killed
15:10:58.232 Post-shutdown script terminated with exit status=256.
15:11:00.017 Could not connect to JACK server as client. - Overall operation failed. - Unable to connect to server. Please check the messages window for more info.

If I now untick the realtime check mark in the Jack Control setup menu, it will come back up with no problems at all.

The second thing that I have noticed is that some applications have a good deal of latency.  Streaming internet audio for one does take a long while to register volume changes.   The Ubuntu Studio applications for the most part are fine, but there are some that 'hesitate' a bit.

So I am wondering if the custom kernel is for realtime use.

Another question I had is in regards to updates.  The icon came up again for new updates.  Thusfar I have NOT been updating on the new custom kernel.  I don't think I should because I do fear that an update may "break" the system.  (So if anyone knows a way to turn updates off, I would appreciate that).

Overall it isn't a biggie of a problem for me as I really just want to check out the Linux audio applications for right now.  Later on down the road I most certainly would create a dedicated Linux machine with properly supported devices on board.  But this is a good start overall.

So I do owe a huge "Thank You" to Amtam, Nullhead, Fly, MG (any anyone else that I may have inadvertently left out) for helping walking me through this mess and getting the X-Fi to work in Hardy without my having to resort to other measures and at least I can stay here in Ubuntu Studio for now.

Well, I have a lot to learn.  I am going to be checking back in here for responses and if anything new comes up in regards to issues with this set up, but I guess now my questions are going to turn to other areas in regards to support for the programs I intend to use.

But as of now with Jack, Audacity, Ardour, Sweep, Rosegarden, TerminatorX and a bunch of other cool programs working. I am going to be busy doing much awaited research.

EDIT:

Back again, I have an application called JOrgan (which is a midi organ controller type program).  It requires Java Runtime Environment 6 to operate.  I have downloaded the program on my machine, but not JRE 6 as of yet.  JRE 6 IS in a repository for Synaptic...so installation should be a breeze, however, I figured I would come back here and check with you guys if the Java Runtime Environment will affect the custom kernel (or my internet audio) in any way.  Simply put...I don't want to break anything.

END EDIT

So thanx again to everyone involved.

So, for those here in the US.  How was your 4th of July weekend?  Mine was pretty good.  I spent Friday and Sat over my parent's home with my wife and two boys.   One neighbor had a block party and had a really cool fireworks display.  Both days my father and I put together a nice BBQ and LOTS of food!  Both days were overcast, but the rain held up.  So it was a decent weekend.

Now it is back to Ubuntu Studio and playing with all those cool programs.

Have a good day to all!!

Geo

### Post by mnnn on 2008-07-06
I agree with you that your current (custom) kernel is not realtime. You should compile newer realtime kernel if you want to (I assume yes, because of your latency problems).

---

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-07-06
About kernel versions: Ubuntu's scheme doesn't match the vanilla Linux kernel version that one could download off [ftp://ftp.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/](ftp://ftp.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/) (i.e comparing -19 with .3 doesn't tell you anything about how recently they were released). FWIW, I know Ubuntu's 2.6.24-18 kernel was based off of 2.6.24.3, because I made a custom kernel out of that one.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-07-06
From what I can gather, the last number in the version is for the revision number ubuntu puts on there for how many times they've rebuilt the kernel. 

You can install all the updates, but you NEED to stay in your custom kernel, so you don't break anything. Use a program called:"startupmanager". Install it like so: ```
sudo apt-get install startupmanager
```and use it from the menu system to select the kernel that you want to boot as default. 

I'm glad you've accomplished most of your obstacles. Also, I'm not sure if there is an option for compiling your kernel with the realtime feature. You might need a patch to make it runtime, but I'm not sure. My [friend]("http://www.google.com") showed me [this]("http://rt.wiki.kernel.org/index.php/Main_Page"). ;)

I had a great 4rth of July! I blew off allot of fireworks and had a overall good time. :D

Amtam mentioned that since I had Ubuntu Studio last on my machine when I compiled the custom kernel, it SHOULD be real-time.  However, with Jack Control crashing when I tick of r-t, but working fine with it unchecked, I am beginning to think otherwise. 

I have not a full blow test of the customer kernel as of yet, but latency seems to be more dependent on the application.  Most programs the latency does seem barely noticeable which really isn't that bad if indeed the custom kernel is NOT real time.  However, I do notice that some programs (those that work outside of Jack namely) and streaming internet does have ALOT of latency.  YouTube for example has a pretty good amount of latency when adjusting volume and other audio functions while on the web.  So if I use a streaming audio application to do recording work with...I could be in trouble.

But the good news is that for the most part the latency with programs using jack is pretty good.  While I don't consider it on the level as what I can get with my Windows programs, it is 'good enough' and I can at least test out the programs.

Today I was mostly trying to work with Jack and I have made a small set up with Jack, QSynth, and the virtual keyboard. However, I am sure it may come into play when running lots of things at one time.  As it is I was only running one synth.

Well, I am off to do more testing.

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-07
About the rt-kernel: it says on this [Wiki](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/RealTime/Hardy) that 

> We use _only_ the full Ubuntu Kernel (not vanilla, not custom version or other). The Ubuntu Realtime kernel is the Ubuntu Kernel plus the real-time pre-emption patch. Also the configuration (aka /boot/config*) is the same except for specific real-time options.

So maybe you need to apply this pre-emption patch first and then use the .config from the default rt-kernel to compile the custom kernel with ALSA enabled? If I find the time, I will try it myself.

[This Guide](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/HowToVanillaKernelWithRealtimePreemption) could be helpfull, but it is based on the vanilla sources and not on the Ubuntu-sources. You will have to find a specific Ubuntu-patch first I guess, but you get the idea... ;)

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-08
Hello Amtam, Nullhead.

I discovered a possible problem with the Nvidia driver last night.  I tried a program in my Ubuntu Studio aps list called "MIXXX".  It is a DJ program.  I had two strange problems with it.

1) Jack for some reason isn't working with it...but setting the audio to straight ALSA does make the program work.
2) There is a display that isn't working because it requires OpenGL.

I know I had OpenGL on my system prior to the custom kernel compilation. Now it isn't there.  OpenGL works with the video driver.   So I decided to investigate.

There is a tab under "Applications" called "System Tools" on the taskbar. Within "System Tools" is the Nvidia X-Server Settings.  Clicking on that, I get an error message.

[You do not appear to be using the NVIDIA X driver. Please edit your X configuration file (just run `nvidia-xconfig` as root), and restart the X server.]

I tried to do as it says and run the file from straight command line (outside of Gnome to avoid errors).  For some reason the file will not run.

So thinking the Nvidia Driver was corrupted, I reinstalled it.  Upon reboot I got the same blue screen with gray text box with the X-Server error as before.  So this forced me to reboot into recovery mode.  This time I DIDN'T select the dpkg option and just told it to fix the X-Server and then after that "Boot Normally".   That worked and I was hoping that the X-Server error I got above would be gone....It wasn't.

So I went through all that for nothing.  Worse, because I reinstalled the video driver, I blasted my audio settings and now I have to uninstall the X-Fi driver (which I did already) and I have to reinstall it.

But before I do that I wanted to bring to your attention that there is a  problem with the X-Server and the N-Vidia card.  Apparently because of this error the card is not running in it's graphics or OpenGL mode.

Supposedly this nvidia-xconfig.bin file supposed to fix the problem.  I found the file in the /usr/bin directory, but it doesn't seem to run.  I did try the CHMOD command in Terminal too.  So I don't know why it will not run.

Anyway, something is fishy with the X-server and the Nvidia driver and I figured I would bring it to your attention and hopefully there is a fix for the problem so I can then follow that up by reinstalling the X-Fi drivers.

So...once again...I am soundless.

Thanx,

Geo

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-09
> So thinking the Nvidia Driver was corrupted, I reinstalled it.

How exactly did you reinstall it?

> This time I DIDN'T select the dpkg option and just told it to fix the X-Server and then after that "Boot Normally". That worked and I was hoping that the X-Server error I got above would be gone....It wasn't.

Just choose the third option from the recovery-menu to install the Nvidia-driver manually. Root shell-prompt. Then the usual

```
sh Nvidia-driver-xxx-blablabla.run
```

It will say the driver is already installed. Keep going. It will ask you to search for a predefined kernel-module on the nvidia-site. Don't do that either. Just let it compile the module for your system and say yes to other questions regarding OpenGL and adjusting X-settings. If it's finished, type

```
modprobe nvidia
```

No error-message should appear. To check if it is loaded, type

```
lsmod | grep nvidia
```

You should see it somewhere in the list. Then reboot to normal mode and you should see the full-screen nvidia-logo when X starts. That's how you can tell if you are really using the nvidia-driver and not the nv-driver, which has no OpenGL. It looks to me you were using that driver and that's why nvidia-xconfig complained.

You can always check and [edit](http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86_64/173.14.09/README/chapter-06-section-02.html) your xorg.conf yourself to be sure.

Really, it shouldn't be that hard. It used to be much harder in the past I can tell you.... ;)

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-09
jukingeo, the modprobe/lsmod commands aren't really necessary.
I just use it to see if the module compiled correctly.

You don't have to uninstall the nvidia-driver first. The installer will detect an earlier install and uninstall it in the same process. If only the Creative-installer was that advanced....[-o<

The nvidia splash-screen/logo only comes up with the manually installed driver from nvidia's website. The restricted driver from the repositories has it disabled.

Did you download the nvidia installer from the nvidia website? If not, download it from there. Amtam is trying to tell you to run the installer that you should've downloaded from Nvidia's website. If you have **not** downloaded it from there, then download it and run it as Amatam has described earlier; ./Nvidia*

Amtam is also right in the sense that this shouldn've be as difficult as it may seem ;). Really all you need to do is download Nvidia's driver installer and run that any time you have a problem with your video. Boot into recovery mode, navigate to where the file is located, run it from the recovery terminal, reboot, enjoy, working, open gl, video :D.

Lets try this:

Remove the builtin drivers for the nvidia card:```
sudo apt-get purge nvidia-kernel-common nvidia-glx nvidia-glx-new nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-glx-envy
```If it asks you to uninstall any of those that put in that command, then by all means **remove 'em!**Then run the installer *again ...* and type more /etc/X11/xorg.conf>>log.txt and upload the txt, in your home directory, as an attachment on your next post.

Hello Nullhead,

I am back home now and I did what both you and Amtam said and guess what?  It worked!

However, I also re-downloaded the driver from Nvidia just to be sure that the other one wasn't corrupt.

Anyway, I am not sure what the "type more /etc/X11/xorg.conf>>log.txt" line was supposed to do.  I did invoke it after the driver installed and nothing came up.

Anyway, as the driver installed, there were a few warning messages that came up...mainly because it couldn't find the old driver that was in place.  Regardless, after it compiled the driver I told it to "boot up normally"  I was crossing my fingers and low and behold FINALLY, the Nvidia screen came up. 

Back in the OS, I was able to go to APPLICATIONS ==> SYSTEM TOOLS ==> Nvidia Server Settings and I got a new screen with a bunch of new information.

It looks like OpenGL support is active, as I do have an "OpenGL settings screen coming up now and there are a few adjustments."

So at this point I am going to assume I am good.  But if there is something I can run just to be sure, then so be it.

So it looks like I am ready to reinstall the X-Fi drivers and get my sound back.

Thanx for your and Amtam's help.

Both of you guys should start up a thread...perhaps were Amtam originally put the guide and store all this information in that guide as a sticky.  I am sure there are many many X-Fi owners that would love to gleen the info here and in those procedures.

Geo

OK guys, I am back up and running with audio.  The OpenGL graphics are now working with MIXXX.  However, there is a bit more crackling on the output using ALSA driver straight.  Turning up the latency doesn't help too much.  The trouble is that Mixxx is SUPPOSED to work with Jack and it isn't working.

The rest of the Jack programs are working again.  Audacity, Ardour....all sound great.

Thanx again!

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-10
> **NullHead said:**

Hello Nullhead,

Yeah, I do see that you have both links on the first page, but there are couple of problems here.  For one the post starts out with solving the issue with the X-Fi using OSS.  If you edited the line that points to Amtam's guide as that is the procedure to get ALSA operational, that would make it more clearer to future readers that now there is a choice and that the ALSA driver has been proven to work in Hardy.   Next the category heading is a bit misleading as people who see AMD 64 who don't know better are going to think this post is JUST for the AMD people.  I made that same mistake myself...but didn't realize it until later on and apparently the procedures work for other systems as well.

However, I am not sure if you can change the category heading.

I don't mean to nitpick, but this is tremendously valuable information and the easier we can make things for noobs, the more they will be encouraged to stay and support Linux.

I originally stayed away from Linux because I was always told, "Oh, THAT OS is only good for geeks and people with a PHD in Rocket Science".

I personally don't agree with that, but I will say that for the average person.  This is still a pretty scary venture.  I consider myself computer savvy and I have set up entire Windows networks before.  If it wasn't for the people I met in this thread such as yourself, Amtam, Fly, Mountain Goat, Tem (and any others I inadvertently left out), I was having qualms that Linux would be a viable OS for working with sound and video.

I was VERY close to making the decision to use Linux only for housekeeping and internet browsing but keeping to Windows for my audio work.

I am very happy to say that I stuck in there and you guys got me running with my obtuse sound card.  So you opened me up to a new world just by getting my sound card to work.  This is my first week that I been ENTIRELY on Ubuntu only.  I have NOT booted up Windows once since this weekend!!!!!!!!!!

So this is really big news for me and this is a VERY important thread.  For the noob this is the difference of doing one evening's worth of work as opposed to my 1 month of prodding around for solutions.  The X-Fi is a VERY popular card and this guide will make quite a few people happy and it will keep many heads of hair intact for future Ubuntu noobs.

So once again...

=D>=D>=D>=D>=D>

Big 'round of applause guys.

I have been doing my part of contributing to the Linux way of life and spreading the word around about this thread over in other Linux forums.  

Thanx again.

I been reading up on Ardour and it is great! Guess what?  I found out that you CAN map all the controls to Midi...just like Ableton's live!  So with a good looping program and Rosegarden setup with Ardour into Jack, that is pretty much a good replacement for Live.  Ya!  \\:D/

So thanx to you guys, I will be sticking around Linux for a while :).

Take care and have a good day!

Now, next problem,  to get Mixxx working with JACK!

Geo

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-10
[SIZE=2]Hello my creative friends,
Hey Geo,
[/SIZE]> Hey! Anyone know what happened to Mountain Goat?  Haven't seen him "grazin'" around here lately.
  Yep, I've been grazin in yonder fields, they are green and nicely hilly too!
 No, but really, I have had some pretty big days with getting the industrial control system I am working on, programmed and installed.
 Four mega buckaroos of product is depending on it keeping the temperature and humidity all nice and controlled.
 So, tonight, 1 :00am I am back home after driving over 400km today across the other side of my beautiful island.
I only wish that I could use a Linux OS such as ubuntu instead of ms windows for the SCADA system. That would be very nice.

And so I wanted to catch up with the latest developments of our group.
 
 First off, all is still working well with my system here.
 Geo, you mentioned the rt switch in Jack, I had the same problem, and I would expect so as well as I am still running the non rt kernel. I did look briefly at what the rt kernel might offer and any issues it has.
 I did find lots of web info about configuring and things to watch for with the rt that you may be interested in and can share that with you if you need.
 
In the coming days I want to look very carefully at what my needs are and will be with regards to a useful OS and would like to know if I will be better served if I do go and use the real time kernel, either by ubuntu studio or by replacing the existing kernel with a real time ubuntu kernel. I need to spend more time researching.
 
 But really what I do want to do is to better understand the process we have just been through with Amtam's fantastic How To.
I can see that I am slowly becoming more familiar with the various commands and also where to finds things.
 I am gaining more confidence and and dash of knowledge, :lolflag:
 From Amtam
 >  [SIZE=2]Originally Posted by **amtam**                     [[IMG]http://ubuntuforums.org/images/buttons/viewpost.gif[/IMG]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5346718#post5346718")[/SIZE]

[/SIZE][SIZE=2]
Geo,
[/SIZE]> Sorry to hear it didn't work out.  BUT, I would like to hear the results of a custom RT kernel.
  Yeah, likewise......
 
 At the moment, like you Geo, I am trying out various audio programs to see what suits best for the many tasks in creating, editing, managing my sound files.
 I loaded Ardore the other day and see that this is quite a fantastic program.
In fact, I am finding many many potentially fantastic programs worthwhile to be used in my little developing studio here on the mountain.
When I start looking at all these programs I start to salivate and need my dribble bib 'cause I don't know where to start.
Then I need to read everything about each program, I must suffer from OCD I think??
 
So, yes at the moment, for me it is a great learning process, with the knowing that I want to learn much and be able to know and fully understand the concepts in building a custom kernel system and what the implications are for enabling or disabling various system and application components.

 [SIZE=2]

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-10
> **mountain_goat said:**
> [SIZE=2]Hello my creative friends,
Hey Geo,
[/SIZE] Yep, I've been grazin in yonder fields, they are green and nicely hilly too!
 No, but really, I have had some pretty big days with getting the industrial control system I am working on, programmed and installed.
 Four mega buckaroos of product is depending on it keeping the temperature and humidity all nice and controlled.
 So, tonight, 1 :00am I am back home after driving over 400km today across the other side of my beautiful island.
I only wish that I could use a Linux OS such as ubuntu instead of ms windows for the SCADA system. That would be very nice.

Hello MG! Nice to see you are back.  Wondered what was going on down there.  Anyway, sounds like an interesting project you are working on there.  I am just learning to program embedded control systems myself.  I am at a "Basic" level and I mean that literally.  I can program Basic Stamps, but that is about it.  I am no where near the level of what these Intel monsters can do.


>  First off, all is still working well with my system here.
 Geo, you mentioned the rt switch in Jack, I had the same problem, and I would expect so as well as I am still running the non rt kernel. I did look briefly at what the rt kernel might offer and any issues it has.
 I did find lots of web info about configuring and things to watch for with the rt that you may be interested in and can share that with you if you need.

Nice to know you are still running fine.  I had a bit of a glitch with the video drivers.  I found out because one of my applications needed OpenGL to work and I know I had OpenGL on my system before the custom kernel.  At any rate what was happening was that the X-Server kept loading up the restricted driver that came with Ubuntu...and hence kept producing those error messages.

Nullhead told me that if I do not get a large Nvidia screen on boot up, that it is NOT using the new Nvidia driver.  I wasn't getting that screen.  So he told me what to do to remove the restricted driver completely and then reinstall the official Nvidia driver.  So I did that and boom!  I got the Nvidia screen and no more X-server error message.  So I have my OpenGL back and as an added bonus, a couple of my games that were not working are working again.

The downside was that I had to reinstall the X-Fi driver again.  So I was a bit bummed out by that, but last night I got everything going again and my OpenGL apps are working too.

As for real time and the custom kernel...well, I found out the easy way that the kernel isn't real time because when I ticked off "real-time" in Jack, it stops working.  When I un-tick it, Jack started working again.

Amtam was working on trying to find a solution and I think he is going to work on getting the rt-kernel to work.

For the most part IF and only IF I use Jack, the latency isn't that bad.  If I use the ALSA driver directly, certain applications have a bit more latency.  The worst is streaming internet and Java applets.

I been trying to get Mixxx (a DJ mixing application) to work.  However, Mixxx was around in the OSS days and like Audacity, it suffers from the crackling like you and I had in Audacity.  Mixxx is 'supposed' to be Jack compliant.  There is a setting for it in preferences.  But when I set Mixxx to use Jack, I get an error message.  Tonight I am going to try TerminatorX instead.  It too is a DJ mixing program that can use Jack.
 
> In the coming days I want to look very carefully at what my needs are and will be with regards to a useful OS and would like to know if I will be better served if I do go and use the real time kernel, either by ubuntu studio or by replacing the existing kernel with a real time ubuntu kernel. I need to spend more time researching.

Well, Ubuntu Studio comes with the RT kernel...so that does mean most operations will be done in real time on an operating system level.  However, with Jack in the non-rt kernel, the latency isn't bad.  I guess because Jack is a server.

Of course, thusfar I only been doing very basic tasks.  I really don't know what is going to happen if I have Ardour, Terminator X, Hydrogen, Qsynth, and Rosegarden all running at the same time...WITH plug-ins.  Everything could grind to a halt then.
 
 >  At the moment, like you Geo, I am trying out various audio programs to see what suits best for the many tasks in creating, editing, managing my sound files.
 I loaded Ardore the other day and see that this is quite a fantastic program.

Yes, I am doing the same.  Thusfar Ardour is supposedly the best 'heart' program for a Linux based DAW so I really didn't look much into anything else.  At first I was a bit put off because I didn't see the usual EQ and send controls, BUT with Ardour being a plug in based DAW, you can easily create a patch point with a plug-in.  So you only need to put EQ where you needed it and likewise for other effects and processes.  So I guess that does keep Ardour in the Linux mainstream with keeping things as light as possible. 

I am still having a hard time finding my way around it, but I did learn that even though it doesn't do MIDI (you need Rosegarden or another Midi sequencer for that), it DOES respond to Midi control messages and you can map all the controls in Ardour to an outside control surface.  THAT is the BIG feature I liked about Ableton's Live and I am VERY happy to see that Ardour does follow suit in that manner. But even bigger than Ardour is learning about Jack and what it can do.

At first I was put off that everything in Linux is in modules.  Why not have one kitchen sink program like Ableton Live?  Well, I found out really fast that like with component stereo systems, having a "component" DAW is probably a better way to go in the long run.  With Jack you can patch in just about anything you want.  So here in Linux it is VERY easy for me to "Jack" a DJ mixing program into something like Ardour.  Then I can set up Ardour for a 'live format' and I believe it should work out as a good live performance tool.  I still have to put everything to the test.  But what I have seen thusfar does hold some promise.


> In fact, I am finding many many potentially fantastic programs worthwhile to be used in my little developing studio here on the mountain.
When I start looking at all these programs I start to salivate and need my dribble bib 'cause I don't know where to start.
Then I need to read everything about each program, I must suffer from OCD I think??

:lolflag: We have a foamer people!  Mad goat on the mountain!

I know what you mean.  I think the best part is that all of these programs are FULL versions ready to go...boom!  You don't have to deal with working with a crippled demo version or get stuck with a LITE version and only can record two inputs at a time.  You don't like something, you nix it and move on to something else.

Just the other day I found out there were about a DOZEN DJ mixing apps.  So that was a lot to look into.  However I narrowed it down pretty quickly because I wanted a waveform progress display and I wanted it to work with Jack.  Only three seemed to fit the bill then.  Mixxx, DJPlay, and TerminatorX seem to fit the bill.  Two of the three come complete with Ubuntu Studio.   Because of my Jack troubles with Mixxx, I am going to try out TerminatorX tonight.
 
> So, yes at the moment, for me it is a great learning process, with the knowing that I want to learn much and be able to know and fully understand the concepts in building a custom kernel system and what the implications are for enabling or disabling various system and application components.
 
The other reason for me wanting to be able to do this, and I'm sure Geo would be thinking much the same, would be for the reason that I want to be competent enough to fix the problems I create, or at least go someway to solving things when they break. Yes, I think you are all guessing right, in that I am talking about possible creative X-Fi and also video driver breakages.

Oh, I didn't break the video driver.  It really wasn't installed properly in the first place.  Remember the dpkg thing?  Yeah, I think that got me into trouble.  Knocked out my OpenGL.  Took me a few days to notice though :confused:.  But as I said earlier, Nullhead told me what to do and that problem is now fixed.  But I know what you are saying.  I am very worried and careful about what I put on the system because after all this, I DON'T want to break something.   I am very far from figuring out how Linux runs and for a long time I am not even going there.  I wanted to tackle one primary objective and that is to see how the Studio programs worked and performed.  So now that I have reached that goal, I am probably going to spend much time doing a lot of experiments trying all those programs out and seeing what I like to work with.

The good news is that this entire week I have been off of Windows (on my home computer that is).  

 > Again, Amtam and Nullhead, I really want to let you know that I feel very grateful for the help you both have given.
 It is reassuring for me to know that I have such knowledgeable and generous people as you, here in ubuntu world.

Likewise...DITTO here!  MG, I think there is a proper way you can thank someone here.  I noticed it under my avatar, but I don't know how to do it.

Well, I am at work right now so I have to run, my break is almost over and I still have a few things more to do before I can leave today.  Then tonight I will be messing around with Terminator X and Ardour.

Have a good one!

Geo

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-10
> As for real time and the custom kernel...well, I found out the easy way that the kernel isn't real time because when I ticked off "real-time" in Jack, it stops working. When I un-tick it, Jack started working again.

Amtam was working on trying to find a solution and I think he is going to work on getting the rt-kernel to work.

I have some good news and some bad news about that. The good news is that I succeeded in building myself a real-time kernel :)

At least, I think I have. I used [Ingo Molnar's](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ingo_Moln%C3%A1r) RT-patch to patch a vanilla kernel, 2.6.25.8 to be precise. Had to use vanilla, because the patch wouldn't work on the Ubuntu-sources, that being patched already (with other patches, not RT).

The result was a few extra options in compiling the kernel that I didn't have before. This is the most important one:

[img]http://intranet.etin.nl/images/rt-kernel1.png[/img]

It's under Processor type and features --> Preemption Mode.
The last option previously was missing and you could only choose Low-Latency (PREEMPT). This resulted in the following kernel:

[img]http://intranet.etin.nl/images/rt-kernel.png[/img]

As you can see, it now says SMP PREEMPT **RT**, where before the RT was missing.

Now for the bad news: the real-time option in Jack still isn't working....
You can imagine how frustrated I must have felt...

But then I thought of something: what if I try the default build-in RT-kernel from the repositories (linux-image-2.6.24-19-rt)?
Would it work then? Of course I couldn't use the X-Fi for that, so I tried it with my on-board AD1988B Intel HD-audio.

To my surprise, the real-time option in Jack didn't work there either?

So I was using the linux-rt meta-package and kernel at that time and it should have worked, right? All I can think of now is that maybe that infamous rt-option might be broken? Is there perhaps another audio-application that has that option, so I can try it?

This little exercise has cost me so much time, it's just not funny anymore....

If someone can show me a screen shot of a working Jack-with-RT option with a different sound card and the Ubuntu RT-kernel running, I may get some inspiration from it and continue... :lolflag:

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-11
There is still hope! I found that there are several other things you have to do in order for the realtime option in Jack to work:

> After you've got the kernel you still need to set up real-time access for your applications. 

All you have to do for this is give your audio group permissions to access the rtprio, nice, and memlock limits. To do this, you just need to run these commands, which will add some lines to the file /etc/security/limits.conf:

```
sudo su -c 'echo @audio - rtprio 99 >> /etc/security/limits.conf'
sudo su -c 'echo @audio - nice -10 >> /etc/security/limits.conf'
sudo su -c 'echo @audio - memlock 250000 >> /etc/security/limits.conf'
```

I did this on a system at work I have setup with the rt-kernel and it worked! Can't wait to get home to try it on the X-Fi...:)

No guarantees yet, but it looks promising! (to me anyway... ;) )

Sounds GREAT! I hope it works out as well.

Last night I ran into a few hickups.  I am right now trying out some DJ mixing programs and I previewed three of them (the ones that support Jack).  Here are my findings:

1) Mixxx:  Thus far my favorite of the bunch, BUT it is only working with ALSA right now and not Jack.  The big stand out is Tempo Control (That is a DJ term.  Part of DJ mixing requires a way to speed up or slow down songs.  Traditionally that was done by a pitch control.  The problem with pitch is that not only does it raise or lower the speed, it also affects the pitch of the recording.  That isn't good.  Tempo control on the other hand allows you to change the speed without altering the playing pitch.  THAT is a much more desired trait because you don't end up with chipmunks or monsters for the vocals as traditionally would be the case with old analog equipment).  Another standout is that it has an over/under Waveform display (this is the thing I needed OpenGL for).  Having a visual waveform is a guide in lining up the peaks in the music to match the beat.  Having the displays for both mixing channels over and under helps to line up beats both visually and through your hears.  Output control is somewhat limited, but then again, I have not seen it work with Jack to really make an evaluation here.

2) DJ Play:  Only option is to use Jack.  A really nice program and it has quite a few different options for outputs.  DJ Play can even hook up to an outside (physical) mixer.   The downside is that it is a multiple window program and it causes A LOT of XRuns.  (I don't know what XRuns really are, but I know it isn't a good thing).  Audio quality is good, but the xruns cause some skipping.  Another bad thing is the the program doesn't offer "Master Tempo".   It has waveform display but it is very tiny.

3) TerminatorX:  Causes almost NO X-Runs and has a really really good sound quality.  BIG waveform display all on top of each other.  It is LADSPA (plug-in) ready and has a whole slew of effects that can process the signal.  You can have many input sources as well and not just two like with Mixxx or DJ Play.  However, there are three huge problems:  1) No tempo control 2) No FF, REW, search 3) Very limited mixing controls. 4) Cannot redirect outputs to other mixers.  And yes, I have tried to open up multiple instances of Terminator X. Jack will not let you.  I guess they figured that if you can open several mixing sources within the program that having multiple instances of the program isn't necessary.  But without a way to cue up (via headphones), I can't see it as a viable mixing tool.



Overall I find this as a huge bummer!  Out of the three graphical DJ programs meant to run on Jack, none really fit the bill.  

If I could take the intuitive display and function of Mixxx, marry that with DJPlay's outstanding output configuration, AND marry that with the scratching and fantastic sound quality of Terminator X, I would have the perfect DJ mixing program!


Mixx comes the closest though.  I just have to find a way to get Jack working with it.


So that is what I been doing the past day or so.

Mountain_Goat...you may want to take notes of my findings in the case you are interested in DJ mixing type programs.

Geo

---

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-11
jukingeo, the xrun's are all about latencies. Latencies you can lower using the rt-kernel which, by the way, I am running with Jack right now! :)
The realtime-setting can now be used, the options above did the trick.

[img]http://intranet.etin.nl/images/jack_rt3.png[/img]

I'm still trying to get the latencies down. It's now @ 10.7 msec. Probably needs a bit more tweaking in the kernel-options or some other settings I guess. But for now, it's another step in the right direction.

I will try Mixxx and Jack as soon as I fix my nvidia-driver, which got seriously f*cked up in the process.....](*,)

I will try Mixxx and Jack as soon as I fix my nvidia-driver, which got seriously f*cked up in the process.....](*,)

Hello Amtam,

Wow!  You are a Linux guru!  I don't think anyone up to now got the X-Fi to work in the realtime kernel.

While you are right about the latency, there is a battle between performance and sound quality.  If you set the latency low, you will get a faster program response time and as you pointed out, lower Xruns. BUT doing so usually degrades the audio response and distortions.  You get alot of crackles, clicks and pops.

Because I can't get Mixxx to work with Jack and I can't turn up the latency to make the audio clean, I just turn it down to the point where the distortion was bearable so I can use the program.  So it is right now 'good enough' for testing...but I could never use this for a project like this.

I do have to ask you another question in regards to the OSS - ALSA thing.  Supposedly programs such as Mixxx and Audacity were around before ALSA.  More then likely the programs used OSS.  But now since everything is going to ALSA, both programs offer ALSA support to follow suit.  However even though these programs are newer something is wrong with the way they use ALSA.  There is always some kind of crackling artifact in the sound.

Now with newer programs that were made when ALSA was released, they don't seem to have that problem.  They work fine.  Of course Jack works fine too as it too is ALSA based.

It just seems that within Linux there are just SO many issues with sound.

I am hoping that once I get Mixxx to work with Jack that it does have a few output options.  DJ Play REALLY has some NICE output options.  You can set it up for complete mix or you can output each deck independently.  Then you have a cue or monitor buss as well.  This is fantastic because I could either output DJPlay directly to an output hardware mixer and just use DJ Play as a source, or I can use Jack to reroute the signal into Ardour and then use that as a basis for a full remix studio.  So I do hope that Mixxx has that capability.

Anyway, I still have a long way to go with Ardour.  I have much to learn with that one.  It isn't like these DJ programs in which you can figure them out in less than an evening.

Anyway, keep up the good work...it looks like it is paying off.

Well, the only downside would be if that if it does work out, it would look like I would have to do everything over again.  But it may be worth it...because as of now, programs like DJ Play are really unusable because of all the Xruns.

Geo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-12
> **mhenriday said:**
> Anyone else here encountering _new_ problems after installing the latest version of the **non-free flashplugin** (**10.0.1.218+10.0.0525ubuntu1~hardy1**) ? I suddenly lost all sound on websites like **YouTube**, whereas playing from my own audio files with the **VLC Mediaplayer** was not affected. Odd, says

Henri

Hello Henri,

I don't know if you are using the OSS driver with the X-Fi, but that is what happened to me as well.  The new OSS driver knocks out the internet audio.  Naturally that wasn't acceptable for me and I was really to ditch my X-Fi card, but a fellow here by the name of Amtam has written a post on how to get the Creative Beta ALSA driver to work.

So all in all you DID come to the right place.

What you first have to do is go to the first post of this thread created by Nullhead.  You will see the links to get OSS working first.  Forget this section, and go to the bottom where it says "Bottom Section For Advanced Users Only".

Don't get scared by reading that, but follow the link below that to Amtam's procedure to get the driver to work.  I am only on Ubuntu for about 2 months now, and I got my X-Fi to work with the ALSA driver.  The best part is that Amtam and Nullhead are ALWAYS here and will help you out if you get stuck.  But both of them helped me as well as a few others to get the ALSA driver to work.  So far all of us pretty much had a good amount of success.

The reason why Nullhead wrote that it is for advanced users is that you do have to recompile a kernel to get the ALSA drivers to work.  I know this sounds scary and it can be, but again these guys are really really good and helpful.  They WILL see you through it.

Granted, this is NOT a walk in the park, but you should be able to do it in an evening.  But even if you don't have a full evening, you can do it in stages.  I can tell you where you can take "breaks".

The advantage to getting the ALSA driver to work with Hardy is that you WILL get about 90% of audio functioning.  With the OSS drivers, I only had 50% of my audio working.

The added bonus of getting the ALSA drivers to work is that if you do audio and video editing work, it opens the door to the use of Jack which is needed for many of the higher end mixing programs.

Ubuntu USED to be compliant with both OSS and ALSA, but it seems to be shifting more and more towards ALSA.

This is a good thing too because ALSA is a huge project that is creating drivers to make all sorts of sound devices work in Linux.

So if you want to take the plunge and go for ALSA drivers with your X-Fi, just drop us a line.  Amtam and Nullhead are huge helps here and they will get you going.

It is much more work then OSS, but the results are great AND you WILL get your streaming internet audio back.

Geo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-13
> **mhenriday said:**
> **Geo**, ! What gives me pause, however, is that a certain *art nouveau* **Heron** is soon to be replaced by a rather conventional-looking **Ibix** ; will that mandate once again the recompiling of a new kernel ? Our friends at **Creative** certainly haven't made it easier for **Linux** users to enjoy their product ! It certainly is a good thing that users like **Nullhead**, **Temüjin**, and **Amtam** (love that self-portrait !) are here to help their less knowledgeable brothers and sisters !...

Henri


Hello Henri,

I too followed Temujin's guide for OSS because when I first discovered that I had no sound in Ubuntu, I right away researched the fact that Creative was STUCK in the Beta stage for their Linux drivers for the X-Fi card.  The good thing was that the drivers were meant for the newer ALSA format.  But the guys over at OSS were pretty busy too and they launched a driver that is a bit easier to install and get working across many different systems and Linux distributions.

As it is the Creative ALSA drivers were tested to work in ONLY Ubuntu Gutsy (7.10), OpenSuSE 10.2, and one of the AMD 64 bit distributions.

Amtam found a way to 'force fit' the X-Fi to work with the beta ALSA drivers.  I can tell you right now that if you have any thoughts of using the Jack Audio Control Kit for audio or video editing work, you MUST go this route over OSS.

But you are correct, I had an older version of OSS on my system first and it DID work with the internet.  There was an update and BOOM!  My streaming internet went dead.  I updated OSS and from then on in, I NEVER had audio on the internet again with OSS.  But my OSS problems didn't stop there.  Because I am into audio recording, I needed Jack to work.  I had no choice but to either get rid of my X-Fi card for something more compliant or give Amtam's procedure a whirl.

I am glad I did.  As of now I have 90% of my audio working.  There are a handful of games that still cause me trouble  But I just about can do what I was doing in windows because of the ALSA driver.

It really boils down to your needs.  If you only need basic system sounds, if you don't play games and just want streaming internet audio perhaps they are working on a fix for OSS.

But to answer your question...I don't know what will happen with the next Ubuntu release.  More then likely I probably have to come back to Amtam for another fix.   But in the very least that would be the worst case scenario for me.  I would figure within 6 months to a years time in Ubuntu I would know by then if I should make the full switch to Linux.  By then I would make a completely new machine with a known Linux compliant sound device.

It is a band-aid...so I am not going to sugar coat it.  But for me I JUST need to know if I like all the Linux applications.

You want the skinny?  Well, thusfar for pro audio recording work, Linux has tremendous potential and there are many things I like.  But there are just some idosyncracies with many of the programs I tested.  For example I was auditioning Jack based DJ mixing programs and NOT ONE fully suited my needs.  Some had really great features, but were lacking in certain areas.  Weird stuff like that.

However, I am confident that there will be a different wave of thinking and many new people will jump on the Linux train.  So that could just mean improvement for the Linux operating system as a whole in the near future.

Overall, I am pretty impressed with what I am seeing.

My advice to you is to hook up with Amtam and let me set you up with the ALSA drivers.  You will have more control over the card.  For one, as I recall you don't have bass/treble controls with OSS.  You cannot record from an outside source with OSS.  There are just many things you can't do with OSS.  It is a VERY basic driver.  You will thank yourself that you did get the ALSA drivers working.  It is THAT much of a difference.

But it is true that my needs are different than yours.  But I will say that if you are coming from Windows XP, you are going to be disappointed by the lack of control and capabilities you will miss by using the OSS drivers.  In addition, ALSA is the new format OSS will eventually be edged out.  So that is something else to keep in mind.

But do put your trust in Nullhead and Amtam...they are very good at what they do here and they WILL help you out to the best of their ability.

If you still decide on sticking with OSS, then stick with Temujin, he is the authority on OSS.

Anyway, bottom line is good luck getting your sound back.  If you do decide to go with the ALSA I will try to help as well.  But the first thing to do is go to the first page of this thread and go to the "Bottom for Advanced Users".  Click on the document preceeded by Amtam's name.  You will be directed to a thread on how to recompile a custom kernel and install the drivers.  Read it over a couple of times and then come back and let us know what you want to do.

Geo
