When it came time to install the patch X-Fi driver. I downloaded extracted it and ran the ./installer as root.

But it didnt install correctly infact it failed.
configure: creating ./config.status
config.status: creating Makefile.conf
make: *** [all] Error 2
make: *** [install] Error 2
Installation Unsuccessful

Doing a tail -n 50 /var/log/creative-installer.log

Reveals this:

Hello Malb,

1) Were you certain you had a successful compiling of the kernel?
2) Did you have any video problems and if such did you download and install the proper video driver (This is a must do if you have an Nvidia card).

If this part was successful, and you verified that the new kernel does appear in your grub boot list (if you are dual booting).  Then there is no need to redo the kernel over.

What I will say is that it is VERY important that you followed Amtam's instructions on making sure that you delete ANY old instances of the ALSA drivers (or OSS drivers for that matter) you had set up on your card prior to build of the new kernel.  While this doesn't affect the new kernel, it will affect the installation of the Beta X-Fi ALSA drivers on the new kernel.

If you have OSS you can completely remove that using Synaptic.  If you had a previous installation of the Beta X-Fi ALSA drivers, you must go back to Amtam's guide and right after the section on recompiling the kernel there is a small command you have to invoke to remove the old X-Fi drivers.  You must do this in recovery mode as well, because if you did it in the terminal while Gnome was active, more then likely the driver was loaded on and would not have been deleted.

Finally did you make sure you deleted the old Creative folder entirely?

Hopefully I stopped you from recompiling the kernel again.  But if you deleted the new custom kernel, my advice is that if you have OSS, take it off using Synaptic.  Then go to Amtam's guide and read it twice over taking in every step and making sure you know what you have to do.  Then go through it.

As a quick recap this is what you do.

1) Delete your sound drivers if they are OSS in Synaptic otherwise go to #2
2) Read over Amtam's guide and then follow it to the letter
3) Create the custom kernel
4) Update your video driver (if necessary)
5) Go to recovery mode (root prompt) and delete the X-Fi Alsa drivers (you will need to do this even though you were not successful with your installation attempt)
6) Delete the Creative folder
7) Boot up he new kernel and make sure it is operating properly
8  Install the X-Fi drivers
9) Install the streaming internet drivers.

Then you should be good to go with the ALSA drivers.

Note:  The custom kernel build IS NOT a real time kernel.  Amtam is still working on that.  So if you need an rt kernel, I would hold off a bit.

Otherwise you should be good to go.

The only big question or variable is the type of video card you have and you may need a bit of extra assistance with that.

If you do need a video card driver, take care of that issue FIRST.  If you don't and install the video driver AFTER you install the X-Fi drivers, you will blow the X-Fi drivers away and have to redo it.

I hope that all helps clear things up.

I think it may be a good idea to also list your system here and what you have on it.  That will help the guys out more...especially in seeking a proper driver for your video card if it gets knocked out.

Geo

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-13
> **jukingeo said:**
> Hello Amtam (or Nullhead),

I have a question in regards to a particular module I need for sound.  I need something called snd-virmidi on my machine and it doesn't appear to be there.

I have invoked the modprobe command in my terminal and this is what I get:

geo@geo-desktop:~$ sudo modprobe snd-virmidi
FATAL: Module snd_virmidi not found.
geo@geo-desktop:~$ 

In the etc/modules config file I did put the snd-virmidi comment in manually but when I do a modprobe it still comes up as "module not found".

So I know for sure it isn't on my system, but there is very little mentioned here in the Ubuntu forum as well as on-line when I do a Yahoo search in regards to manually installing this module.

Supposedly it should be part of the kernel.  So I am not sure if that creating the custom kernel somehow affected loading that module.

Basically the module allows the use of virtual midi connections between programs.  I need it for a program called JOrgan which allow you to have a graphical interface to switch and change program change messages via midi.  However, this is done internally and not through a physical midi port.  Hence the name SND-VIRtualMIDI.

And Yes, I did put a post here in the Ubuntu Forum about installing the module and thusfar I hadn't had a single response.

Well, I checked my own .config and it's not enabled there. I probably disabled it because I didn't need it. Or it may have been disabled in the first place, because most people won't need it. Anyway, if you need it, you simply have to activate it (as a module) and recompile. It's under Device Drivers -> Sound -> Alsa -> Generic Devices.


> Amtam...how is your progress going on the RT kernel.  So far it looks like you were seeing light at the end of the tunnel.  It would be cool to get the RT kernel working with Jack/ALSA and the X-Fi card, because it looks like I will be needing the RT kernel for some applications

The RT-kernel is running happily here. The latencies on my system are now about 10ms. I don't think it will get any better with this drivers. It says somewhere in the RT-Howto that a restricted driver (like the X-Fi's) may introduce some higer latencies. So 10ms is actually quite good I think. I can get my onboard Intel to about 10ms too, not lower. But some other drivers like the Audigy reach 5 or even 3 ms.

You may try for yourself to see what results you get. If you're gonna recompile anyway because of the snd-virmidi module, you can take 2 steps in one :)

Patching the kernel for RT isn't that hard. Download a vanilla kernel (I used 2.6.25.8 ) from kernel.org and the RT-patch from Ingo Mollnar from [here](http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/rt/).

Make sure the versions match. Then unpack the kernel and rename it to 2.6.25.8-rt7. Then make a copy of it to 2.6.25.8-rt7.orig. The patch expects both of these dirs to exist. Copy the patch to /usr/src, unzip it and apply it:

```
patch -p0 < patch-2.6.25.8-rt7
```

It takes about 1 second to patch. You shouldn't get any errors.

From there on, it's business as usual. Make menuconfig. Check under Processor type and features if High Resolution Timer Support is enabled.
And under Preemption Mode choose Complete Preemption (Real-Time).

The rest is the same as for the 'default' custom kernel. I removed the --append-to-version=-custom, because the -rt7 was added automatically as a version by renaming the kernel-tree.

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-13
The problem seems to be with osutils.c it seems.

```
osutils.c: In function ‘kvirt_to_page’:
osutils.c:340: error: incompatible type for argument 1 of ‘pgd_val’
```

What's on line 340? Maybe it has something to do with your CPU, which has 4 cores. A similar problem exists with people who use ReiserFS.

Perhaps it is a good idea to check the Gentoo wiki entry. I used some of the patches there too, but not all of them.

### Post by Malbojia on 2008-07-13
> **amtam said:**
> The problem seems to be with osutils.c it seems.

```
osutils.c: In function ‘kvirt_to_page’:
osutils.c:340: error: incompatible type for argument 1 of ‘pgd_val’
```

What's on line 340? Maybe it has something to do with your CPU, which has 4 cores. A similar problem exists with people who use ReiserFS.

Perhaps it is a good idea to check the Gentoo wiki entry. I used some of the patches there too, but not all of them.

heres the code block. I'm not a programmer. so i cant see the prob.

```
static inline struct page * 
kvirt_to_page(unsigned long kvirt)
{
    pgd_t *p_pgd;
#if defined(__x86_64__)
    #ifdef pud_offset
    pud_t *p_pud;
    #endif
#endif
    pmd_t *p_pmd;
    pte_t *p_pte;
    struct page *p_page = NULL;
    
    //CTDPF("kvirt_to_kva: kvirt = 0x%lx ", kvirt);
    p_pgd = pgd_offset_k(kvirt);
    if (!pgd_none(*p_pgd)) {
#if defined(__x86_64__)
    #ifdef pud_offset 
       p_pud = pud_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
       p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pud, kvirt);
    #else
       p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
    #endif
#else
        p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
#endif
        //p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
        if (!pmd_none(*p_pmd)) {
#if LINUX_VERSION_CODE < KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
            p_pte = pte_offset_map(p_pmd, kvirt);
#else
            //p_pte = pte_offset_map(p_pmd, kvirt);
            p_pte = pte_offset_kernel(p_pmd, kvirt);
#endif
            if(pte_present(*p_pte))
                p_page = pte_page(*p_pte);
#if LINUX_VERSION_CODE >= KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
            //pte_unmap(p_pte);
#endif
        }
    }

    return p_page;
}
```

```
static inline struct page * 
kvirt_to_page(unsigned long kvirt)
{
    pgd_t *p_pgd;
#if defined(__x86_64__)
    #ifdef pud_offset
    pud_t *p_pud;
    #endif
#endif
    pmd_t *p_pmd;
    pte_t *p_pte;
    struct page *p_page = NULL;
    
    //CTDPF("kvirt_to_kva: kvirt = 0x%lx ", kvirt);
    p_pgd = pgd_offset_k(kvirt);
    if (!pgd_none(*p_pgd)) {
#if defined(__x86_64__)
    #ifdef pud_offset 
       p_pud = pud_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
       p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pud, kvirt);
    #else
       p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
    #endif
#else
        p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
#endif
        //p_pmd = pmd_offset(p_pgd, kvirt);
        if (!pmd_none(*p_pmd)) {
#if LINUX_VERSION_CODE < KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
            p_pte = pte_offset_map(p_pmd, kvirt);
#else
            //p_pte = pte_offset_map(p_pmd, kvirt);
            p_pte = pte_offset_kernel(p_pmd, kvirt);
#endif
            if(pte_present(*p_pte))
                p_page = pte_page(*p_pte);
#if LINUX_VERSION_CODE >= KERNEL_VERSION(2, 6, 0)
            //pte_unmap(p_pte);
#endif
        }
    }

    return p_page;
}
```

Did you download amtam's pre-patched driver?

### Post by Malbojia on 2008-07-13
Well after a few more tries. It was a damn option in CPU support under the menuconfig. It was set to Core 2/New Xeon.

I dont know how it was setup like but when I acquired a older ubuntu .config by default the 3x86/4x86/5x86/6x86 was checked. After resetting it and re running the make. The card driver finally installed. 

On reboot I lost my nvidia driver. Loaded up the "nv" to see if in X aslactl found it and it did. 
Reintstalled the nvidia driver. X.org wont load anymore with the nvidia module. Also it knocks out the ctalsa module once every 3 reboots. I put the project on the back burner for now.

Thank you for the suggestions during my troubles.

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-14
Hi all,
Amtam, before reading your note that you needed the plain jane kernel I downloaded ubuntu RT kernel and after complie and make and so forth I get the following:

> root@paul-desktop:/usr/src/linux# uname -a
Linux paul-desktop 2.6.24-19-rt #1 SMP PREEMPT RT Wed Jun 18 16:35:41 UTC 2008 i686 GNU/Linux
I tried jack, but as you found out it wouldn't work with the RT option set, no matter how I set it up. I tried with different drivers and other setting, but it was the RT that was crashing the jack server.

Then over the past few evenings I took a good look at your lean and mean config file and then proceeded to dive into altering my 2.6.24..3-custom kernel in a similar way, stripping out so so much. It sure did knock back the compile time from nearly one hour to around 20 minutes.

Though I found that it doesn't boot, it can not find the hard drive to mount. I was expecting this as I took out the IDE/MFM options as I thought this was old drive technology. My system is a spanking newy with SATA drives. So I need to look at why this is so a s well. I don't think I need scsi either??.
I found the hard drive section to be vague, with many possibilities for mistakes.

But by stripping down the unneeded or unwanted modules, made the 
"linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_i386.deb"
file go from 205MB down to less than 35 MB which is amazing!!
:lolflag:

Then after reading your success with the RT patch with plain jane kernel I have been busy reading and learning about patching and compiling kernels. And just wanted to let you know I am in the process of doing this now.

So, now that I have read from your post that you need particular componets I downloaded the following
> RT-patch from Ingo Mollnar from 
[http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/rt/patch-2.6.25.8-rt7.bz2](http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/rt/patch-2.6.25.8-rt7.bz2)

plain 2.6.25.8 kernel from 
[http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/linux-2.6.25.8.tar.bz2](http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/linux-2.6.25.8.tar.bz2)
I did the rename so that the kernel matched the patch name, then I patched as required, all went well here.

I guess my question is, since I downloaded this two bz2 files into my home\downloads dir and that is where I unpacked and patched it, can I continue to makeconfig from here or should I get this into the /usr/src folder??

If so, how do I do this, what commands and syntax would I use??

I thought it might be best to ask you gurus before I mess things up too much.
Then I want to clear off some of the other kernels ???

So, running a half baked system again presently, and having some fun, but I think like you said a few posts ago, wasting many hours on it too.

Geo, there is some important info you may want to take a look at regarding real time kernel [here]("http://rt.wiki.kernel.org/index.php/CONFIG_PREEMPT_RT_Patch") and [here]("http://www.digitalhermit.com/linux/Kernel-Build-HOWTO.html") and [here]("http://rt.wiki.kernel.org/index.php/RT_PREEMPT_HOWTO") . I would be interested to hear what you might make of some of what they are saying in terms of latencies and operation with a rt kernel??
Beside that, how are things over in your wide land?

Well, once again, I read your post with interest and look forward to further success.
And I would like to continue to show my appreciation to you Amtam and Nullhead for the ongoing support.


OK, my brain is frazzeled yet again, it's way to late or early in the morning and I need to do work programming when the sun rises agian from it's long slumber behind those hills over yonder! I'm turning into a night owl, or is that a goat owl??

> **mountain_goat said:**
> Hi all,
Amtam, before reading your note that you needed the plain jane kernel I downloaded ubuntu RT kernel and after complie and make and so forth I get the following:

I tried jack, but as you found out it wouldn't work with the RT option set, no matter how I set it up. I tried with different drivers and other setting, but it was the RT that was crashing the jack server.

I'm not sure what you did here.... You say you downloaded the Ubuntu RT kernel? You mean you installed the linux-2.6.24-19-rt kernel-image?
Because I'm not sure a Ubuntu RT kernel-**source** even exists. If it does, I would very much like to know where I can get it...

Then you say you compiled it. Without the sources, that's not possible. Or am I missing someting? The uname -a you posted returned the default date of June 18, so I'm still a bit confused... 

> 
Then over the past few evenings I took a good look at your lean and mean config file and then proceeded to dive into altering my 2.6.24..3-custom kernel in a similar way, stripping out so so much. It sure did knock back the compile time from nearly one hour to around 20 minutes.

Though I found that it doesn't boot, it can not find the hard drive to mount. I was expecting this as I took out the IDE/MFM options as I thought this was old drive technology. My system is a spanking newy with SATA drives. So I need to look at why this is so a s well. I don't think I need scsi either??.


Yes, you **do** need SCSI-support if you only have SATA. I made the exact same mistake you did when I started ripping out stuff :lolflag:
My system is SATA-only too. SATA and SCSI have many similar settings in the kernel.

> 
But by stripping down the unneeded or unwanted modules, made the 
"linux-image-2.6.24.3-custom_2.6.24.3-custom-10.00.Custom_i386.deb"
file go from 205MB down to less than 35 MB which is amazing!!
:lolflag:

Then it's even smaller than mine; which is about 40MB :)

> 
Then after reading your success with the RT patch with plain jane kernel I have been busy reading and learning about patching and compiling kernels. And just wanted to let you know I am in the process of doing this now.

So, now that I have read from your post that you need particular componets I downloaded the following
I did the rename so that the kernel matched the patch name, then I patched as required, all went well here.

I guess my question is, since I downloaded this two bz2 files into my home\downloads dir and that is where I unpacked and patched it, can I continue to makeconfig from here or should I get this into the /usr/src folder??

Move it to /usr/src. It is supposed to be there. Make a symlink to it also, so /usr/src/linux points to the source (2.6.25-8-rt7)

> 
If so, how do I do this, what commands and syntax would I use??

Just copy it using the cp -r command or use a filemanager to do it for you.

> 
I thought it might be best to ask you gurus before I mess things up too much.
Then I want to clear off some of the other kernels ???


You can always remove older kernels, but don't throw away your old shoes until you have new ones... :lolflag:

Made some progress on the latencies-front: they are down to 2.67ms now :)
The X-Fi can use a sample-rate of 192KHz and with that setting I get no xruns anymore.

What was the acronym for SCSI again?  Small Computer SERIAL Interface?  Yes?  I believe Sata is Serial Advanced Technology Attachment.

> **jukingeo said:**

Also, I wanted to ask you something.  What is your opinion of Puppy Linux.  Supposedly I talked to a bunch of guys in the organ forums and they got it to work with Jack and a rt kernel.  However, that doesn't mean anything in regards to the X-Fi.  But supposedly Puppy is really really lightweight and could be entirely loaded into memory, thus it is VERY fast...even on a not so fast machine. No, I never heard of it, but then there are hundreds of distributions out there. It might work, but I very much doubt the X-Fi driver would compile on it. But it can't hurt to try. Who knows, you may have found the Holy Grail! ;)

I think I am now at a point where there is little more to be tweaked with the current state of the driver. For the most of it, it's kinda working, but it's nowhere near 'production-levels' yet. Creative (or better yet, the ALSA-developers) has/have to come up with someting better soon. It's been fun playing and hacking around like this, but I have to admit I'm getting a little bit tired of it all. The fact we all get 'the silent treatment' from Creative doesn't help either. ### Post by amtam on 2008-07-15
> With regard to moving the files, I had already tried to use file manager with copy and paste, but I could only copy and when I pointed to the usr/src/ directory I could no longer paste, so I thought I would need to do this vis the command line and probably with a sudo as well. I don't know the commands at all still, even though I spent many years with DOS and CP/M system. Hey Geo, old Kaypros, now that brings back memories!! I bet Amtam doesn't know what a Kaypro is, hehehehehe. What about Osbornes and oh yeah, I had an Atari 520ST and 1040ST, they were the bees knees in those days, especially good for music as it had inbuilt midi.

Hehe... CP/M was a little before my day, but I 'grew up' using DOS, so typing commands and stuff like that doesn't scare me, unlike most 'new' computer users who only know how to click on buttons and other GUI-stuff..... ;)

I had some experience with Commodore64 and MSX-machines, but that was mostly to play games with.

About the copy/past of the source to /usr/src:

```
gksu nautilus
``` will give you a root-filemanager so you can copy the files.

### Post by borlosky on 2008-07-15
> **NullHead said:**
> [FONT="Tahoma"]
[CENTER][SIZE="5"]***Readme***[/SIZE][/CENTER]

Creative has attempted and failed at making a driver for their X-FI sound card line. They finally broke and released datasheets to both OSS and ALSA (**A**dvanced **L**inux **S**ound **A**rchitecture). Oss, obviously, has released a driver before ALSA has even started development. Therefore I choose not to have Creative's section included on this page anymore. It causes users too much trauma trying to get it to work and OSS works great anyways.

Enough chatter ... here is the link to Temüjin's wonderful guide on how to get your high-end soundcard working:

[CENTER]
[SIZE="5"]***Guide***[/SIZE][/CENTER]

Use this guide to install OSS: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2)

If OSS fails to install or update use this guild to help you uninstall it: [http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054](http://4front-tech.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2054)

[SIZE="2"]**This guide supports all versions of Ubuntu from 7.04 and up.**[/SIZE]

[CENTER][SIZE="2"]!!!BOTTOM SECTION FOR ADVANCED USERS **ONLY**!!![/SIZE][/CENTER]

Amtam wrote a guide here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675)
You can use that to try to setup Creative's officially unofficial driver.[/FONT]

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-16
> **amtam said:**
> Hardy isn't the best release Canonical has ever released, to put it mildly... It looks like it was rushed. Lots of unfinished and/or broken stuff.

Yeah, I am learning that quite the bit of my frustrations with Linux was because I came in at a bad time.  It does still make me wonder if I should roll back to 7.10.  But then I get the advice from others to switch over to 64Studio or OpenSuSE.

Well, strange as it may seem, I am actually writing this response via a different OS yet.  Yup!  This message comes to you from Puppy Linux.  Don't get scared...I am not leaving Ubuntu ...yet.  What Puppy is for is for another project I am working on.  It is going to be for JOrgan (A virtual pipe organ).

So this does mean I am probably going to set up another box.  I am probably going to use my really old computer for it considering Puppy can run entirely in RAM.  Like Ubuntu, Puppy does have a Live Disk (that is how I am here via Puppy of course).  BUT it can do something Ubuntu can't do and that is save all my preferences and system settings to a file on the hard drive.  Ahhhhhh!  Nice feature.  Saves me from having a triple boot system.

At any rate Puppy will eventually go on a USB Flash Memory and I will set everything up here on this machine, then I will just transfer the completed project over to the "organ" computer and all I have to do is configure the I/O devices.

But getting back on track, yeah, Ubuntu is doing some peculiar things.  To be honest with you, for audio work, I am really looking for something lighter weight still.  I had to go into Preferences in Ubuntu and I shut off a WHOLE bunch of things.

Believe it or not, that did solve quite a few problems.  I DON'T need special effects in having screens wipe and fade in/out...etc.

> 

I don't know, my crystal sphere is murky about that... ;)
I'm guessing Creative will release a new driver just before Intrepid will come out. It will work on Hardy, but won't compile on Intrepid... 

L'histoire se répète, as the French would say....

LOL!  Always a step behind.  


> 

No, I never heard of it (ed Puppy Linux), but then there are hundreds of distributions out there. It might work, but I very much doubt the X-Fi driver would compile on it. But it can't hurt to try. Who knows, you may have found the Holy Grail! ;)

OH, I just booted up Puppy tonight.  I have not figured out where the sound configurations are.  But I do know that there are guys using it for audio work and it seems to be a favored OS for the guys in the virtual pipe organ realm over at the JOrgan (and Miditzer) forums.  One thing I can say for sure...it is faster than Ubuntu.  And Windows?  Heh Heh...I am not even going to go there.  I logged into Windows tonight to "get something",  and being two weeks on straight Ubuntu, I REALLY notice how slow Windows is now.  ...Let's try that again...SLLLLOOOOWWWW!

> I think I am now at a point where there is little more to be tweaked with the current state of the driver. For the most of it, it's kinda working, but it's nowhere near 'production-levels' yet. Creative (or better yet, the ALSA-developers) has/have to come up with someting better soon. It's been fun playing and hacking around like this, but I have to admit I'm getting a little bit tired of it all. The fact we all get 'the silent treatment' from Creative doesn't help either. If only a Creative-developer would give some tips or feedback, but **nothing** of the kind.... :(

Amtam...you did fantastic.  I didn't think I would see the day that I could get my X-Fi running in Hardy.  I was ready to jump ship (to a another distro) and buy a new audio device.

I was really worried though about going through all the trouble and what if I didn't like the Linux applications (as compared to Windows).

But thusfar, now that the X-Fi is working, I am beginning to really see what can be done.

I mentioned that these were my goals for Linux and these needs needed to be met:

1) Do internet surfing and standard everyday office and 'housekeeping' work.  (PASSED but not with flying colors due to some bugs in Ubuntu).

2) Can be set up as an OS for a virtual pipe organ project.  (PASSED.  As others proved it can be done).

3) Can do DJ mixing work.  (ALMOST PASSED --I still have one or two issues to work out with MIXXX...but that is going to be my program of choice.

4) Can do studio audio and video editing (Jury is still out on this one as I have much research to do).

5) Games. With the new Nvidia driver and the X-Fi working, I have good video and audio on 90% of my games now. (ALMOST PASSED).

So with that much progress made in the past few weeks, I can pretty much say that overall I can do 80% of what I did in Windows here in Linux.  Thus Linux overall is winning me over.  I am not fully there yet.

So far I am really liking the audio applications and getting used to Jack.

Now Ubuntu on the other hand...has been stellar with community support, but unfortunately Hardy isn't winning awards with me right now.  But just for the sake of argument I am going to keep it on my system (for now).  But if Intrepid is going to be just as buggy as Hardy...I most definitely will drop Ubuntu for something like 64Studio.

But thanx to you and everyone here in this thread I can test out my Linux audio/video applications and for that Ubuntu is good enough.  

Whew!  It's getting late and I really haven't been messing around with Puppy too much.  One thing is for certain.  They don't call the web browser FireFox in Puppy, they call it SeaMonkey.  Outside of a a few different graphics and item placement it is very similar to FireFox.  It IS a Mozilla release that is for sure.  I am wondering if Mozilla made it specially for Puppy because of it's "lightweight" requirement.

G'Night.

Geo

Hello MG,

I am not sure what Puppy is using, but the interface is QUITE different from Ubuntu. But I made a nice discovery today.  

I brought my Puppy to work today, stuffed it in the CD-Rom, and booted it. (ok, I know...that doesn't sound too good if you  are an outsider just looking in.  JFYI Puppy is another Linux distribution, so don't call animal abuse).

So guess what?  I HAVE SOUND HERE AT WORK! With ALSA no less.  Off of the Live CD! Using the built in motherboard sound device!!!

Anyway I quickly looked up the sound on my machine.  I thought it was Realtek, but I know that wouldn't work in Linux. But I found out I have something called SoundMAX.  It isn't listed on the ALSA site, so I am REALLY surprised it is working like this.

Man, I tell you, I am bummed out!  I get sound on my computer at work first try WITHOUT installing the CD!  The internet STREAMS too!  And it took over a month to get sound working at my house.

But Ubuntu wouldn't do that with the Live CD here at work either.  I am sure it probably would work if I played around with the configuration settings, but I am not installing it here at work.  But I am AMAZED that Puppy Linux worked with the sound right off the live CD.

So far it seems to have full housekeeping...so this Puppy is well trained.  The only thing I had a problem with is trying to get a printer connected.  But then again my machine is on a network here at work...so I don't have a printer directly hooked up to my machine.  But I will say in that department Ubuntu was stellar.  I just hooked up my printer, and Ubuntu just searched for and downloaded the Linux driver.

Well, I best be stopping here since this is by right an Ubuntu discussion, but so far I am happy with Puppy Linux and the next step is to put it on a flash memory stick get Jack working with it.

Anyway, let me know if you also have success with the RT kernel.  If so, then it looks like I may be doing this ALL over again.

G'Day

Geo

### Post by tobsol on 2008-07-16
> **NullHead said:**
> You're saying; when you start a movie in the default movie player, it kills all the other sound and doesn't even play the movie? I would try playing the movie in VLC. You can install VLC through the "Add/Remove programs" menu selection and search for VLC and you can install it that way.

No, sorry if I was unclear. The system behaves as if only one application can access sound at the time. So if I start playing an MP3 first, mplayer will complain about the device being busy, and vice-versa. 

It's as back in the days when soundcard-support was really horrible for linux and every time any application used /dev/dsp no other could use it until it was free again.

---

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-16
Hi Tobsol
> No, sorry if I was unclear. The system behaves as if only one application can access sound at the time. So if I start playing an MP3 first, mplayer will complain about the device being busy, and vice-versa. 

It's as back in the days when soundcard-support was really horrible for linux and every time any application used /dev/dsp no other could use it until it was free again.I believe this will be the case if you choose to you OSS.
And that is one of the many beauties of alsa, (if you can get it going with your X-Fi card)

If you have need for better control, more outputs, use of inputs then I would encourage you to look at going the alsa way. See Amtam's HowTo guide.

There are two different paths to take.
1 . Patch standard ubuntu kernel and recompile and install X-Fi driver,
2 . Or if you want real time, use vanilla kernel with rt patch then compile and then install X-Fi driver.

We, (read I) are still messing about with many things, settings and testing it for working in jack control with the various applications. But all in all, it seems to be a working solution and one that for me, would not want to even go own the OSS path, though I understand that going OSS may suit others. I understand that OSS is on its way out and that alsa will be the primary sound module.

But, I am only a beginner and have a lot of catching up and learning to do within Linux.

Have fun with what ever you do....

mountain goat ~ sitting in my little mountain shelter looking out upon the green hills

I'm also interested in certain organ apps, but mostly modular synthesis where I can build the sounds from blocks and then build the control surface and link it back to my midi controller, Kawai K5000s and phat-boy.

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-16
> **tobsol said:**
> No, sorry if I was unclear. The system behaves as if only one application can access sound at the time. So if I start playing an MP3 first, mplayer will complain about the device being busy, and vice-versa. 

It's as back in the days when soundcard-support was really horrible for linux and every time any application used /dev/dsp no other could use it until it was free again.


Hello Tobsol,

Mountain_Goat summed it up just as good or if not better than I could say it.

Because I was very much looking into Linux for mostly audio (and some video) editing work (I have Ubuntu Studio on my machine), I found my self constantly struggling with the OSS driver.  I fould out that I needed Pulse Audio to get my game cards working, then I needed something else to get streaming internet to work. Then when there was a little tiny update, things would get knocked out.  Supposedly there is now a conflict with the new internet drivers and Pulse, so my internet got knocked out.  Then OSS updated the driver and I no longer needed pulse for my games, but still the internet didn't work with sound.  But the biggest problem was that Jack Audio Control Kit didn't work with OSS and thus, I couldn't access many of the cool audio programs that came with Ubuntu Studio.

So when I discovered Amtam's guide to get the Creative/ALSA drivers to work, I quickly ditched OSS and jumped on the bandwagon.

I can say that through Amtam's guide (and much help within this thread) that I now presently have 90% of my sound operational (as compared to my sound set up in Windows).  There are a few issues I still have with certain games, and some of my hardware isn't Linux compliant.  But I am ALMOST there.

In the end I am probably going to set up another machine JUST for Linux sound creation and editing.  Then I am going to get a proper audio interface, but what my goal was to do was to get some sound on my current computer set up so this way I can use Jack and all the nice programs that came with Ubuntu Studio.  I needed that in order to see if it was feasible to switch from Windows to Linux for the purpose of audio/video editing and creation.

I don't know what your needs are, but even if you just want to get some sound for your games and get the internet to work, I still think it is worth it to recompile the Ubuntu kernel and use the ALSA X-Fi drivers.

Another point that Mountain Goat brings up is that OSS is the old and ALSA is the new.  OSS is eventually going to be left in the dust.  Already many of the popular sound/video based distributions of Linux are geared to using ALSA.   Puppy Linux, which is another distribution I am using (for a separate project) is also using ALSA for audio.

So probably the best thing to do is enlist in Amtam's help and follow his guide.  I was petrified of the words "recompile kernel".  But it really did sound worse than the actual procedure.  Really the only big hang up is if you have an issue with the video card and this is discussed here as well.

Anyway, hope that helps you out a bit.

Geo

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-07-17
OSS isn't really being left behind ... more like neglected. OSSv4 is actually the best version of OSS to date and it is actually worth comparing to ALSA now. 

Our problem with OSSv4 is that there are beta drivers that don't like to play nice with the rest of the system. OSS is more for audio fillies. 

ALSA is more welcomed to operating systems like Ubuntu, but OSS is not to be left out. 

OSS is still under development, by the way. ;)

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-17
> **mountain_goat said:**
> Geo
Wow, must be the middle of the night for you, ha, you will turn into a night owl like me if you're not careful Geo.

Yeah, I have quite a few projects going on at the same time.  I shifted most of my interests towards Linux right now.

> As far as my compiling is going, I need to ask Amtam a question, but haven't got time right  now to ask, so maybe later tonight.

Sounds good.  Just let me know if you do get something going. As you know I been spending most of my time testing apps out lately.

> PS - I did play around with some of the organ apps last week, and there was one that seems to be ok, but as I'm in that other OS (you know which one) I don't remember the name of it, but there seemed to be some great patches in it!

There are a few programs.  Aeolus is one that is a 'complete' organ.  You just hook up your sound device and a midi keyboard and instant organ.  The trouble with Aeolus is that same simplicity factor.  You can't modify it or expand on it.   There is another organ program called Genpo, but I have not delved too much into that one.   The favored one right now is JOrgan.  JOrgan is fully customizable and it is Soundfont based.  That means you can change the sounds.  You can also change the button layouts (and looks via organ skins).  You can add ranks/stops whenever you want to expand on a virtual organ.

What is really cool is that you can go out there and buy an old cheap organ console.  The organ doesn't even have to work.  Then you strip out the internal components and wire all the key contacts and switches up for MIDI.  Then you can go into JOrgan and map out each and every switch on the console to whatever rank/stop you want to control through the Soundfont synth.

If you don't know what I mean by Soundfonts, in simplest terms a Soundfont synth is like a Rompler.  Yeah, it can play back sampled sounds.  So it is very well possible that you can go to your favorite church and directly sample it's organ and then recreate the sound virtually with JOrgan.

You can even direct the output of JOrgan to multiple outputs on a sound card.  So you very well could have 6 or 8 channels and mimic organ chambers.  Thus when you play you can re-create the spacial illusion a real pipe organ gives. (This was a feature that always amazed me on my Church's organ, which is electronic, but it does have a 6 channel sound system.  Most of it's sound came from the rear of the church, but you can set side and front cues as well.  The front speakers were set to a solo or swell manual and the 'shutter' effect was most pronounced from the front of the church.  Needless to say even for an electronic organ, the sound is VERY realistic).

So you say you prefer the sound of a Hammond organ vs a pipe organ?  No problem...JOrgan has a Hammond skin and you can map out the drawbar sliders too.  Just load in some Hammond Soundfonts.

You like the 60's Farfisa sound?  No problem.  Just load up some Farfisa sounds via the Soundfonts.

The only bad thing that I can think of with JOrgan is that it isn't extremely intuitive.  It has a large learning curve and there isn't any instant gratification as you would have with Aeolus. But with power comes a price.  Being that organs are my favorite instruments...I am taking the plunge.

Geo

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-17
Hi Freeloader,
When you go into menuconfig, go to
[COLOR=Blue]   Device drivers --->[/COLOR]
then
[COLOR=Blue]  Sound --->[/COLOR]
Where you should find
[COLOR=Blue]   < > Sound Card Support
          Advanced Linux Sound Architecture  --->
          Open Sound System  --->[/COLOR]


Select the alsa option and set the flags as Amtam describes.
Then when you have completed, save and exit

Hope this helps..

mountain goat ~ having a lazy day

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-18
> **freeloader105 said:**
> Then I tried uninstalling the old unsuccessful installation of the driver as such:
'cd /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers
./configure
make uninstall'

but it wouldn't work.

What exactly do you mean with 'it wouldn't work'? You have to be more specific. Did you get any errors? Were you unable to go into the directory? I need to know more.... ;)

> 
Then I just removed the dir with 'dir -r /opt/Creative'


That won't do the trick; the driver remains installed like that.
You **have** to uninstall it properly before you can continue.

> Then I reextracted the tar which was at /home (not a necessary step, but whatever) and tried to install it with 'tar zxvf XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz && cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18' (and what does the the 'cd ~' just before it do?) but I got the following error:
'tar zxvf XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18_patched.tar.gz && cd XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18'

I then deleted the Creative dir and tried reinstalling the drivers but got the same result. Any ideas?

The ~ is short for your home-dir. It doesn't really matter how you get in that dir; you can do it manually too.

Again, boot into **recovery-mode** to uninstall the driver. Uninstalling in normal-mode won't work, especially not after a failed attempt. Then go to the /opt/Creative/XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18/drivers dir and try again:

```
./configure
```
```
make uninstall
```

Then remove the dir, reboot and try again. You don't have to extract the driver again; just go into the XFiDrv_Linux_US-1.18 dir in your home-dir and type

```
./installer
```

That is, if you succeeded in building the kernel correctly :)
Really, that was the hard-part to begin with; installing the driver itself is pretty basic.

> Perhaps Amtam has to edit the guide to include that information as to what to do here.

Come on you guys; it is even explained in the screenshots itself:

> Arrow keys navigate the menu. <Enter> selects submenu's.

;)

> **mountain_goat said:**

Hello MG,

Yeah it sux!  I just wasn't paying attention because the memory card and the first hard drive have a similar partition set up.  So when I started Puppy over gparted defaulted back to the first hard drive on my system.  It was only after I started the format that I noticed the first partition started with the number 79 (80gig) instead of 37 (4gig).  So right there I knew exactly what happened.  But it was a little too late.  

But I am trying to look on the upside of this disaster. It is opening the door to changes and improvements.  For one  Windows was really getting slow and as anyone can tell you, a good fresh install is good every once in a while to get speed back up.  However, since it is going to be a fresh install, I am going to put on a 'less bloated' virus and spyware protection, because for internet and email work I am going to use Linux exclusively from now on.

As for audio, well, now is a good time as any to pull out the X-Fi because it was a pain to set up on my Dell machine.

My Dell originally came with a special LITE version of the Soundblaster Live card.  It worked great with the system, but I found out that it isn't a true Soundblaster Live that you would buy in the store.  It was a specially made downgraded OEM version that didn't come with the soundfont synth.  Naturally Dell never told anyone about this.  I made the discover with my foray into the world of Miditzer (a virtual organ project).  With Miditzer I NEEDED the soundfont synthesizer.  Fluidsynth didn't work very well with Windows back then AND even to this day.   BUT Fluidsynth works great in Linux.  Other than that even the OEM Soundblaster Live card had surround sound and a 6 channel output.

So I did a bit of digging on the ALSA site and the OEM Dell Soundblaster Live card IS supported via ALSA.  Another thing is that the Soundblaster Live also has connections on the back for a MIDI cable.  The X-Fi doesn't.  The Midi function is also supported with ALSA, (where as the X-Fi isn't).  Finally, the OEM Soundblaster Live was MADE for my machine, and reinstallation would be a snap.

What I would loose is the X-Fi's 96/192khz capability.  (It is 192 in Windows with the digital inputs in 2 channel mode.  So yeah the 192k function was pretty much stripped down).  The OEM Soundblaster Live would NEVER be suitable for a recording sound device.  BUT it would be absolutely fine for playback.

Now my machine has firewire and that is supported in Jack via FreeBob.  I was looking into a really cool firewire piece made by Focusrite called the Saffire.  It does have 96/192 capability and it has a 4 in / 6 out with 2 XLR inputs.  Sure it is a far cry from the Alesis IO26 mega-box I have right now.  But I MAY still keep that for Windows.

Still I may just go all out and get a really good card (even though I can't afford it) such as something from RME.

Regardless, the decision is final with the X-Fi.  It was too much of a hassle to set up in both Windows and here in Linux.  As it is while I do commend everyone's efforts here with the X-Fi, the plain and simple fact is that it really isn't supported well and I am not even on an RT kernel (which I could be with using the OEM card or buying another Linux compliant card)! Also, what will happen when Intrepid comes out?  What if I want to use JackLab or 64Studio instead of Ubuntu?  I will always have to worry if the card will work or not.

So I am going to bid adieu to the X-Fi and it is going to go up on Ebay this weekend.

Hopefully I can fully come to a decision with what to do this weekend as I can't do much because the OEM Soundblaster is at my job from when I installed the X-Fi and I am home for the next three days (I have a 4 day work week). Maybe I will get lucky and find a good all in one solution, but thusfar the Focusrite Saffire I like the best, but it isn't a PCI audio device.  So there would be the issue with trying to get my system sounds and internet sound through it because it can only use Jack via FreeBob.

One solution to that issue would be to install the OEM Soundblaster live and pipe its outputs into the Saffire and connect my speakers to the Saffire.  Sure Jack would have to run all the time, but it WILL work.  I could invest in a small patch bay so I can easily reconfigure the system for audio recording work.  Yeah, it is a bit of extra work to do, but it would be considerably cheaper than an RME card.

Decisions, Decisions.  But I am going to have to make one this weekend because I DO need to get my Windows back up.

I will let you know what I will do.  But I am pretty sure that the X-Fi is going to be gone.

So if everyone is reading in, thanx again for your help...but given the nature of my work and now I do know for sure I am going to stay with Linux (but not necessarily Ubuntu) I think I am better off just being realistic and getting serious and get rid of the X-Fi.

So what made my decision to stay with Linux for sure?  Well because of several pleasant finds.

1) Puppy Linux:  I love it, it is small, it is fast and it will handle the organ project easily
2) I managed to get Mixxx working beautifully on multiple outputs through Jack (with the X-Fi no less) and I believe it is a suitable mixing program for me to work with.
3) Ardour with it's many options and Midi assignable controls along with other outside applications does look like it does pose close competition to Ableton's Live.
4) I heard that you can set up any RT multi-media based Linux distribution with Wine and run Windows based applets and soft synths through Jack.  In fact JackLab comes with this feature set up already.  So slowly, I do see more and more that I can do in Linux.  I am going stick with it.  So the X-Fi is going to be the first casualty from the Windows set up.

So while I thank everyone for helping I do have to give extra props to Amtam and Nullhead.   Both of you extensively helped me out enough that I was able to get Jack working and could try out the programs to the extent that now I do know that I am going to stay with Linux.  It is just that the future of Ubuntu with me is still unclear.  Both JackLab and 64Studio have extremely strong multimedia ties that do surpass Ubuntu Studio.

But for now I am going to stick with Ubuntu Studio until I get Windows back up and running again. No sense in mucking about here and doing something wrong with my Linux setup.

So, now it is onward to find a new sound system for my machine.

Have a good day!

Geo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-18
> **freeloader105 said:**
> Hey amtam, the sound works now. Thanks! I just had to reboot for the changes to take effect. The one thing I'm not sure works yet is mic recording. I tried recording a sound with 'Sound Recorder' but nothing played back. And yes, it's all plugged in correctly, not set to mute either. Any ideas?

Congradulations on getting up and running!

What you probably have to do with all your applications now is set them all to ALSA.  If you used OSS prior and tried to use Sound Recorder, you may still have it set to OSS.  This is true of Jack as well.  Make sure you set it to ALSA.  Another thing, go into your terminal and type "Alsa Mixer".  This will pull up all the controls to operate the X-Fi.  Make sure that your mix input is selected AND it's level control is up.  I found that I had to push my controls up on just about all my outputs and the inputs.   Another thing is that you can only record from ONE input at a time with the X-Fi.  So you have to make sure that the microphone is not only set to record but that nothing else is either.

Once you get your levels set in ALSAMIXER, you can then find a graphical alternative to put on your taskbar so you don't always have to go into the terminal.

That SHOULD help, but mind you, I usually don't record from mic sources so I have not tested the X-Fi in's and out's.  BUT I have tested Ardour to record a mix I made...but that was through Jack...and not the X-Fi, so it doesn't count.

Well, Enjoy your new drivers on the X-Fi, it will truly open up a door to a world of applications which you couldn't access with OSS. 

Geo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-18
> **freeloader105 said:**
> 

What do you mean mix input? Nearly all the outputs and inputs in my ALSAMIXER are set to mid-high levels. And I don't see any mix input either. Have a look:


Sorry, my bad.  Can you see the top menu in the left corner?  Third item down says [Playback] Capture All

Use the tab key to go to [Capture].  That is what I meant about mix input.  That was my bad because I am using a gui volume control right now.

Anyway, if you notice all the controls change and only one is labeled in red.  THAT is the active input.  You can use the arrow keys to move to the input you want (lets say the mic) and then push down on the space bar to active it the input.  As I said, you can only have one input in the mix. While you are there you can move the volume up just until it touches the red.  Now go into your recording application and try and record something, you should be good.

> 
Interestingly enough, though, when I turn up the microphone's volume all the way, I can hear myself speak. Kind of a loopback. Though neither gnome-sound-recorder nor Audacity will actually record anything. gnome-sound-recorder doesn't even give you a choice as to which sound system to use.

Yeah, that is not a good thing.  Your mic does have a foldback so you can feedback on yourself if you are not careful. The best thing to do if you are going to use the mic with an application that can play back and record at the same time, you are best off going back to the playback settings of AlsaMixer and move all the way to the end.  You will see a volume control for mic there.  Bring it down all the way, then you shouldn't have that problem.

If you are getting sound through the mic then perhaps it is already set for capture (record).  So more than likely if you really turned up the mic high in play back and in capture settings it is set low, that could be a reason why it isn't recording very well.  As I said above, just raise up the mic level to about where it just turns red in the capture setup, then go back to the playback setup and turn the mic down.

From there go into your recording application and set it up to record for the mic (if needed).  In most cases all you will be presented with is a way to 'capture' audio.  You may have gain and volume controls in your application too and that needs to be checked as well.

Thus with all said and done, you SHOULD be able to record.

Oh!  Wait a second! Are you using Jack and a Jack based application?  If so, then you may have to open up Jack's connection tab and virtually make the connection between your sound card (capture input) and the application you want to record with.  If you don't you will not record anything.

Hope that helps!

---

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-18
> **macuser2000 said:**
> I tried the guides that are at the front of the thread, however none of them worked for me, does anyone have another guide that works?

Did you scroll down on that first page and check out the link below the part that says:

"Bottom Section for Advanced Linux User's Only" ?

That is the guide you are really after.  I know that title heading can scare most away, but truly if you want to get an X-Fi to work in Ubuntu, that is the best way.

Yes, you are going to have recompile a kernel, yes you are going to have to download and update your video driver, yes you are going to have to manually install the X-Fi Alsa Drivers.  It WILL take a long time, I am not going to sugarcoat it.  BUT the results are worth it.  I found that by using that procedure over ALL of the OSS procedures that I was able to get about 85% to 90% of my applications with sound.  The best I could with OSS was 50%.

So yeah, it is worth it.  If you do ANY kind of audio and video editing work you are going to need the ALSA drivers.  Simply put most of those programs work with Jack and Jack doesn't like OSS.

The procedure should work unless you have a really out there video card or a strange proprietary mother board configuration.

Anyway, Amtam wrote that guide and he is always here to help.  He got me going.  Nullhead is also very knowledgeable and pitches in as well.   There is also Mountain_Goat and myself that can help since we already been through the procedure and successfully got the X-Fi to work.

Best thing to do is read over that guide and if you have any questions come back and ask. When you are ready to take the plunge there will be someone here to help you out.

Geo

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-19
> **legatoistheman said:**
> Hey everyone, I followed the guide to get OSS working and it worked great, sound worked and also worked in wow using wine, however when i did addendum two from this guide to get sound in flash to work [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4874981&postcount=2)  i rebooted and not only does sound in flash in firefox not work, i cant even see flash video in firefox anymore, also sound does not work in world of warcraft anymore but it does work with a movie or song on my system.

Hello Fred,

Sorry to hear of your troubles, but this is EXACTLY what happened to me.  In the interim of when the first OSS guide was made up, OSS released a new driver.  With that new driver, you no longer need fixes such as pulse audio to run games and the like. I also had system sounds with the new version of OSS. BUT the big problems were that Jack (a program needed to operate some audio manipulation and recording applications) and the internet wasn't working.  In trying to fix the problem, I just became more and more frustrated with OSS.

My suggestion is to go to the first page of this thread and go past the guides for OSS and look at the very bottom that says:

"Bottom Section for Advanced Users Only".

Don't let that scare you away and just click on the guide in that section.  This guide is the 'manual' to get the Creative Beta ALSA drivers to work with the X-Fi. 

I am not going to sugarcoat it.  The task is intensive, but Amtam did an excellent job at writing the guide and if you follow it, you should have success.  With the ALSA driver you SHOULD get about 90% of the items on your machine working,  Why not 100%?  Well, I don't know.  Some programs out there still run better with OSS than ALSA, but I couldn't live without my streaming internet and I wanted sound for my audio applications (Jack) and my games.  The ALSA drivers got me the furthest.

If you do want to take the plunge, read Amtam's guide first and then ask questions here on anything that you are not clear on.  Both Amtam and Nullhead visit the site often and will lend a helping hand.

Then follow the guide. Depending on your video driver it most likely will get knocked out.  If you have an Nvidia card, it is as simple as going to their site and downloading the Linux driver for your card.  We can tell you how to do that if that is the case.  The important rule is that if you do have a problem with your video STOP THERE!  DO NOT attempt to install the X-Fi drivers without fixing the video first.  If you do it the other way around, the video driver will knock out the X-Fi driver.

Again, I don't want this to sound scary.  It isn't easy, but to put you at ease, I am only about 2 months on Linux and of that I spent 1 month trying to get OSS to work with everything.  Whereas with following Amtam's guide I had my sound up to 90% operational within a couple of days.

Also if you do decide to go down this route, you MUST uninstall all the OSS drivers and associations from your machine before starting.  You should be able to do it within Synaptic.

Hope that helps!

Geo

> john@john-desktop:~/Desktop$ sudo dpkg -i oss-linux-4.0-1016_i386.deb
Selecting previously deselected package oss-linux.
dpkg: regarding oss-linux-4.0-1016_i386.deb containing oss-linux:
 oss-linux conflicts with libflashsupport
  libflashsupport (version 1.9-0ubuntu1) is present and installed.
dpkg: error processing oss-linux-4.0-1016_i386.deb (--install):
 conflicting packages - not installing oss-linux
Errors were encountered while processing:
 oss-linux-4.0-1016_i386.deb

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-20
> **jaktar said:**
> That's what I did previously.  I know I've read of people enabling multiple cards (separate discreet cards though).  I'll give it a shot, I'm sure it'll work just fine now.

Multiple sound cards is a very touchy issue in Linux.  The worst scenario is if you have two of the SAME card.  I remember a fellow over in the JOrgan forums that had two identical Soundblaster Live cards and it wouldn't work, primarily because Linux didn't know which card was which.  Then he switched one of his cards to an Audigy and then Linux could identify the card but a strange thing happened, upon successive boot ups, the cards positioning changed places.  Since more then likely your sound devices are not identical, you could be having a problem like this.

Note: (JOrgan is a program that allows your computer to operate a virtual pipe organ.  This is a classic scenario where you would have more than 2 channels of output)  

I believe there was a file (which escapes me as of now) that can edited so this way ALSA knows which device is which.

I THINK the file was Modprobe.conf  I THINK the commands are something like this (someone is going to have to help me out here):

alias sound-slot1 snd-"your 1st card"
alisa sound-slot2 snd-"your 2nd card"

Again, I am not sure 100% what the commands are, but supposedly when Ubuntu boots up, it will know how to set up the sound cards.

That is, of course if you DO want to try to get both cards to operate.  If you want to use one over the other, then I would do as the other's say and disable the motherboard sound card through your machine's bios.

Well, in the very least you can follow this lead when searching for configuring multiple sound cards.

Good luck and hope you get it all working.

Geo

---

### Post by topsub on 2008-07-20
I believe this is my first post to ubuntu forums but i have browsed and read for maybe 2 or so months.

i got my xfi creative Extreme music sound card to work with my install of 8.04.

I had to do what i believe a round about way.

My install was on a fresh install on my desktop.

I have 750 gig hard drive that is just ubuntu. Along with windows on my 74 gig raptor and then another 750 gig drive for data.

My problem was once i got the sound working i couldn't get my nvidia card working again. I was stuck at like 800 x 600 on my 24 inch screen and it sucked! So i finally got around it and here is how i did it below

I install fresh install ubuntu. 
Used this guide - [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675) to install the sound drivers
After that i moved into installing my nvidia drivers using envy ( 7800GTX 256 )
Then after that it messed up my sound install so i went into recovery mode and went to root prompt and uninstalled the XFi drivers again.
then logged back into ubuntu and reinstalled the sound drivers and restarted

After that for me everything was working and i have sound so far. I am listening to music videos on you tube with no problem.

Of course its just 2.1 like everyone says.

I have only used ubuntu for 2 months now and i figured out how to recompile with no problem. So don't be scared its just a fancy word.

Hopefully someone will find this helpful. If you want me to run any commands or anything just let me know. I am not sure how to prove i hvae it config and working.

Thanks for all the post and questions it help me get it working.

---

Same here...Two months on Ubuntu and I been playing around with Puppy Linux for a week now.

I too was very scared and almost ran off when I heard the word "recompile".  I always associated that word with the hind end of heavy programming.  However, it was no where near as I imagined, in fact I think it went much easier than I would have thought.  The reason for that is that I have caught the tail end of the Dos days with PC's.  I bought my first PC when Windows 3.1 came out and was touted as "stable".  However, Windows 3.1 was NOT a true operating system.  It was just a gui.  The meat and potatoes was still Dos and it was inevitable to do command line work.  Most of the games of that time period (early 90's), didn't like Win 3.1 and programmers still relied on Dos.  Then when Windows "broke".  Most of the time you had to fix it through Dos.  So knowing the command line was essential.

The Terminal (or Console as it may be called in some Linux distros) is VERY similar to my experience when deal with Dos.  Sure the commands are different and there are permissions to deal with, but for the most part many commands in the Terminal mirror some commands back in my days when using Dos.

So, those of you that had played around with Dos will probably have an easier time getting around the terminal.  But it isn't necessary.

I will say that up to now this is THE guide to get the X-Fi running in Ubuntu Hardy.  I been poking around over a month before I found Amtam's guide and NO procedure ever got me more than half of the items with sound when I was dealing with OSS.  I now have over 90% of my sound operational and I have a better video driver as a result of it.

So if you have an X-Fi and you don't want to get rid of it, this is the way to go, especially if you want to do audio and video editing work.  I would be bold enough to say that if you are going to upgrade to Ubuntu Studio, following Amtam's guide is a MUST.

Anyway to Topsub, Welcome aboard and congradulations on getting your X-Fi running.

Geo

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-21
> **topsub said:**
> 
I had to do what i believe a round about way.

My install was on a fresh install on my desktop.


My problem was once i got the sound working i couldn't get my nvidia card working again. I was stuck at like 800 x 600 on my 24 inch screen and it sucked! So i finally got around it and here is how i did it below

I install fresh install ubuntu. 
Used this guide - [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=4823915&postcount=675) to install the sound drivers
After that i moved into installing my nvidia drivers using envy ( 7800GTX 256 )
Then after that it messed up my sound install so i went into recovery mode and went to root prompt and uninstalled the XFi drivers again.
then logged back into ubuntu and reinstalled the sound drivers and restarted


I use Envy now too. I used to install the Nvidia-drivers manually, but if you use different kernelsources than the default Ubuntu-sources (2.6.24.3) to compile, they sometimes don't work. For some reason, the modules won't install. With Envy they do. Great little utility. And the drivers are newer than the default restricted-modules driver.

About the guide: the best way I think is to compile first, then fix your nvidia-drivers and last, install the X-Fi drivers. The fact that installing the nvidia-drivers breaks the x-fi drivers is of course a little strange, if not ridiculous, but that's probably more the x-fi-driver's fault than it is nvidia's...](*,)

> 
About the guide: the best way I think is to compile first, then fix your nvidia-drivers and last, install the X-Fi drivers. The fact that installing the nvidia-drivers breaks the x-fi drivers is of course a little strange, if not ridiculous, but that's probably more the x-fi-driver's fault than it is nvidia's...](*,)

Agreed.  I double checked my video driver install after something wasn't working right.  So for the hell of it, I reinstalled the video drivers (this was after everything was set).  The act of reinstalling the video driver knocked out the Soundblaster X-Fi and I had to uninstall it completely and reinstall it.  That is why it is best to stop after recompiling the kernel and double check everything so if you need to update your video driver (a must for Nvidia card owners) you can do so PRIOR to installing the X-Fi drivers.

Geo

---

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-21
Hi peeps,
I've been checking in with the goings on over past days but haven't said much.
Geo, as you know I've been playing around with trying to get a plain jane kernel and the real time patch to be co-joined. Well, in short, I've had problems with their co-operation and have left it for the moment until I learn a little  more of how this stuff is structured.

Meanwhile, as you may be aware, the ubuntu rt patch is available from the repo, so I have put my attention to that over the past days (the whole weekend actually) and did a heck of a lot of reading as well, (that's why I haven't posted anything).

So what I have done is, configure up a small lean config for the 2.26.24 kernel we have. 
It packages up to around 35 MB or so. Amtam, I got the SATA sorted, use ACHI I think it was called. I worked out what my system has in the way of hardware, chips and bits and pieces. I think you can do the same with your lean config as well. Make sure you also have VFAT enabled if you want to be able to read MS file systems on MMC/SD/CF or USB memory.

Now, reading from the ubuntu forum, the rt patch only still gives the OS about 50% real time preemptive, where as going with Amtams recent workings where you take a plain jane vanilla kernel and patch in Ingo Molnar's real time patch you can expect that to go to around 95% real time. Remember that it is not possible to have 100% real time, not on these operating systems and I'm sure even some hardware may even prevent this too. An interesting read [here]("http://www.captain.at/howto-linux-real-time-patch.php") if you're inclined to learn more.

Geo
>  As for audio, well, now is a good time as any to pull out the X-Fi because it was a pain to set up on my Dell machine.

My Dell originally came with a special LITE version of the Soundblaster Live card. It worked great with the system, but I found out that it isn't a true Soundblaster Live that you would buy in the store. It was a specially made downgraded OEM version that didn't come with the soundfont synth. Naturally Dell never told anyone about this. I made the discover with my foray into the world of Miditzer (a virtual organ project). With Miditzer I NEEDED the soundfont synthesizer. Fluidsynth didn't work very well with Windows back then AND even to this day. BUT Fluidsynth works great in Linux. Other than that even the OEM Soundblaster Live card had surround sound and a 6 channel output.

So I did a bit of digging on the ALSA site and the OEM Dell Soundblaster Live card IS supported via ALSA. Another thing is that the Soundblaster Live also has connections on the back for a MIDI cable. The X-Fi doesn't. The Midi function is also supported with ALSA, (where as the X-Fi isn't). Finally, the OEM Soundblaster Live was MADE for my machine, and reinstallation would be a snap.Yes, I have a Dell here as well, a Dimension E520, which I had ordered specifically to be fitted with a Creative X-Fi extreme music card. So when I found out about that Dell produce I guess a creative clone of sorts I started to wonder if in my box was not the real deal. But I checked and it is, whew!! The Dell X-Fi card uses a different chip set and is not as capable.

> I was looking into a really cool firewire piece made by Focusrite called the Saffire. Yes, this should be a reasonably nice piece of kit. I just worry about the fact that it is an external device, firewire or HS USB??

When I read that you are looking into giving up on the X-Fi and maybe you are wanting to send it off on a flam de gondola down on your local waterway I feel uncertain about which way I should eventually head.
Do I continue to put lots and lots of hair pulling time into being challenged into making a system that will perform how I would wish for my sound creating and editing?
I'm certainly learning a lot about the working of Linux, and I know I'm still just a beginner. But going through Amtam's guide and other help also from Nullhead I read and try to work out exactly what each command is doing.

Or do I, (and I have been) looking at what other quality sound cards would could I choose from that can will have the specs of the X-Fi, but with the knowledge that I will be able to utilise it fully and not have an OS that I think will break when I sneeze.
And as you say> 
Also, what will happen when Intrepid comes out? What if I want to use JackLab or 64Studio instead of Ubuntu? I will always have to worry if the card will work or not.i think you are having the same worrying thoughts. Maybe it will, maybe it won't, it is an uncertainty.

And then i could just wait for a newer driver to magically appear out of creative's rear!!
I check on Daniel K, whom I mentioned in previous posts, there is much interesting articles about his work and the encounter with Creative, but I didn't see that he produced a driver for Linux. the driver he produced for windows out performs creatives driver by a mile in terms of functionality. In fact it is mentioned that creative even intentionally cripple their drivers for marketing reasons. In your XP, (read old, as in now gone XP) your X-Fi card is capable of much more than the creative drive will allow, and this is where Daniel K's driver gave you back functionality.

So, I will think on it over the next while, but I do intent to work through Amtam's workings and see what I can come up with. Plus it sort of is fun too. Though I know I could be doing many other things with my time that would be fun too, get outdoors and into the kayak for a day would be pleasant.

We have been speaking on organs a few posts ago, and you mentioned sampling, well I wanted to tell you that if you ever get the chance, do it!
I went to Holland a couple of years back and, well, I always take my sound recording gear with me, and I visited some of the magnificent cathedrals while there and had the opportunity to record. One I most remember, and I think Amtam will know of it, is in the southern part of Holland in the city of Maastricht . Oh, man, the sound from that piece was just amasing. The guy playing was just practicing, so the place was empty and I came right up to the altar with my recording kit, hiding behind the altar so he didn't even know I was there. Unfortunately I didn't setup correctly, as I was way too close and the recording came out badly clipped. But the sound, that is what I say WOW to, it makes your spine tingle!

Ok, getting back to it, I am trying to sort out a problem why I can't re-install the creative driver on the rt patched 2.26.24-custom??

Oh, Amtam, with all my mucking about i keep breaking my ATI fglrx so, again. I did a bit of ubuntu reading and figured that the closed sourced ATI drivers can be very problematic.
Last night it was getting worse and worse with regards to the video driver. At one stage, when i boot up it would say my video hardware could not be detected and set it up for some horribly low resolution and asking me to configure it manually. No matter what I did at this point it just stayed broken. You can imagine I was getting a little frustrated by now.
I was unable to get my system back on an even keel!
I took a look at envyng, Ha, it totally messed up the video system, it even messed up the installation of the driver!

So I have wipe, cleaned, scrubbed, sandblasted fglrx off my system for now, and have configured xorg using the supplied open source driver and it seems fine.
It's stable but without some of the niceties of the ATI propriety driver.

Sound is my main concern, so I need everything else to be stable and a given at this point.

So, at this point I have not got any further with having success with a vanilla kernel patched with Ingo's rt patch. Just have straight ubuntu with ubuntu's rt patch and no alsa again, oh damn!

Where are you at Geo?


mountain goat ~ blaaaaagghhhh with a sore throat ;)

Back then that is exactly what I thought I was getting too.   So one day I discovered Miditzer and the program required a change in the soundfonts.   I wanted to access some DSP effects and the soundfont synth (that comes with a REAL SB Live) then I realized something was wrong.  I remembered having a true Soundblaster on my old machine and it came with many more options and capabilities...including the soundfont synth.   So I wrote emails to Dell about it and also Creative.  After a LONG delay, Mum was the word from Dell and Creative told me that the "Soundblaster Live I have was created as an OEM spec card for DELL and designed to their specifications and Dell didn't deem it necessary to have the full package sold with their machines."  So I responded by saying, "So you mean I have a Soundblaster Live LITE and not the real deal".  They said, "In short, Yes".  I confronted Dell about it to saying that I DID pay for a FULL Soundblaster Live, but got a Live LITE.  After a bit of tongue fumbling they mentioned there was a way to get the Soundfonts to work with the card and they sent me a bunch of files and a LONG procedure that I had to go through,  but their fix NEVER worked.  It didn't work simply because the card doesn't have the capability.  So that taught me one thing with Dell.  By only standard and no upgrades.  Do the upgrades later.

So I bought the X-Fi card because it did have the soundfont synth.  Fluidsynth was available back then, but it was (and still isn't) very good with Windows.   Supposedly it supposed to be MUCH better in Linux.   So being that I don't need the sound card Soundfont synth and the X-fi's Soundfont synth didn't work in Linux anyway, I am going to pop the old OEM SB Live back into my machine.   In that regard it should make the re-installation of Windows much easier (since the re-install disks were made for my machine and the card was originally in my machine).



 > Yes, this should be a reasonably nice piece of kit. I just worry about the fact that it is an external device, firewire or HS USB??

I was told USB sound is NOT a good thing to go with in Linux.  Even with an RT kernel there are latency issues.  Firewire doesn't seem to have as near as many problems and there seem to be more success stories attached to those with Firewire than USB.  Anyway, the good thing about it is if it doesn't work right, I just have to unplug it and send it back. I don't have to tear the whole machine apart.

> When I read that you are looking into giving up on the X-Fi and maybe you are wanting to send it off on a flam de gondola down on your local waterway I feel uncertain about which way I should eventually head.

So I had to reinstall everything all over again.  So the second time around everything worked then.  So, from that day forth it worked fine without a hitch.  So that is why I was so adamant about removing the card when I first came to this thread.  I didn't want to disturb my Windows settings.  But as careful as my plan was to make sure that Linux didn't interfere with my goals at keeping Windows intact, along comes "Murphy" and I am sure everyone knows his famous law by now. "If it can go wrong, it will".  AND it did!  So Murphy's Law works! Don't doubt it.

> 

Do I continue to put lots and lots of hair pulling time into being challenged into making a system that will perform how I would wish for my sound creating and editing?

As it is I am thinking of working and trying out THREE different distributions outside of Ubuntu.  (Those are Puppy Linux, 64 Studio and JackLab).  The X-Fi would be a noose around my neck here.  Now, the next thing.  What happens to my X-Fi when Hardy is updated?  Do I have to wait around and hope that Amtam, or someone else writes a guide that will make the card work again?

> And as you sayi think you are having the same worrying thoughts. Maybe it will, maybe it won't, it is an uncertainty.

> And then i could just wait for a newer driver to magically appear out of creative's rear!!
