In fact it is mentioned that creative even intentionally cripple their drivers for marketing reasons. In your XP, (read old, as in now gone XP) your X-Fi card is capable of much more than the creative drive will allow, and this is where Daniel K's driver gave you back functionality.

Yeah, that is very nice, but now I am playing around with drivers in TWO different OS's?  Yeah I sure am going to put Windows back on.  I have to.  I really just want something that is going to work whether I am in Windows or just about any distribution of Linux.

Don't get me wrong the X-Fi IS a great card.  It is just that Creative doesn't want to create a working driver for us Linux people.  What can you do?

> So, I will think on it over the next while, but I do intent to work through Amtam's workings and see what I can come up with. Plus it sort of is fun too. Though I know I could be doing many other things with my time that would be fun too, get outdoors and into the kayak for a day would be pleasant.

Well, I probably would be still following along.  But now with my Windows currently on hiatus, it gave me time to really think things hard over.   I will say that Amtam's work is not for naught.  It is still great for those that have the X-Fi and just want to use it to do everyday tasks, watch movies, streaming internet, play a few games and MP3 files, perhaps much around with some audio and video work.  But I am going for the power play.  I REALLY want to see what Linux can do on a commercial level.  I want to see how it can handle professional gear and professional recording situations.  I want the biggest and the baddest Linux audio/video editing apps.  The average Ubuntu user isn't going to do what I want to do.  So I guess you have to gauge yourself on what you want to do and get out of Linux.  If you are as crazy as I am and want to link Jack with Ardour, Rosegarden, Hydrogen, Mixxx and have it all work together in real time?  Then I think you too should reevaluate hanging on to your X-Fi card as well.  When I first started with Ubuntu, I loved the X-Fi and wouldn't part with it...especially if there was a slight chance to get it working in Ubuntu.  But now, as I see the applications and really do see the potential for audio creation in Linux, I see the X-Fi as a very expensive anchor and I am ready to cut myself loose from that anchor.

> We have been speaking on organs a few posts ago, and you mentioned sampling, well I wanted to tell you that if you ever get the chance, do it!

Taking a live recording and a sample are two different things. What these guys do when they sample a real pipe organ for soundfont creation is nothing short of amazing.  For the greatest accuracy, by right every pipe should be sampled and that is with as little effect from the room as possible.  A very long (and to a certain extent) expensive task. Then there are other things to take into consideration such as shutters, vibrato or celeste effects.

For me, I started to do that too AND I even put work aside.   I have to stop that now.  I think if I DID have more time to play with it I would jump on the bandwagon with you guys.  But I guess I realized how much time and effort I put into this and I am happy to say that I DID get it going (with everyone's help).  I think I would have kept it going for a while, but Murphy said otherwise and I guess it is time for a change.  The X-Fi served me wonderfully in Windows.  Sure I am going to have to take a step back from the 6 channel sound back to four...but it is only temporary.  But I don't want to be boxed in anymore.  My goal is to make professional sounding recordings on a minimal budget.  Linux may be an answer.  The jury is still out, but with getting the X-Fi to run I was at least able to see that there IS something out there.  There IS stuff out there in open source land that is comparable to the expensive programs that I been using.  The X-Fi fix here helped me realize that.  So now it is time to keep moving forward.

Ya! It is the link to Amtam's guide).

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-21
> **mountain_goat said:**
> Geo,
I found it on [EveyOnesTube]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_wOzQoQ8mY0"), the organ I recorded in Maastricht!
This is not my video. I do sound only. Unfortunately the sound on this video isn't like hearing the real thing, as I'm sure you'll know.
You don't hear the lows at all. At about the 1:15 mark you get to hear a little. What a shame the person recording this is not using better gear :(

mountain goat ~ hitt'n the hay to see another day

Recording bass with a pipe organ has traditionally been not an easy thing to do.  You usually will never get the true power and sound on a recording because of the sound energy.  The sound pressure for recording bass would overload most mics and yet produce very little sound on the recording.  The average pipe organ can hit bass notes in the 25 - 32 hz range.  The presence of the very rare open Diapason 64foot pipe goes to 15hz and that is more meant to be felt than heard.  As it is there are only two or three organs in the world with a 64foot open pipe.

Anyway, getting WAY off track again.  There are many interesting topics about it and I can provide you with a ton of links.  Just drop me a private message.

Geo

### Post by mountain_goat on 2008-07-22
Geo,
I have read that you should be able to just delete your xorg.conf file.
To do that, I think there are a number of methods,
At terminal, enter code
> gksu nautilus
Which will fire up nautilus with the privilege to be able to modify and delete.
navigate to 
> /etc/X11
and delete your xorg file. you may have a few of them, each one ending in a number.
The system uses the config with the highest number.
I'm online here at home while working, so if you need I can get out of windows and into ubuntu to help directly.

Hope what I have written is clear enough.

mountain goat ~ just back from a walk in the forest on a bright sunny day

### Post by NullHead on 2008-07-22
> **jukingeo said:**
> Hello Nullhead or Amtam,

Tonight I removed the X-Fi card from my machine and installed the old Soundblaster Live.

In Ubuntu I removed the X-Fi drivers, and I removed the custome kernel and bounced back to the real time Ubuntu Studio kernel I have on my grub boot menu.  However, there is a problem with the video now.

Nullhead, I tried to do the sudo apt-get purge nvidia... command that you told me to do prior to reinstalling the video drivers when I had a problem with my OpenGL.   After that I loaded up the Nvidia Driver, but they don't seem to work!  I just get a black screen now.  None of the options in the recovery menu work either.  The X-Server fix and the dpkg commands do not work either.

Would you know what I have to do to fix this?  I would like to get the old real time kernel up and running again.

So as of now I am down TWO operating systems.  I wouldn't be able to write this if it wasn't for my setting up of Puppy Linux.

Well, let me know what to do if you can.  I probably will not respond though until the day after tomorrow.  Tomorrow evening I am going to try to reload Windows back up.

Geo

OK lets try a number of things. 

1, 
     You're going to want to remove the driver that you downloaded from Nvidia's website. To do this, navigate to where you keep that .run file from Nvidia's website and run: ```
sudo ./NVIDIA-Linux*.run --uninstall
```This "*should*" uninstall the driver, but I'm not sure, as I haven't had to do this in a wile. 

2,
     Now you want to install the Nvidia binary from the Ubuntu repository. To do this lets run some apt commands in the terminal. 
Run```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-kernel-common nvidia-glx-new
```

3,

     Lets start the kernel module and pray that it works. ```
sudo /etc/init.d/nvidia-kernel
```Lets check for errors. Run this command in a terminal and attach the .txt file it will generate in your home directory. ```
dmesg >> ~/dmesg_log.txt
```

*sudo /usr/bin/pray*   :confused:

4,

     Restart the computer; pray some more. 


Let me know how it goes.

     Now you want to install the Nvidia binary from the Ubuntu repository. To do this lets run some apt commands in the terminal.

### Post by amtam on 2008-07-23
Hey jukingeo, you really have a knack for 'attracting' problems it seems... ;) :lolflag:
First the nvidia-driver mess, now this. Murphy is having a ball with you....;)

But remember: what doesn't kill you, makes you stronger!

Lots of people who have ever tried to dual-boot Windows and Linux have run into this Grub-problem. Don't worry, it's easy to fix, you don't have to reinstall Ubuntu, just Grub itself.

But it's also a good idea to have Windows and Ubuntu on seperate, physical harddrives and not on partitions on the same drive. Harddrives are very cheap these days and having 2 (or more) of them can save you a lot of trouble.

About Windows: I always make a so called 'ghost-image' of my XP-install. That way if I screw something up, I can simply put back the image. Only takes about 10 minutes and I'm up and running again with all installed apps and configs. For that, I use a customized, bootable XP Live-CD called [BartPE](http://www.nu2.nu/pebuilder/). Very handy. Also handy is a bootable USB-stick with GParted on it, so you can pre-partition your harddrives in the blink of an eye... ;)

I hope you get the Grub-thing fixed soon so you can try Nullhead's instructions about the nvidia-driver. Good luck!

### Post by NullHead on 2008-07-23
Well I don't think you can delete posts. 

You can use the ubuntu live cd if you wish. It can be a bit more tricky, but I can talk you through it. 

Insert the live cd and boot to it as if you were going to install it. Open up a terminal and type:```
grub
```Figure out which hard drive you have linux on. If it's your first hdd then it'd be in grub hdd code, (hd0) If you want grub to install onto a partition, then it'd be like this (hd0,0) the second zero being the number of the partition. It starts at zero and works up. So your first partition would be partition 0 and your second partition would be 1 so for your second partition it'd be (hd0,1) instead of (hd0,0). 

You want to set your root hdd in grub so you can install it onto your hdd. ```
root (hd0)
```Then you want to have grub install into your master boot record with this command: ```
setup (hd0)
```

Restart and it *should* work.

---

### Post by NullHead on 2008-07-23
It should find 'em ... I guess it's always worked for me. I wouldn't know what to do if there are no menu selections, but you can use super grub to boot to your hdd with out having grub installed ... so I'd probably do that and cook up a menu item from my hdd install and then run "sudo update-grub" and then you would probably be good to go.

### Post by jukingeo on 2008-07-24
> **NullHead said:**
> Indeed, this is way too off topic. I wrote on your profile page asking you to use IRC with me so I can help you there.

Hello Nullhead,Guys.

There have been major changes as of yesterday.  First off, I edit/deleted my posts regarding Grub.  Small chat was one thing, but that was way off.

I don't want to get too off topic, but I weighed my options last (night and I realized that I had 5 major problems with Ubuntu, Grub and the video problem were the two biggies.

So I decided to change operating systems and after much fence sitting, I decided on 64Studio based on the main fact that it seems to be getting higher praise than JackLab And it is Debian/Gnome based.   I was HOPING 64Studio was like Ubuntu in operation and I am happy to say that I was pleasantly correct.  There are a few differences but for details I will put them in another post.

This also means that I am going to leave this thread.  I am going to keep Amtam's guide in my signature as a link to help other people.  Yeah, I just leaving this thread, but not the Ubuntu forum.  I figure because 64Studio is very close in appearance and operation to Ubuntu, many of the tips and tricks I learned here will apply.

So for those here that still want to chat about audio and everyday stuff just look for me in other threads (search for jukingeo) or drop me a line.  I also will be hanging out over at 64Studio as well.

To all:  Once again thank you for your help and getting my X-Fi running.  While it WAS short lived, at least it did give me enough of a perspective on what could be done with Linux-audio.  While I still may be doing a lot of this ](*,).  I will be sticking with Linux.

(Nullhead, I am going to send you a private message because (believe it or not) switching to 64Studio may have solved many of my Ubuntu problems, I still have Grub problems.)

Thank You,

Geo
